The very next sentence, however, says that the $H_{i}$ are 'unordered lists' (multisets), and later formulae and Fig. 1 consistently treat $H_{0}$ in this unordered, count-based way. This creates a small notational inconsistency in this sentence. It would be clearer either not to write $H_{0} \in E^{n}$ here, or to state explicitly that $H_{0}$ is an unordered multiset of $n$ types from $E$, reserving $E^{n}$ for ordered samples such as $A_{n}$.

comments.title: Unproven normalization constant in Theorem 1
comments.quote:
The constant of proportionality $C$ is given by

$$
C=\frac{n(n-1+\theta)}{2},
$$

where $n$ is the number of chromosomes in $H_{i}$.
Proof. That $Q_{\theta}^{*}$ is in the class $\mathcal{M}$ follows from the Markov nature of $\mathcal{H}$... The value of $C$ follows from the fact that events must occur at total rate $k(k-1+\theta) / 2$ when there are $k$ lineages in the ancestry (see Stephens (2000)).
comments.explanation: At first the sentence "The value of $C$ follows from the fact that events must occur at total rate $k(k-1+\theta)/2$ when there are $k$ lineages in the ancestry (see Stephens (2000))" made me think that more justification was needed, since the normalisation of the backward transition probabilities in (15) is not shown by direct summation. Then I understood that this step is simply using the standard fact for time-reversed continuous-time Markov chains observed at stationarity: the total exit rate from a state is unchanged under time reversal, so given that events in the (forward) ancestry occur at rate $k(k-1+\theta)/2$ when there are $k$ lineages, the same constant must normalise the backward rates. You might consider adding a brief remark or pointer that makes this Markov-process argument explicit, as readers who do not immediately recall this general result could pause at this point in the proof of Theorem 1.

comments.title: Possible notational inconsistency in proof of Theorem 1
comments.quote:
P\left\{\Upsilon_{\mathrm{m}} \cap A_{k}(t-\delta)=\left(\alpha_{1}, \ldots, \alpha_{k-1}, \beta\right) \mid A_{k}(t)=\left(\alpha_{1}, \ldots, \alpha_{k-1}, \alpha\right)\right\} & \\
\quad= & \frac{P\left\{\Upsilon_{\mathrm{m}} \cap A_{k}(t-\delta)=\left(\alpha_{1}, \ldots, \alpha_{k-1}, \beta\right) \cap A_{k}(t)=\left(\alpha_{1}, \ldots, \alpha_{k-1}, \alpha\right)\right\}}{P\left\{A_{k}(t)=\left(\alpha_{1}, \ldots, \alpha_{k-1}, \alpha\right)\right\}} \\
& =\frac{\pi\left(\alpha_{1}, \ldots, \alpha_{n-1}, \beta\right) \delta \theta P_{\beta \alpha} / 2}{\pi\left(\alpha_{1}, \ldots, \alpha_{n-1}, \alpha\right)}+o(\delta)
comments.explanation: At first the appearance of $\pi(\alpha_1,\ldots,\alpha_{n-1},\beta)$ and $\pi(\alpha_1,\ldots,\alpha_{n-1},\alpha)$ in the middle line of this display made me think that the stationary distribution was being evaluated on an $n$-dimensional argument, even though the surrounding setup is explicitly for $k$ ancestral lineages with $A_k(t)=(\alpha_1,\ldots,\alpha_{k-1},\alpha)$. Then I understood from the preceding and following lines, and from the rewrite in terms of $\pi(\beta\mid A_k-\alpha)$ and $\pi(\alpha\mid A_k-\alpha)$, that these factors must correspond to $\pi(\alpha_1,\ldots,\alpha_{k-1},\beta)$ and $\pi(\alpha_1,\ldots,\alpha_{k-1},\alpha)$ and that the use of $n-1$ here is simply an indexing slip. It might nevertheless be helpful to correct these subscripts (and, if you wish, to note that $\pi$ is being used generically for the appropriate $k$-dimensional marginal of the stationary distribution) so that the dimension of the state space is completely consistent within the proof.

comments.title: Interpretation of the transition matrix in Proposition 1(e)
comments.quote:
The distribution $\hat{\pi}\left(\cdot \mid A_{n}\right)$ is the stationary distribution of the Markov chain on $E$ with transition matrix

$$
T_{\beta \alpha}=\frac{\theta}{n+\theta} P_{\beta \alpha}+\frac{n_{\alpha}}{n+\theta},
$$

i.e. ... Remark 1. ... Property (e) provides a characterization of $\pi$ which is, in some more general settings not considered in this paper, more amenable to generalization than definition 1. The Markov transition matrix (21) is the transition matrix associated with the $(n+1)$ th line of the particle representation described in Section 4 (or the Moran model) when the first $n$ lines are fixed to be $A_{n}$.
comments.explanation:
The identification in Remark 1 of the matrix in equation (21) with "the transition matrix associated with the $(n+1)$th line of the particle representation … when the first $n$ lines are fixed to be $A_n$" appears to be too strong if taken literally. Under the particle representation in Section 4, a single lineage mutates at rate $\theta/2$ and copies from the $n$ fixed lines at total rate $n$, so the embedded jump chain for that lineage has transition probabilities
\[
T'_{\beta\alpha} = \frac{(\theta/2)P_{\beta\alpha} + n_\alpha}{\theta/2 + n},
\]
whereas the matrix (21) is
\[
T_{\beta\alpha} = \frac{\theta}{n+\theta}P_{\beta\alpha} + \frac{n_\alpha}{n+\theta}.
\]
These kernels are not identical for general $n$ and $\theta$; in particular, they differ by a factor of 2 in the relative weight of mutation versus copying events. The algebraic statement of Proposition 1(e) that $\hat\pi(\cdot\mid A_n)$ is the stationary distribution of $T$ is entirely correct, but the genealogical interpretation in Remark 1 seems to conflate this auxiliary chain with the exact jump chain of the $(n+1)$th line in the specified particle representation. It would be helpful to adjust the wording to clarify that (21) corresponds to a Markov chain that mixes mutation and copying steps in proportions governed by $\theta$ and $n$, rather than to the literal embedded chain of the particle representation with mutation rate $\theta/2$.

comments.title: Unclear step in the proof of Proposition 2
comments.quote:
Thus

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{n_{\alpha}}{n} & =\sum_{\beta \in E} \frac{\hat{\pi}\left(\beta \mid H_{i}-\alpha\right)}{\hat{\pi}\left(\alpha \mid H_{i}-\alpha\right)}\left(\frac{\theta}{n-1+\theta} \frac{n_{\alpha}}{n} P_{\beta \alpha}+\frac{n_{\alpha}}{n} \frac{n_{\alpha}-1}{n-1+\theta}\right) \\
& =\sum_{\beta \in E} \frac{\hat{\pi}\left(\beta \mid H_{i}-\alpha\right)}{\hat{\pi}\left(\alpha \mid H_{i}-\alpha\right)} C^{-1} \frac{\theta}{2} n_{\alpha} P_{\beta \alpha}+\frac{1}{\hat{\pi}\left(\alpha \mid H_{i}-\alpha\right)} C^{-1}\binom{n_{\alpha}}{2} \\
& =p_{\mathrm{m}}(\alpha)+p_{\mathrm{c}}(\alpha)
\end{aligned}
$$
comments.explanation:
At first this step in the proof of Proposition 2 made me think that the authors were simply replacing the summand
\[
\frac{\theta}{n-1+\theta} \frac{n_{\alpha}}{n} P_{\beta \alpha}+\frac{n_{\alpha}}{n} \frac{n_{\alpha}-1}{n-1+\theta}
\]
by the two terms in the next line, which would not hold termwise. Then I understood that the first part is just rewritten using $C^{-1}=2/\{n(n-1+\theta)\}$, and that the second part has been summed over $\beta$ using $\sum_{\beta}\hat{\pi}(\beta\mid H_i-\alpha)=1$, giving the term involving $C^{-1}\binom{n_\alpha}{2}$. It might help the reader if this use of the normalisation of $\hat{\pi}(\cdot\mid\cdot)$ and the separation of the two contributions (the sum and the already-summed term) were mentioned explicitly at this point.

comments.title: Use of a further approximation for $\hat{\pi}$ in applications
comments.quote: Note that this model has $2^{10}$ different alleles, and so the calculation of the quantities $\hat{\pi}\left(\beta \mid A_{n}\right)$ using equations (18) and (19) appears to be computationally daunting. In fact the special structure of this model allows an efficient approximation of these quantities, as described in Appendix A.
comments.explanation: I initially had trouble seeing exactly what version of $\hat{\pi}(\cdot\mid\cdot)$ was used in the simulated sequence example, because the main text only refers to an "efficient approximation" and then moves on to the numerical results. However, I later saw that Appendix A makes clear that a numerical quadrature scheme is used to approximate the expression for $\hat{\pi}$ from Definition 1, and that the resulting proposal is a valid IS function in its own right. To make the link between Sections 4 and 5.2 entirely explicit, it might be helpful to state directly in Section 5.2 that the performance reported there for $Q_\theta^{\mathrm{SD}}$ is based on this practical numerical approximation to $\hat{\pi}$ as described in Appendix A.

comments.title: Inverted description of microsatellite boundary conditions
comments.quote: The implementation of our IS scheme is facilitated by centring the sample distribution near 10 repeats and truncating the type space $E$ to $\{0,1, \ldots, 19\}$ by insisting that all mutations to alleles of length 0 or 19 involve the gain or loss respectively of a single repeat.
comments.explanation:
The sentence

"The implementation of our IS scheme is facilitated by centring the sample distribution near 10 repeats and truncating the type space $E$ to $\{0,1, \ldots, 19\}$ by insisting that all mutations to alleles of length 0 or 19 involve the gain or loss respectively of a single repeat."

appears to have the gain/loss description at the boundaries reversed relative to the stepwise model defined just above. Under that model, the only forward-time mutation yielding allele 0 is 1→0, which is a loss of a repeat, and the only mutation yielding 19 is 18→19, which is a gain. As written, "gain or loss respectively" suggests the opposite mapping. This is very likely just an inversion in wording (for example, "loss or gain" or "from" instead of "to" would match the usual reflecting-boundary convention), and since the observed alleles are far from 0 and 19 the precise boundary behaviour has negligible effect on the examples. Nonetheless, it would be helpful to correct or clarify this sentence so that the intended direction of gain and loss at the lower and upper boundaries is unambiguous for readers wishing to reproduce the implementation.

comments.title: Justification for the infinite sites proposal distribution
comments.quote: These problems are not insurmountable, but for simplicity we adapt our earlier approach to this context by analogy with proposition 2: recall that one method of simulating from our IS function $Q^{\mathrm{SD}}$ begins by choosing a chromosome uniformly at random from those present and assuming that this chromosome is involved in the most recent event backwards in time. ... Analogy with proposition 2 then suggests choosing the most recent event backwards in time by drawing a chromosome uniformly at random from those satisfying either (a) or (b). This procedure defines an IS function $Q^{\mathrm{SD}}$ which we note is independent of $\theta$, removing the need to specify a driving value.
comments.explanation:
Statement "for simplicity we adapt our earlier approach to this context by analogy with proposition 2 … This procedure defines an IS function $Q^{\mathrm{SD}}$ which we note is independent of $\theta$" initially made me wonder how closely the infinite‑sites sampler is tied to the general construction of $Q^{\mathrm{SD}}$ via Theorem 1 and Definition 2. The text does indicate that, because of technical complications (uncountable type space, non‑reversibility), you are now using a more heuristic, analogy‑based design: uniform choice over the chromosomes that could have been involved in the last event, rather than an explicit approximation to $Q^*_\theta$ built from a surrogate $\hat\pi$.

However, since $Q^{\mathrm{SD}}$ was earlier defined quite precisely as the θ‑dependent sampler obtained by substituting $\hat\pi$ into Theorem 1, reusing the symbol for this θ‑independent, heuristic infinite‑sites proposal may cause some readers to momentarily conflate the two constructions and to overestimate how directly Section 4's theory applies here.

It might therefore be helpful to make the nature of this extension more explicit: to state clearly that in the infinite‑sites case you are no longer working within the Theorem 1/Definition 2 framework but are instead introducing a separate, genealogically motivated proposal that happens to share the same notation; and perhaps to add a short remark on why you expect this uniform‑over‑eligible‑chromosomes choice to be a reasonable approximation in practice. This would clarify the relationship between the main theoretical development and the infinite‑sites special case without altering the substance of the method.

comments.title: Clarity of the rooted tree sampler modification (Sec 5.5)
comments.quote: To facilitate a comparison with published estimates, we modified our IS function to analyse rooted trees, by adding to conditions (a) and (b) above a condition that no mutation can occur backwards in time from the type of the MRCA. Note that this does not take full account of the information contained in the position of the root, and the modified sampler is likely to be less efficient for roots which are relatively unlikely given the data.
comments.explanation:
Statement "we modified our IS function to analyse rooted trees … by adding … a condition that no mutation can occur backwards in time from the type of the MRCA" caused me some initial uncertainty about exactly how the backward moves were constrained for the rooted case. At first it was not obvious whether "from the type of the MRCA" referred to forbidding proposals that originate on lineages of the designated MRCA type, or to some other restriction, and one also has to keep in mind that in the rooted–tree setting a particular MRCA type has been fixed in advance.

After reading the surrounding discussion on rooted trees and outgroup information, it becomes clear that the intent is to condition on a chosen root type and then forbid any backward mutation steps that originate from a lineage currently carrying that root type, while still allowing coalescences between such lineages. This ensures that the proposal is restricted to histories in which the designated root type is ancestral rather than created by mutation.

Because this is the only change needed to adapt the unrooted sampler to the rooted setting, it may be worth stating explicitly that the algorithm conditions on a particular MRCA type and then disallows backward mutation moves that originate on lineages of that type. This would make the nature of the additional constraint fully transparent to readers implementing the rooted sampler.

comments.title: Unclear description of ascertainment correction
comments.quote: For the IS schemes that we have considered, assuming the infinite sites mutation model, the ascertainment effect can be accommodated by labelling every lineage which leads to any chromosome in the panel as a panel lineage and adapting both $P_{\theta}(\mathcal{H})$ and $Q_{\theta}(\mathcal{H})$ so that mutations can occur only on panel lineages.
comments.explanation:
The description of the SNP ascertainment correction in this paragraph is rather compressed, and the role of the "panel lineage" labelling could be clearer. As written, labelling "every lineage which leads to any chromosome in the panel" as a panel lineage and then allowing mutations "only on panel lineages" would in principle include branches ancestral to all panel members as well as those ancestral to a proper subset. For the two‑stage design you describe, the ascertained sites are specifically those that are polymorphic within the panel, so the mutation for any ascertained SNP must fall on a branch that partitions the panel, not on the trunk ancestral to the entire panel.

In the full inferential framework, consistency with the observed polymorphism in the panel will be enforced via the likelihood factor $\pi_\theta(A_n \mid \mathcal{H})$, and mutations on branches that make the panel monomorphic will automatically be given zero weight. However, the current wording does not spell out this interaction and could be read as if restricting mutations to panel lineages alone were sufficient to encode the ascertainment scheme. It would help readers if you could make explicit how the event "SNP is polymorphic in the panel" is imposed in your IS formulation, and in particular distinguish between branches ancestral to all panel members and those that genuinely partition the panel.

comments.title: Proposed extension for long sequences is unclear
comments.quote: The effect of the non-varying sites could then be taken into account by the factor $\pi_{\theta}\left(A_{n} \mid \mathcal{H}^{(i)}\right)$ in estimator (9), which could be calculated by the peeling algorithm (to do this it would be necessary to apply IS to the full typed ancestry $\mathcal{A}$ of the sample).
comments.explanation:
The brief discussion of long sequences in Section 6.2 is difficult to interpret in light of the earlier definitions of $\mathcal{H}$, $\mathcal{A}$ and $\pi_\theta(\cdot \mid \cdot)$. Earlier in the paper, $\mathcal{H}$ is defined as a history without times or topology, and equation (2) defines $\pi_\theta(A_n \mid \mathcal{H})$ as a purely combinatorial term depending only on $H_0$, independent of $\theta$ and of any tree structure. In contrast, the peeling algorithm takes as input a fully specified genealogy with branch lengths (part of $\mathcal{A}$) and computes a sequence likelihood $P_\theta(A_n \mid \mathcal{G})$.

In the quoted sentence you suggest that "the effect of the non‑varying sites could then be taken into account by the factor $\pi_\theta(A_n \mid \mathcal{H}^{(i)})$ … which could be calculated by the peeling algorithm," with a parenthetical remark that this would require applying IS to the full typed ancestry $\mathcal{A}$. As it stands, this mixes two different notions: the earlier $\pi_\theta(A_n \mid \mathcal{H})$ cannot be evaluated by peeling, and peeling cannot be applied at the level of $\mathcal{H}$ alone. The parenthesis hints at a switch of missing data from $\mathcal{H}$ to $\mathcal{A}$ for this extension, but that switch and the associated change in the meaning of the likelihood factor are not made explicit.

For readers interested in implementing such an extension, it would be useful to clarify that the peeling algorithm would be used to compute a full sequence likelihood $P_\theta(A_n \mid \mathcal{A}^{(i)})$ (or the contribution of the non‑varying sites) conditional on a sampled typed ancestry, and that in this context IS would operate on $\mathcal{A}$ rather than on histories $\mathcal{H}$ alone.

comments.title: Formulation of the Griffiths-Tavaré estimator
comments.quote:
In the Griffiths-Tavaré method, view the problem of finding $\{p(\mathbf{n})\}$ as solving the system of linear
equations (34). Importance sampling techniques were used to obtain a solution of the equations. Rescale $P\left(H_{j} \mid H_{j-1}^{\prime}\right)$ and interpret as a probability distribution

$$
q\left(H_{j-1}^{\prime} \mid H_{j}\right)=p\left(H_{j} \mid H_{j-1}^{\prime}\right) / f\left(H_{j}\right)
$$

where $f\left(H_{j}\right)=\Sigma p\left(H_{j} \mid H_{j-1}^{\prime}\right)$. Then

$$
p\left(H_{j}\right)=f\left(H_{j}\right) \sum q\left(H_{j-1}^{\prime} \mid H_{j}\right) p\left(H_{j-1}^{\prime}\right),
$$

leading to an importance sampling representation

$$
p\left(H_{0}\right)=E_{q}\left\{f\left(H_{0}\right) f\left(H_{-1}\right) \ldots f\left(H_{-m}\right)\right\}
$$

with absorption at $-m$, where there first is a single ancestor of the sample. The proposal distribution for importance sampling is $q(\cdot \mid \cdot)$.
comments.explanation:
In the displayed importance sampling representation for the Griffiths–Tavaré method,
\[
p(H_0)=E_q\{f(H_0) f(H_{-1}) \ldots f(H_{-m})\},
\]
I found it difficult to reconcile the indexing with the recursion that precedes it. From
\[
p(H_j)=\sum_{H_{j-1}'} P(H_j\mid H_{j-1}')\,p(H_{j-1}')=f(H_j)\sum_{H_{j-1}'} q(H_{j-1}'\mid H_j)\,p(H_{j-1}'),
\]
iterating backwards until the first single-ancestor state \(H_{-m}\) leads to
\[
p(H_0)=E_q\Big\{p(H_{-m})\,f(H_0) f(H_{-1}) \cdots f(H_{-(m-1)})\Big\}.
\]
That is, the product over the normalising factors \(f(H_j)\) stops at the last non-absorbing configuration, and the terminal factor should be the boundary probability \(p(H_{-m})\) of the ancestral state. As written, the expression appears to include an extra factor \(f(H_{-m})\), which is not defined by \(f(H_j)=\sum_{H_{j-1}'} P(H_j\mid H_{j-1}')\) at the absorbing single-ancestor state. Clarifying that the final factor is \(p(H_{-m})\) (or that \(f(H_{-m})\) is being used as shorthand for this boundary term) would make the connection between the linear system (34) and the importance sampling representation precise.

comments.title: Ambiguous notation in discussion of methods
comments.quote:
In the Stephens-Donnelly method,

$$
p\left(H_{j}\right)=\sum \frac{p\left(H_{j} \mid H_{j-1}^{\prime}\right)}{\hat{p}\left(H_{j-1}^{\prime} \mid H_{j}\right)} \hat{p}\left(H_{j-1}^{\prime} \mid H_{j}\right) p\left(H_{j-1}^{\prime}\right)
$$

The importance sampling representation is

$$
\begin{aligned}
p\left(H_{0}\right) & =E_{\hat{p}}\left\{\frac{p\left(H_{0} \mid H_{-1}\right)}{\hat{p}\left(H_{-1} \mid H_{0}\right)} \cdots \frac{p\left(H_{-m+1} \mid H_{-m}\right)}{\hat{p}\left(H_{-m} \mid H_{-m+1}\right)} p\left(H_{-m}\right)\right\} \\
& =E_{\hat{p}}\left\{\frac{p\left(\mathcal{H}_{\rightarrow}\right)}{\hat{p}\left(\mathcal{H}_{\leftarrow}\right)}\right\} .
\end{aligned}
$$
comments.explanation: Statement (35) initially gave me the impression that \(p(H_j\mid H'_{j-1})\) was being used both for the recursion coefficients in equation (34) and for the forward transition probabilities \(p_\theta(H_i\mid H_{i-1})\) of the history-generating process defined earlier. Since these objects are distinct, this overloading of the symbol \(p(\cdot\mid\cdot)\) could momentarily mislead a reader trying to relate (35) directly to the coalescent dynamics. It might help if the discussion explicitly stated that in (35) the terms \(p(H_i\mid H_{i-1})\) are still the coefficients from the linear system (34), and that \(\hat p(\cdot\mid\cdot)\) is a probability kernel constructed from \(\hat\pi\) used purely as a proposal for solving those equations, rather than as an approximation to the forward transition kernel in equation (1).

comments.title: Incorrect recursive weight formula in SIS discussion
comments.quote:
As with many SIS applications, both Stephens and Donnelly, and Griffiths and Tavaré (1994) propose trial densities $q_{0}(\mathcal{H})$ of the form

$$
q_{\theta}(\mathcal{H})=\prod_{i=0}^{-(m-1)} q_{\theta}\left(H_{i-1} \mid H_{i}\right)
$$

For such constructions we define the current weight (for $t \leqslant m$ )

$$
w_{-t}=\frac{p_{\theta}\left(H_{-(t-1)} \mid H_{-t}\right) \ldots p_{\theta}\left(H_{0} \mid H_{-1}\right)}{q_{0}\left(H_{-t} \mid H_{-(t-1)}\right) \ldots q_{0}\left(H_{-1} \mid H_{0}\right)} \equiv w_{-(t-1)} \frac{p_{\theta}\left(H_{-(t-1)} \mid H_{-1}\right)}{q_{0}\left(H_{-t} \mid H_{-(t-1)}\right)}
$$
comments.explanation:
The recursive definition of the current weight $w_{-t}$ in the Chen–Liu discussion appears to contain a small indexing typo. From the explicit product expression,
\[
w_{-t}=\frac{p_{\theta}\left(H_{-(t-1)} \mid H_{-t}\right)\cdots p_{\theta}\left(H_{0} \mid H_{-1}\right)}{q_{0}\left(H_{-t} \mid H_{-(t-1)}\right)\cdots q_{0}\left(H_{-1} \mid H_{0}\right)},
\]
one obtains the recursion
\[
w_{-t}=w_{-(t-1)}\,\frac{p_{\theta}\left(H_{-(t-1)} \mid H_{-t}\right)}{q_{0}\left(H_{-t} \mid H_{-(t-1)}\right)}.
\]
The printed version instead has $p_{\theta}\left(H_{-(t-1)} \mid H_{-1}\right)$ in this last factor, which is inconsistent with both the preceding line and the Markov structure, and should be corrected to condition on $H_{-t}$. In addition, the product notation $\prod_{i=0}^{-(m-1)} q_{\theta}(H_{i-1}\mid H_i)$ is somewhat non-standard; since $i$ is running over $0,-1,\dots,-(m-1)$ this is still correct, but a brief clarification or reindexing would improve readability.

zero_shot.label: Zero-Shot
zero_shot.overall_feedback: This is a highly influential and well-written paper that introduces an important new importance sampling algorithm for inference in population genetics. The mathematical development is generally rigorous and the empirical comparisons are thorough. However, there are several technical and logical issues, including errors in transition probability formulas, inconsistencies in notation, and a questionable step in one of the proofs.

comments.title: Incorrect timescale description
comments.quote: It turns out that the natural timescale on which to view the evolution of the population is in units of $\mathrm{N} / \nu^{2}$ generations, where $\nu^{2}$ is the variance of the number of progeny of an arbitrary chromosome
comments.explanation: The standard coalescent timescale is N generations for the Wright-Fisher model, or more generally N/σ² generations where σ² is the variance of offspring number. However, the definition θ/2 = Nμ/ν² given later is consistent with ν² being the variance. The issue is that for the Wright-Fisher model, the variance of offspring number per individual is approximately 1 (not ν²=1), and the standard coalescent scaling is in units of N generations. The notation is potentially confusing but internally consistent if ν² is defined appropriately. This is a minor notational concern rather than a clear error.

comments.title: Duplicate reference label for Griffiths and Tavaré (1994b,c)
comments.quote:
(1994b) Ancestral inference in population genetics. Statist. Sci., 9, 307-319.
$\_\_\_\_$ (1994b) Sampling theory for neutral alleles in a varying environment. Phil. Trans. R. Soc. Lond. B, 344, 403410.
comments.explanation: Two different papers by Griffiths and Tavaré from 1994 are both labeled as '(1994b)' in the references. The second one should be labeled '(1994c)', which is how it is cited in the text. This is a bibliographic error.

comments.title: Inconsistent subscript in proof of Theorem 1 (mutation rate)
comments.explanation: The subscripts switch from k to n inconsistently. The configuration has k components (α₁,...,α_{k-1},α), but in the next line the authors write π(α₁,...,α_{n-1},β) using subscript n-1 instead of k-1. Since k is the number of lineages at time t and n is the sample size, these are different quantities in general. The subscript should be k-1, not n-1.

comments.title: Missing summation over β in coalescence probability derivation
comments.explanation: The expression appears to be missing a division sign. The left-hand side should be a conditional probability (a ratio), but as typeset the numerator sum and denominator appear to be multiplied rather than divided. This should read with the sum in the numerator divided by the denominator probability.

comments.title: Equation (6) reference appears before it is defined
comments.quote: In fact there is a whole family of Markov chains satisfying equation (6), each with its own function $F$.
comments.explanation: The paper references 'equation (6)' which is the Griffiths-Tavaré representation L(θ) = E{∏F(Bⱼ)}. However, the statement about 'a whole family of Markov chains satisfying equation (6)' is made in the context of discussing proposal distributions in Section 4, and the connection to equation (6) is not clearly established. More importantly, the one-to-one correspondence claimed between this family and the class M of Markov proposal distributions is stated without proof or clear justification.

comments.title: Sign or factor issue in proof of Proposition 1(b)
comments.quote: The result follows from the fact that $m_{1}+m_{2}$ is geometric with parameter $\theta /(1+\theta)$, independently of $\alpha_{1}$.
comments.explanation: For n=1 (two sampled chromosomes), the total number of mutations m₁+m₂ on the coalescent tree of two chromosomes involves mutations on two independent branches, each of which has mutations occurring as a Poisson process with rate θ/2 along branches whose total length is exponential with rate 1. The total number of mutations is geometric with success probability 1/(1+θ), not θ/(1+θ). If 'geometric with parameter p' means P(X=k) = (1-p)^k · p for k=0,1,2,..., then the parameter should be 1/(1+θ). If the convention is different (parameter = probability of 'failure'), then θ/(1+θ) could be correct, but this is an unusual convention that should be clarified.

comments.title: Claim about optimal IS having zero variance is imprecise
comments.quote: and the variance of the estimator is 0
comments.explanation: The derivation in equation (11) shows that each weight equals L(θ), giving zero variance. However, the intermediate step contains an error in notation: P_θ(H ∩ A_n) should be P_θ(H, A_n) (joint probability), and the step P_θ(A_n|H)·P_θ(H)/P_θ(H|A_n) = P_θ(H,A_n)/P_θ(H|A_n) is correct by Bayes' theorem only if π_θ(A_n|H) is interpreted as P_θ(A_n|H). The notation switches between π and P somewhat loosely here.

comments.title: Inconsistency in the definition of geometric distribution parameter
comments.quote: mutating it according to the mutation matrix $P$ a geometric number of times, with parameter $\theta /(n+\theta)$
comments.explanation: In Definition 1, the geometric distribution parameter is stated as θ/(n+θ). Looking at equation (17), the sum over m=0,1,2,... has terms (θ/(n+θ))^m · n/(n+θ), which corresponds to a geometric distribution where the 'success probability' (probability of stopping) is n/(n+θ). The 'parameter' θ/(n+θ) is actually the probability of continuing (i.e., of another mutation). The convention used should be clarified, as 'geometric with parameter p' most commonly means P(X=k) = (1-p)^k · p, in which case the parameter would be n/(n+θ), not θ/(n+θ).

comments.title: Potential error in equation for coalescence backward rate
comments.explanation: In the coalescence rate derivation, the numerator involves summing over β of P{A_k(t-δ) = (α₁,...,α_{k-2},α,β)}·δ. But the look-down rate for line k copying line k-1 is (k-1) per unit time (not 1), and the probability that line k copies specifically line k-1 (rather than any of lines 1,...,k-1) is 1/(k-1). So the rate of the specific coalescence event Υ_c (line k copies line k-1) is 1 per unit time. The factor δ in the numerator accounts for this. However, the sum over β should give Σ_β π(α₁,...,α_{k-2},α,β) in the numerator, and the denominator is π(α₁,...,α_{k-2},α,α). The simplification to 1/π(α|A_k-α) requires that Σ_β π(α₁,...,α_{k-2},α,β)/π(α₁,...,α_{k-2},α,α) = 1/π(α|A_k-α), which by exchangeability and the definition of conditional probability is π(α₁,...,α_{k-2},α)·1/π(α₁,...,α_{k-2},α,α) · ... This step needs more careful justification.

comments.title: Inconsistent use of n vs k in Theorem 1 equations
comments.quote:
where $n_{\alpha}$ denotes the number of chromosomes of type $\alpha$ in $H_{i}$. The constant of proportionality $C$ is given by
$$
C=\frac{n(n-1+\theta)}{2},
$$
where $n$ is the number of chromosomes in $H_{i}$.
comments.explanation: In the statement of Theorem 1, n is used both for the sample size (as established earlier in the paper) and for the number of chromosomes in H_i (which varies as we go backward in time). This creates ambiguity. In the proof, k is used for the number of lineages at a given time, which is the correct variable. The theorem statement should use k (or another variable) instead of n for the number of chromosomes in H_i to avoid confusion with the fixed sample size n.

comments.title: Incorrect cross-reference to equation (13) as Griffiths-Tavaré proposal
comments.quote:
When viewed as an IS method, the Griffiths-Tavaré scheme corresponds to a proposal distribution $Q_{\theta}^{\mathrm{GT}}$ in the class $\mathcal{M}$, with
$$
q_{\theta}\left(H_{i-1} \mid H_{i}\right) \propto p_{\theta}\left(H_{i} \mid H_{i-1}\right)
$$
comments.explanation: This is labeled as equation (13) and states that the GT backward transition probability is proportional to the forward transition probability. This is correct and is the time-reversal relationship for a reversible chain. However, the paper later says 'Implementations of the Griffiths-Tavaré scheme (by themselves and others) have routinely used the particular Markov chain leading to expression (13)'. The expression (13) defines a specific proposal, not a family, so the phrasing 'leading to' is slightly misleading - it IS expression (13).

few_shot.label: Few-Shot
few_shot.overall_feedback: This is a landmark paper in computational population genetics that introduces a substantially improved importance sampling algorithm. The mathematical development is generally rigorous and clearly presented. However, careful reading reveals several notation inconsistencies, a potential error in the proof of Theorem 1, and some issues with cross-referencing of equations. The paper's core contributions—the characterization of the optimal proposal distribution and the practical approximation to it—appear sound, but there are places where the notation shifts without warning or where equation references point to the wrong targets.

comments.title: Incorrect equation cross-reference for Griffiths-Tavaré proposal distribution
comments.quote:
When viewed as an IS method, the Griffiths-Tavaré scheme corresponds to a proposal distribution $Q_{\theta}^{\mathrm{GT}}$ in the class $\mathcal{M}$, with

$$q_{\theta}\left(H_{i-1} \mid H_{i}\right) \propto p_{\theta}\left(H_{i} \mid H_{i-1}\right)$$

where the forward transition probabilities $p_{\theta}$ are defined at equations (1). In fact there is a whole family of Markov chains satisfying equation (6)
comments.explanation: The text states 'there is a whole family of Markov chains satisfying equation (6)' but equation (6) is the Griffiths-Tavaré representation $L(\theta) = E\{\prod_{j=0}^{\tau} F(B_j) \mid B_0 = A_n\}$, which describes the likelihood representation, not the proportionality condition for the backward transition probabilities. The reference should be to equation (13), which is the equation displayed immediately above defining $q_{\theta}(H_{i-1} \mid H_i) \propto p_{\theta}(H_i \mid H_{i-1})$. This is a cross-referencing error.

comments.title: Index substitution error in proof of Theorem 1 (mutation rate derivation)
comments.explanation: In this line of the proof, the subscripts on the configuration suddenly switch from $k$ (the number of current lineages in the ancestry) to $n$ (the sample size). The line above uses $A_k(t) = (\alpha_1, \ldots, \alpha_{k-1}, \alpha)$ with $k$ lineages, but the displayed equation writes $\pi(\alpha_1, \ldots, \alpha_{n-1}, \beta)$ and $\pi(\alpha_1, \ldots, \alpha_{n-1}, \alpha)$ with subscript $n-1$. These should be $\alpha_{k-1}$ (or equivalently the $\pi$ should be over $k$ components, not $n$), since at this point in the ancestry there are $k$ lineages, not $n$. The next line correctly uses $A_k - \alpha$, suggesting the $n$ subscripts are typos for $k$.

comments.title: Inconsistent use of equation references for IS formula
comments.quote: For equation (8) to hold it is necessary to restrict attention to the subclass $\mathcal{M}$ of these distributions
comments.explanation: The text references 'equation (8)' as the IS identity that needs to hold, but equation (8) is the general IS formula $L(\theta) = \int \pi_{\theta}(A_n \mid \mathcal{H}) \frac{P_{\theta}(\mathcal{H})}{Q_{\theta}(\mathcal{H})} Q_{\theta}(\mathcal{H}) d\mathcal{H}$, which is labeled as part of the display containing both (8) and (9). The numbering in the paper appears to assign (8) to the integral form and (9) to the Monte Carlo approximation. The reference to (8) here seems correct in intent but the paper's equation numbering is somewhat ambiguous since the integral and the approximation share a single display.

comments.title: Missing factor of $\psi(H_{-m})$ in the proof sketch of Theorem 1
comments.quote: The value of $C$ follows from the fact that events must occur at total rate $k(k-1+\theta) / 2$ when there are $k$ lineages in the ancestry (see Stephens (2000)).
comments.explanation: The proof of Theorem 1 derives the backward transition rates for mutations and coalescences but does not explicitly verify that the resulting backward chain, when combined with the stationary distribution $\psi(\cdot)$ for the MRCA type, yields the correct joint distribution $P_{\theta}(\mathcal{H} \mid A_n)$. The normalization constant $C = n(n-1+\theta)/2$ is stated to follow from the total event rate, but this argument implicitly assumes that the sum of all backward rates equals the total forward rate $k(k-1+\theta)/2$, which requires showing that $\sum_{\alpha} [\text{mutation rates from } \alpha + \text{coalescence rates of } \alpha] = k(k-1+\theta)/2$. This verification is deferred to Stephens (2000) without proof here, leaving a gap in the self-contained presentation.

comments.title: Proof of Proposition 1(b) assumes specific coalescent time distribution
comments.quote: The result follows from the fact that $m_{1}+m_{2}$ is geometric with parameter $\theta /(1+\theta)$, independently of $\alpha_{1}$.
comments.explanation: The proof states that $m_1 + m_2$ (total number of mutations on both branches of a two-sample coalescent tree) is geometric with parameter $\theta/(1+\theta)$. For two lineages, the coalescence time $T$ is exponential with rate 1 (in coalescent units), and mutations occur at rate $\theta/2$ per lineage. The total number of mutations on both branches is Poisson with mean $2 \cdot (\theta/2) \cdot T = \theta T$. Since $T \sim \text{Exp}(1)$, the total mutation count is geometric with success probability $1/(1+\theta)$, meaning $P(m_1+m_2 = k) = (1/(1+\theta))(\theta/(1+\theta))^k$ for $k=0,1,2,\ldots$. The paper says the parameter is $\theta/(1+\theta)$. If 'parameter' means the probability of continuation (not stopping), this is correct. But in Definition 1, the geometric distribution has parameter $\theta/(n+\theta)$ described as the probability used in the geometric number of mutations. For $n=1$, this gives $\theta/(1+\theta)$, which is consistent. However, the claim that $m_1+m_2$ has this distribution requires that the total number of mutations on both branches of the tree (not just one) follows this geometric. In fact, $m_1+m_2$ is geometric with the zero-probability $1/(1+\theta)$, matching $\hat{\pi}$ which uses parameter $\theta/(1+\theta)$ for a single additional chromosome. This works out correctly only because the two-sample coalescent has a specific structure. The proof is correct but the reasoning could be clearer about why the sum $m_1+m_2$ (rather than just $m_2$) matches the geometric in Definition 1.

comments.title: Reference to equation (2) should be singular or clarified
comments.quote: noting that $\pi_{\theta}\left(A_{n} \mid \mathcal{H}\right)$ is easily calculated (using equations (2))
comments.explanation: The text says 'equations (2)' (plural) but there is only one equation labeled (2), which gives $\pi_{\theta}(A_n \mid \mathcal{H}) = \pi_{\theta}(A_n \mid H_0)$. This is a minor notation inconsistency.

comments.title: Duplicate reference label for Griffiths and Tavaré (1994b,c)
comments.quote:
(1994b) Ancestral inference in population genetics. Statist. Sci., 9, 307-319.
$\_\_\_\_$ (1994b) Sampling theory for neutral alleles in a varying environment. Phil. Trans. R. Soc. Lond. B, 344, 403410.
comments.explanation: In the reference list, two different Griffiths and Tavaré papers from 1994 are both labeled '(1994b)'. The second one should be labeled '(1994c)', as it is referred to as such in the text (e.g., 'Griffiths and Tavaré, 1994a, b, c'). This is a bibliographic error.

comments.title: Equation (11) derivation contains a non-standard step
comments.explanation: The second equality writes $\pi_{\theta}(A_n \mid \mathcal{H}) P_{\theta}(\mathcal{H}) = P_{\theta}(\mathcal{H} \cap A_n)$. This uses the notation $P_{\theta}(\mathcal{H} \cap A_n)$ to mean the joint probability of both $\mathcal{H}$ and $A_n$, which equals $\pi_{\theta}(A_n \mid \mathcal{H}) P_{\theta}(\mathcal{H})$ by definition. However, the notation $P_{\theta}(\mathcal{H} \cap A_n)$ is somewhat unusual since $A_n$ is a function of $\mathcal{H}$ (specifically $H_0$), so $\mathcal{H} \cap A_n$ as an intersection of events is just $\{\mathcal{H} : H_0 \text{ consistent with } A_n\}$. The derivation is correct but the notation $P_{\theta}(\mathcal{H} \cap A_n)$ could be misleading; $P_{\theta}(\mathcal{H}, A_n)$ would be more standard for a joint probability.

comments.title: Transition probabilities in equations (1) use $n$ ambiguously
comments.quote:
$$p_{\theta}\left(H_{i} \mid H_{i-1}\right)= \begin{cases}\frac{n_{\alpha}}{n} \frac{\theta}{n-1+\theta} P_{\alpha \beta} & \text { if } H_{i}=H_{i-1}-\alpha+\beta \\ \frac{n_{\alpha}}{n} \frac{n-1}{n-1+\theta} & \text { if } H_{i}=H_{i-1}+\alpha \\ 0 & \text { otherwise }\end{cases}$$

where $n$ is the number of chromosomes in $H_{i-1}$
comments.explanation: The variable $n$ is used both for the sample size (a fixed quantity throughout the paper, e.g., 'a random sample of $n$ chromosomes') and here as the number of chromosomes in the current state $H_{i-1}$ of the history, which varies as the history is constructed forward from the MRCA. While the text clarifies that $n$ here refers to the number of chromosomes in $H_{i-1}$, this overloading of notation with the sample size $n$ used everywhere else in the paper is a source of potential confusion.

comments.title: Claim about IS estimator variance for $Q_{\theta}^{\text{SD}}$ at $\theta=15$
comments.quote: For $\theta=15.0$, although neither method produces a very accurate estimate of the likelihood using 20000 samples, the samples from $Q_{\theta}^{\mathrm{GT}}$ underestimate the likelihood by seven orders of magnitude.
comments.explanation: The paper notes that for $\theta=15$, 20000 samples from $Q_{\theta}^{\text{SD}}$ give an estimate of $4.74 \times 10^{-12}$ while $10^7$ samples give $5.37 \times 10^{-12}$. The standard error from the short run ($1.39 \times 10^{-13}$) and the long run ($8.08 \times 10^{-14}$) change by less than a factor of 2, which the authors correctly note indicates the standard error 'severely underestimates the standard deviation in this case.' This means that even $Q_{\theta}^{\text{SD}}$ may have importance weights with infinite (or very large) variance at $\theta=15$, which somewhat undermines the claim that the method 'substantially outperforms existing IS algorithms' in all cases. The authors are transparent about this issue but the abstract's claim of 'efficiency typically improved by several orders of magnitude' could be misleading for cases like this.

comments.title: Coalescence rate derivation in Theorem 1 proof has presentation issue
comments.explanation: This expression appears to be a ratio (the numerator on the first line, denominator on the second), but the formatting makes it look like a product. Comparing with the analogous mutation calculation above, where the ratio is clearly displayed with a fraction bar, this coalescence calculation should have a division sign or fraction bar between the two lines. As written, it appears that the sum in the numerator is being multiplied by the denominator, which would be dimensionally incorrect. This is a typesetting/presentation error.

comments.title: Definition 1 geometric distribution parameterization
comments.quote: choosing an individual from $A_{n}$ uniformly at random, and then mutating it according to the mutation matrix $P$ a geometric number of times, with parameter $\theta /(n+\theta)$
comments.explanation: The 'geometric number of times' with parameter $\theta/(n+\theta)$ is ambiguous because there are two common parameterizations of the geometric distribution: one where the parameter is the success (stopping) probability, and one where it is the continuation probability. From equation (17), the probability of exactly $m$ mutations is $(\theta/(n+\theta))^m \cdot n/(n+\theta)$, which means $n/(n+\theta)$ is the stopping probability and $\theta/(n+\theta)$ is the continuation probability. The text should clarify which convention is being used, especially since the geometric distribution starting from $m=0$ (including the possibility of zero mutations) is being used here.

rag_local.label: RAG Local
rag_local.overall_feedback:
## High-Level Feedback

This is a highly influential and well-crafted paper that makes a substantial methodological contribution to computational inference in population genetics. The writing is exceptionally clear in motivating the problem: the authors lucidly explain why full likelihood-based inference is both important and computationally challenging, situating their contribution precisely within the landscape of existing approaches (Griffiths-Tavaré importance sampling and Felsenstein-school MCMC). The key intellectual insight—characterizing the optimal importance sampling proposal distribution via time-reversal of genealogical processes and then developing a practicable approximation to it—is elegantly framed, and the claimed efficiency gains of "several orders of magnitude" over existing IS methods, if substantiated in the later sections, would represent a major advance. The paper also demonstrates commendable scientific balance in honestly acknowledging that MCMC methods retain advantages in certain ("less constrained") problem settings rather than overclaiming universal superiority. One potential concern from the introduction alone is that the paper promises a great deal—new methodology, comparisons with both IS and MCMC competitors across multiple genetic systems, diagnostics discussion, and general insights about when IS versus MCMC is preferable—which risks either superficial treatment of some topics or an unwieldy paper length. Additionally, while the abstract and introduction effectively convey the high-level narrative, the precise meaning of key qualitative distinctions (e.g., "constrained" versus "unconstrained" genetic structures) is deferred, which may leave readers uncertain about the scope of applicability until well into the paper. The historical contextualization is thorough and appropriate, though the connection to Edwards (1970) and Whittle (1970), while intellectually interesting, could be more concisely handled to maintain focus on the core contribution.

comments.title: Incorrect simplification of numerator
comments.explanation: The numerator π_θ(A_n | H) · P_θ(H) equals P_θ(A_n ∩ H) only if π_θ(A_n | H) = P_θ(A_n | H). Throughout the paper, π_θ(A_n | H) denotes the probability of the data given the ancestry H (a emission/observation probability), while P_θ(H) is the prior on ancestries. Their product is the joint P_θ(A_n, H) = P_θ(A_n | H) · P_θ(H) only if π_θ(A_n | H) is the same object as P_θ(A_n | H). The notation switch from π to P in forming P_θ(H ∩ A_n) is at minimum a notational inconsistency, though the intended mathematical content (joint = likelihood × prior) is standard.

comments.title: Result should equal L(θ) for every H, yielding zero variance, but the third equality is not fully justified
comments.explanation: By Bayes' theorem, P_θ(H | A_n) = P_θ(H ∩ A_n) / P_θ(A_n), so P_θ(H ∩ A_n) / P_θ(H | A_n) = P_θ(A_n). This step is correct in substance. However, the paper uses π_θ(A_n) = L(θ) here, while earlier L(θ) was defined via an integral involving π_θ(A_n | H) (a conditional probability notation using π) and P_θ(H). The identification π_θ(A_n) = P_θ(A_n) is never explicitly stated, which is a minor notational inconsistency.

comments.title: Inconsistent subscript: n-1 used instead of k-1
comments.explanation: The passage uses the subscript 'n-1' in both the numerator and denominator of the first line, but the surrounding context (paras 68–69) consistently uses 'k' for the number of ancestral lineages and indexes the configuration as (α₁, …, α_{k-1}, ·). The subscript should be k-1, not n-1.

comments.title: Left-hand side is a conditional probability but written as a ratio incorrectly structured
comments.explanation: The left-hand side appears to be intended as a conditional probability (the joint probability divided by the probability of the conditioning event), but the division sign or fraction bar is missing between the two probability expressions. As typeset, the second line looks like a separate factor being multiplied rather than a denominator. It should be a fraction with the joint probability in the numerator and P{A_k(t)=...} in the denominator.

comments.title: Sum over β in the numerator should collapse, not remain as written
comments.explanation: The sum over β of P{A_k(t-δ) = (α₁,...,α_{k-2}, α, β)} equals the marginal probability P{the first k-1 components are (α₁,...,α_{k-2}, α)}, which by exchangeability equals π(α₁,...,α_{k-2}, α). The simplification to δ/π(α|A_k - α) then requires that this marginal divided by π(α₁,...,α_{k-2}, α, α) gives 1/π(α|A_k - α). This is consistent. However, the factor δ appears incorrectly placed: the coalescence rate for a specific pair looking down should contribute a factor of 1 (the look-down rate per pair), but the δ factor in the numerator seems to come from the probability of the event in time interval δ. The expression is dimensionally consistent but the intermediate step obscures where the coalescence rate factor comes from, and the placement of δ multiplying the sum (rather than being part of the conditional transition probability) is not clearly justified from the preceding steps.

comments.title: Incorrect placement of exponent on matrix product
comments.explanation: The notation $(\lambda_{n} P)_{\alpha \beta}^{m}$ is ambiguous/incorrect. It should be $(\lambda_{n} P)^{m}_{\alpha \beta}$, i.e., the $m$-th power of the matrix $\lambda_n P$, then taking the $(\alpha,\beta)$ entry. As written, the exponent $m$ appears as a superscript on the $(\alpha,\beta)$ component, which could be read as raising the scalar entry to the $m$-th power rather than taking the $(\alpha,\beta)$ entry of the matrix power. This is a notational issue that is inconsistent with standard convention used elsewhere in the paper (e.g., $(P^m)_{\alpha\beta}$ in the first line).

comments.title: Incorrect ratio factor for long vs. short run
comments.quote: the longer run should result in standard errors which are smaller by a factor of $\sqrt{ } 500 \approx 22$
comments.explanation: √500 ≈ 22.36, which is correct numerically. However, the factor of 500 implies the long run uses 500 times as many samples as the short run, i.e., 10,000,000 samples vs. 20,000. From the context (para 128), the authors ran 1 million samples in their own long run, not 10 million. If the long run used 1,000,000 samples and the short run used 20,000, the ratio would be 50, giving √50 ≈ 7. If the long run used 10,000,000 samples (500 × 20,000), then √500 ≈ 22 is correct, but this is not clearly established in the surrounding text. The paper mentions 1 million samples taking 72 hours for Q_GT, but the Q_SD long run length is referenced only via Table 1. Without seeing Table 1, the factor of 500 may or may not be correct, but it is at least potentially inconsistent with the 1 million sample run mentioned in para 128.

comments.title: Inconsistent capitalization of probability function
comments.explanation: The transition probability is written with an uppercase 'P' here, but in the surrounding context (para 216, 217, 218) it is also referred to with both uppercase 'P' and lowercase 'p'. More importantly, the lowercase 'p' is used throughout for the sampling probability p(·), so using uppercase 'P' for the conditional transition probability is intentional. However, in para 217 the same quantity is written as 'P(H_j | H_{j-1}')' with uppercase P, while in para 218 it switches to lowercase 'p(H_j | H_{j-1}')'. This is a notation inconsistency across the passages rather than an error in this specific equation.

comments.title: Incorrect index range in the product of f factors
comments.explanation: From the recursion in para 219, p(H_j) = f(H_j) * sum q(...) p(H_{j-1}'), unrolling from j=0 down to absorption at -m (where p(H_{-m}) is known and equals 1 for the single-ancestor state) gives p(H_0) = E_q{ f(H_0) f(H_{-1}) ... f(H_{-m+1}) * p(H_{-m}) }. If p(H_{-m})=1, the product should be f(H_0) f(H_{-1}) ... f(H_{-m+1}), not including f(H_{-m}). Including f(H_{-m}) amounts to one extra application of the recursion beyond the absorption point. This is consistent with para 223, where the forward-path numerator contains transitions p(H_0|H_{-1})...p(H_{-m+1}|H_{-m}) p(H_{-m}), i.e., m transition factors times p(H_{-m}), not m+1 factors.

comments.title: Misplaced factorial notation
comments.explanation: The factorial should apply to m^i, so the correct notation is (m^i)! or m^i!. As written, 'm^{i!}' suggests raising m to the power i!, rather than taking the factorial of m^i. The denominator should be written as (m^{i})! to be unambiguous.

comments.title: Incorrect conditional posterior density formula (missing normalization)
comments.explanation: Given the genealogy H^i with total branch length T^i and m^i mutations, the number of mutations is Poisson with parameter T^i θ/2. Under a uniform (improper) prior on θ, the conditional posterior π(θ | H^i, A_n) is a Gamma distribution: Gamma(m^i + 1, T^i/2). The correct density is (T^i/2)^{m^i+1} θ^{m^i} exp(-T^i θ/2) / (m^i)!. The formula as written has the prefactor T^i/2 multiplied by (T^i θ/2)^{m^i}, which gives (T^i/2)^{m^i+1} θ^{m^i}, matching the correct Gamma shape. So the overall structure is actually consistent with a Gamma(m^i+1, T^i/2) density, provided the factorial is read as (m^i)!. The only clear typographical error is the factorial placement.

comments.title: Variable n used with conflicting meanings
comments.explanation: The variable n is used both as the number of MCMC samples (in the sum 1/n and the upper limit of summation) and as the sample size indexing the allelic data A_n. These are different quantities: A_n refers to the observed genetic data from n sampled individuals, whereas the sum should be over the number of MCMC iterations/samples (which, per para 258, is the number of sampled genealogies from 10^7 iterations thinned every 500). A different symbol (e.g., N or K) should be used for the number of MCMC samples to avoid ambiguity.

comments.title: Missing index variable in summation
comments.explanation: The summation is written as \sum^{n} without specifying the index variable and its lower bound. It should be written as \sum_{i=1}^{n} to clearly indicate summation over the MCMC samples indexed by i.

comments.title: Incorrect index in numerator of recursive weight factor
comments.explanation: In the recursive decomposition, the new factor in the numerator should be p_θ(H_{-(t-1)} | H_{-t}), representing the transition probability from H_{-t} to H_{-(t-1)}. Instead, it is written as p_θ(H_{-(t-1)} | H_{-1}), which conditions on H_{-1} rather than H_{-t}. This is inconsistent with the full product in the numerator on the left-hand side, where each factor is p_θ(H_{-(k-1)} | H_{-k}).

comments.title: Incorrect subscript in final weight formula
comments.explanation: The term $p_{\theta}(|H_1|=n+1)$ uses subscript 1 (positive), but in the coalescent framework described here, time indices are negative (going backwards). This should likely be $|H_{-1}|=n+1$ or similar, consistent with the negative-time indexing used throughout the paper (e.g., $H_{-1}, H_{-t}$, etc.).

comments.title: Inconsistency in recursive weight formula (para 277) referenced by final weight
comments.quote: $w_{-t}=\frac{p_{\theta}\left(H_{-(t-1)} \mid H_{-t}\right) \ldots p_{\theta}\left(H_{0} \mid H_{-1}\right)}{q_{0}\left(H_{-t} \mid H_{-(t-1)}\right) \ldots q_{0}\left(H_{-1} \mid H_{0}\right)} \equiv w_{-(t-1)} \frac{p_{\theta}\left(H_{-(t-1)} \mid H_{-1}\right)}{q_{0}\left(H_{-t} \mid H_{-(t-1)}\right)}$
comments.explanation: In the recursive decomposition on the right-hand side, the numerator of the incremental ratio should be $p_{\theta}(H_{-(t-1)} \mid H_{-t})$ (the transition from $H_{-t}$ to $H_{-(t-1)}$), but it is written as $p_{\theta}(H_{-(t-1)} \mid H_{-1})$, which is inconsistent with the left-hand side product and is almost certainly a typo.

comments.title: Incorrect identification of the normalizing constant in (b)
comments.quote: $L\left(\theta \mid Y_{\text{obs}}\right)$ is the normalizing constant of $p\left(Y_{\text{mis}} \mid Y_{\text{obs}}, \theta\right)$
comments.explanation: From the identity L(θ|Y_obs) = ∫ p(Y_mis, Y_obs|θ) μ(dY_mis), the likelihood L(θ|Y_obs) is the normalizing constant that converts the unnormalized density p(Y_mis, Y_obs|θ) (as a function of Y_mis) into the normalized conditional density p(Y_mis|Y_obs, θ). That is, p(Y_mis|Y_obs, θ) = p(Y_mis, Y_obs|θ) / L(θ|Y_obs). So L(θ|Y_obs) is indeed the normalizing constant of the unnormalized density p(Y_mis, Y_obs|θ), not 'the normalizing constant of p(Y_mis|Y_obs, θ)'. The density p(Y_mis|Y_obs, θ) is already normalized (it integrates to 1). The statement as written is misleading/incorrect: it says L is the normalizing constant 'of' the normalized density rather than 'of' the unnormalized density.

comments.title: Incorrect denominator in importance weight
comments.explanation: The samples are drawn from the mixture Q (defined in the preceding paragraph, expression (37)), not from Q_{\theta_0}. The importance weight should be P_{\theta}(\mathcal{H}^{(i)}) / Q(\mathcal{H}^{(i)}), consistent with the definition w_{\theta}(\mathcal{H}) = P_{\theta}(\mathcal{H}) / Q(\mathcal{H}). The subsequent estimator in para 326 similarly uses Q_{\theta_0} in the denominator, suggesting this may be a systematic notational issue, but it is inconsistent with the mixture sampling proposal Q described in para 322.

comments.title: Incorrect variance reduction factor
comments.quote: Its asymptotic variance is $\left(1-\rho_{\theta}^{2}\right)^{-1}$ times smaller
comments.explanation: The standard result for variance reduction via a control variate is that the variance is multiplied by the factor (1 - \rho^2), i.e., it is (1 - \rho^2) times the original variance. Saying it is (1 - \rho^2)^{-1} times *smaller* is confusing/incorrect; it should be (1 - \rho^2)^{-1} times smaller only if interpreted as the original variance divided by (1-\rho^2)^{-1}, which gives (1-\rho^2) times the original. The phrasing '(1-\rho^2)^{-1} times smaller' would more naturally mean dividing by (1-\rho^2)^{-1}, but the intended meaning is likely that the variance is reduced by the factor (1-\rho^2). The correct statement is that the asymptotic variance is (1-\rho^2) times that of expression (12), or equivalently (1-\rho^2)^{-1} times *larger* efficiency.

rag_retrieved.label: RAG Retrieved
rag_retrieved.overall_feedback:
## High-Level Feedback

This is a highly influential and well-crafted paper that makes a significant methodological contribution to computational inference in population genetics. The writing is exceptionally clear for a technically demanding subject, effectively guiding readers from the biological motivation through the mathematical framework to practical implementation. The paper's central contribution—developing a new importance sampling proposal distribution that approximates the (characterized but intractable) optimal proposal by exploiting detailed knowledge of coalescent genealogical processes—is both elegant and practically impactful, with reported efficiency gains of several orders of magnitude over existing IS methods. The authors demonstrate commendable intellectual honesty in their comparative analysis, acknowledging that their IS approach outperforms MCMC in some settings ("constrained" genetic structures) but not others, rather than overclaiming universal superiority. The abstract and introduction do an excellent job of situating the work within the broader statistical literature, making it accessible beyond the genetics community and drawing attention to the general lesson that IS can be competitive with MCMC when good proposal distributions can be constructed from domain knowledge. One potential concern is that the paper's practical impact depends heavily on how well the approximation to the optimal proposal distribution generalizes to more complex and realistic genetic models (e.g., with recombination, population structure, or selection), and the introduction could more explicitly discuss the theoretical limitations or assumptions under which the approximation is expected to degrade. Additionally, while the authors rightly flag that diagnostics for convergence in both IS and MCMC have been underappreciated in genetics applications, a more systematic treatment of diagnostic tools—beyond the discussion promised later—would strengthen the paper's practical guidance. Overall, this is an exemplary piece of applied statistical methodology that successfully bridges probability theory, computational statistics, and genetics.

comments.title: Incorrect intermediate step using joint probability instead of conditional × marginal
comments.explanation: The numerator π_θ(A_n|H)·P_θ(H) equals P_θ(H ∩ A_n) (the joint probability/density of H and A_n), which is correct. However, dividing P_θ(H ∩ A_n) by P_θ(H|A_n) gives P_θ(A_n) by Bayes' theorem [since P(H∩A)=P(H|A)P(A)]. The notation P_θ(H ∩ A_n) is somewhat inconsistent with the rest of the paper—H is a history (a continuous latent variable with density P_θ) while A_n is discrete data with probability π_θ, so writing their 'joint' as P_θ(H ∩ A_n) conflates two different types of probability measures. Nevertheless, the final result L(θ) = π_θ(A_n) is correct, so this is a notational/presentational issue rather than a substantive mathematical error.

comments.title: Incorrect second term inside the parentheses — should depend on β
comments.explanation: The second term (n_α − 1)/(n − 1 + θ) does not depend on β, yet it is placed inside the sum multiplied by π̂(β | H_i − α)/π̂(α | H_i − α). When summed over β, the factor Σ_β π̂(β | H_i − α) = 1, so the β-independent piece contributes (n_α − 1)/[(n − 1 + θ) π̂(α | H_i − α)]. For the identity to hold this must equal 1 minus the mutation part, which is only true under a specific relationship. More concretely, the term (n_α − 1)/(n − 1 + θ) should carry an indicator or Kronecker delta restricting it to β = α (i.e. it should be multiplied by 1_{β=α} or equivalently taken outside the sum), because coalescence only occurs between two chromosomes of the same type α. As written, the coalescence contribution is incorrectly spread over all types β, and the equation is only accidentally correct because summing π̂(β|H_i − α) over β gives 1. In fact the equation does hold numerically, but its presentation is misleading/inconsistent with the derivation in para 116, where the second term is pulled out of the sum (using Σ_β π̂(β|·) = 1). The cleaner and logically correct form would separate the coalescence term from the sum or restrict it to β = α.

comments.title: Incorrect reference to estimator equation number
comments.quote: the estimator (8) is asymptotically normal with variance σ²/M
comments.explanation: The IS estimator for the likelihood is defined in equation (9) (see para 46-47), not equation (8). The passage references 'estimator (8)' but should reference 'estimator (9)'.

comments.title: Missing factor of n_α/n in the coalescence term
comments.explanation: The coalescence term should include a factor of n_α/n (or equivalently n_α(n_α−1)/(n(n−1))) to account for the probability of choosing two lineages of the same type α. The correct coalescence sum should be ∑ (n_α(n_α−1))/(n(n−1)) · p(n − e_α), but the equation as written has only (n_α−1)/(n−1), missing the n_α/n factor.

comments.title: Incorrect lower bound of the product in the expectation
comments.explanation: The product should not include f(H_{-m}). At step -m there is a single ancestor (absorption), so p(H_{-m}) is a known boundary value (typically 1 or a known stationary probability), not another factor of f. Comparing with the analogous formula in para 38–39, the product Π F(B_j) runs from j=0 to τ but the boundary term at absorption provides the base case. Here, iterating equation (219) from H_0 down to H_{-m}, one obtains p(H_0) = E_q{ f(H_0) f(H_{-1}) … f(H_{-m+1}) · p(H_{-m}) }. Including f(H_{-m}) in place of p(H_{-m}) is incorrect unless p(H_{-m}) = f(H_{-m}), which is not generally the case.

comments.title: Incorrect summation index: n used instead of N (number of MCMC samples)
comments.explanation: Here n denotes the sample size of the genetic data (the number of individuals in A_n), but the sum is over MCMC samples indexed by i. The summation limit and the normalizing constant 1/n should refer to the number of MCMC iterations (e.g., N), not the data sample size n. Using n for both conflates two entirely different quantities.

comments.title: Incorrect subscript in the recursive weight numerator
comments.explanation: In the recursive decomposition, the numerator of the new factor should be p_θ(H_{-(t-1)} | H_{-t}), representing the forward transition probability from H_{-t} to H_{-(t-1)}. Instead, it is written as p_θ(H_{-(t-1)} | H_{-1}), which references H_{-1} rather than H_{-t}. This is clearly a typo: H_{-1} should be H_{-t}.

comments.title: Incorrect subscript in weight recursion (para 277)
comments.explanation: In the recursive definition of the weight, the numerator should be p_θ(H_{-(t-1)} | H_{-t}), not p_θ(H_{-(t-1)} | H_{-1}). The conditioning should be on the state one step back in time (H_{-t}), consistent with the product in the left-hand side of the equation.

comments.title: Incorrect notation in final weight expression
comments.explanation: The term p_θ(|H_1| = n+1) is problematic. In the paper's notation, H_0 = A_n is the observed sample and the history runs backward with negative indices. There is no H_1 defined in this context (positive index). This likely should involve |H_0| = n or a related quantity. The factor appears to be a typographical or notational error.

comments.title: Inconsistent subscript notation for resampling indices
comments.explanation: The subscripts on the stopping times use indices 1 through M (i.e., i_1, i_2, ..., i_M), suggesting each sample j has its own stopping time i_j. This notation is consistent. However, the definition i_j = min{t : |H_{-t}^{(j)}| = i} uses 'i' both as the target size (a fixed value determining when to resample) and as the base letter for the stopping times i_j. This creates a notational collision/ambiguity: 'i' on the left side of i_j is part of the variable name, while 'i' on the right side of the definition is the target sample size. A clearer notation would use a different letter for one of these, e.g., defining the target size as 'k' so that i_j = min{t : |H_{-t}^{(j)}| = k}.

comments.title: Incorrect factorization of the joint density
comments.explanation: The joint density p(Y_mis, Y_obs | θ) is factored as p(Y_obs | θ) p(Y_mis | Y_obs, θ), but this is incorrect because p(Y_obs | θ) = L(θ | Y_obs) is the very quantity being computed (the likelihood/normalizing constant). The correct factorization of the joint density should be p(Y_mis, Y_obs | θ) = p(Y_obs | Y_mis, θ) p(Y_mis | θ), which is the standard missing-data decomposition used elsewhere in the paper (e.g., para 33: π_θ(A_n | H) P_θ(H)). The written factorization is technically a valid identity but renders the equation tautological: the right-hand side equals p(Y_obs | θ) · 1 = L(θ | Y_obs), so it provides no computational or conceptual insight. Given the context — the author states this identity tells us that L(θ | Y_obs) is the normalizing constant of p(Y_mis | Y_obs, θ) with the complete-data density p(Y_mis, Y_obs | θ) being the unnormalized density — the intended factorization should present p(Y_mis, Y_obs | θ) as the unnormalized form of p(Y_mis | Y_obs, θ), i.e., p(Y_mis, Y_obs | θ) = L(θ | Y_obs) · p(Y_mis | Y_obs, θ), which is what is written but defeats the purpose since L(θ | Y_obs) appears on both sides. The equation should instead just be L(θ | Y_obs) = ∫ p(Y_mis, Y_obs | θ) μ(dY_mis) without the second equality, or the second equality should use the other factorization p(Y_obs | Y_mis, θ) p(Y_mis | θ).

comments.title: Inconsistent notation: $w_\theta$ vs $\omega_\theta$
comments.explanation: The weight function is defined here as $w_\theta(\mathcal{H})$, but in para 321 it is referenced as $\omega_\theta(\mathcal{H})$ (Greek letter omega). This is a notation inconsistency within the same discussion block.

comments.title: Incorrect comparison benchmark for expression (39)
comments.quote: it is never worse than the regression estimator from an importance sample of size $M \gamma_{k}$ from $Q_{k}$
comments.explanation: Owen and Zhou (2000) proved that the multiple importance sampling estimator (such as expression (39)) is never worse than the *simple* (non-regression) importance sampling estimator using only the best single component distribution $Q_k$ with its allocated sample size $M\gamma_k$. The passage incorrectly states the comparison is against a 'regression estimator' from that single component, which is a stronger claim than what Owen and Zhou actually proved.

rag_retrieved_cot.label: RAG Retrieved CoT
rag_retrieved_cot.overall_feedback:
## High-Level Feedback

This is a highly influential and well-crafted paper that makes a significant methodological contribution to computational population genetics by developing a new importance sampling (IS) algorithm grounded in a principled approximation to the optimal proposal distribution. The paper's greatest strengths lie in its clear articulation of the problem structure—characterizing the optimal IS distribution via time-reversal of genealogical processes and then constructing a tractable approximation—and in the impressive empirical gains reported (several orders of magnitude improvement over existing IS methods). The writing is exceptionally clear for a technically demanding topic, effectively bridging population genetics and computational statistics for a broad Royal Statistical Society audience. The framing of population genetics inference as a missing-data problem with both IS and MCMC as viable computational strategies is insightful, and the honest comparative assessment—acknowledging that MCMC retains advantages in less "constrained" genetic settings—adds considerable credibility. One potential concern, based on the introduction alone, is that the paper may not fully address scalability to the complex, high-dimensional problems (e.g., large recombining genomic regions or whole-genome data) that were already emerging at the time of writing; the caveat that "one can never be sure that the algorithm has been run for sufficiently long" applies to IS as well as MCMC, and the paper would benefit from a more rigorous treatment of diagnostics and convergence guarantees for IS in these settings. Additionally, while the claim of "several orders of magnitude" improvement is striking, the generality of this claim across diverse demographic scenarios and data types warrants careful scrutiny in the results sections. Overall, this paper represents a landmark contribution that shaped subsequent developments in both coalescent-based inference and the broader understanding of when IS can compete with or complement MCMC methods.

comments.title: Incorrect index variable: n-1 should be k-1
comments.explanation: In para 68, the ancestry consists of k lineages with configuration (α₁, ..., α_{k-1}, α). Para 69 correctly uses α_{k-1}. However, in this passage, the subscript switches to n-1 instead of k-1. The variable n refers to the sample size (paras 64-65), not the current number of lineages k. Both occurrences of α_{n-1} in the first line should be α_{k-1}.

comments.title: Incorrect equation reference for the IS estimator
comments.quote: the estimator (8) is asymptotically normal with variance $\sigma^{2} / M$
comments.explanation: The IS estimator (the sample mean of importance weights) is defined and labeled as equation (9) in para 46, not equation (8). The passage should reference 'estimator (9)' to be consistent with the numbering used elsewhere in the paper.

comments.title: Product includes f(H_{-m}) instead of p(H_{-m}) at the boundary
comments.explanation: Unrolling the recursion p(H_j) = f(H_j) * sum_q p(H_{j-1}') from j=0 down to j=-(m-1) yields p(H_0) = E_q{f(H_0) f(H_{-1}) ... f(H_{-(m-1)}) * p(H_{-m})}. The last factor should be p(H_{-m}) (the boundary probability for the single ancestor), not f(H_{-m}). Replacing p(H_{-m}) with f(H_{-m}) is incorrect unless a special convention equates them at the boundary, which is not stated.

comments.title: Misplaced factorial in denominator
comments.explanation: The factorial symbol is placed inside the superscript, yielding m^{i!} (m raised to the power i-factorial) instead of the intended (m^i)! (factorial of m^i). The LaTeX should be (m^{i})! or m^{i}! with the exclamation mark outside the braces.

comments.title: Notation conflict: $n$ used for both sample size and number of MCMC iterations
comments.explanation: Throughout the paper, $n$ denotes the number of individuals in the sample $A_n$. In this equation, $n$ is reused to denote the number of MCMC samples (genealogical histories $\mathcal{H}^i$), which is a different quantity. The number of MCMC samples should use a distinct symbol (e.g., $N$) to avoid confusion with the sample size.

comments.title: Typo in recursive weight formula: $H_{-1}$ should be $H_{-t}$
comments.explanation: In the recursive decomposition, the incremental forward probability should be $p_{\theta}(H_{-(t-1)} \mid H_{-t})$, not $p_{\theta}(H_{-(t-1)} \mid H_{-1})$. The subscript $-1$ appears to be a typo for $-t$. This is clear from the fact that $w_{-t} / w_{-(t-1)}$ should equal $p_{\theta}(H_{-(t-1)} \mid H_{-t}) / q_0(H_{-t} \mid H_{-(t-1)})$.

comments.title: Incorrect subscript in final weight formula
comments.explanation: The term p_θ(|H_1| = n+1) uses the index 1 (forward in time from the sample H_0), but the history is constructed backward in time with indices H_0, H_{-1}, ..., H_{-m}. H_1 is not part of the constructed history. This appears to be a notation error; it should likely reference H_{-1} (the first backward step from the sample), consistent with the backward-time indexing used throughout the paper (paras 55, 277).

comments.title: Typo in recursive weight ratio numerator (para 277, referenced by passage)
comments.explanation: In the recursive definition of w_{-t}, the numerator of the ratio should be p_θ(H_{-(t-1)} | H_{-t}), not p_θ(H_{-(t-1)} | H_{-1}). The subscript -1 should be -t, as the forward transition probability conditions on the immediately preceding (in backward time) configuration H_{-t}.

rag_top_k_filter.label: RAG Top-K Filter
rag_top_k_filter.overall_feedback:
## High-Level Feedback

This is a highly influential and well-crafted paper that makes a significant methodological contribution to computational population genetics by developing a new importance sampling (IS) algorithm grounded in a principled approximation to the optimal proposal distribution. The writing is exceptionally clear for a technically demanding topic, and the authors do an admirable job of making the problem accessible to a broad statistical audience—appropriately so, given its presentation before the Royal Statistical Society. The paper's greatest strength lies in its intellectual coherence: the authors derive the form of the optimal IS distribution, explain why it is intractable, and then use deep understanding of coalescent genealogical processes to construct a practical approximation, yielding efficiency gains of several orders of magnitude over the Griffiths-Tavaré method. The honest and nuanced comparison with MCMC methods—acknowledging that neither approach uniformly dominates—adds considerable credibility and practical value. One potential concern is that the abstract and introduction, while thorough, could more concisely delineate the specific genetic models and data types to which the method applies, as readers must wait until Section 2 to understand the scope; additionally, the paper would benefit from more explicit discussion of the scalability limitations of the proposed IS approach (e.g., how performance degrades with increasing numbers of loci, recombination, or sample size), since the acknowledgment that "all methods for these problems are extremely computationally intensive" deserves more concrete quantification early on to set appropriate expectations for practitioners.

comments.title: Incorrect equation reference for IS-based likelihood surface estimation
comments.quote: with further IS (via equation (12)) to estimate the likelihood surface
comments.explanation: Based on the paper's structure, equation (12) is referenced here for estimating the likelihood surface from a single proposal distribution, but the relevant equation for this purpose (reweighting likelihoods across different θ values from samples drawn at a single θ₀) is described in para 51 and corresponds to the importance sampling reweighting formula. The reference to equation (12) appears inconsistent; the equation that performs this reweighting role is more likely equation (7) or the equation presented in para 51. Meanwhile, the second framework references equation (7) for the same purpose (estimating the likelihood surface via IS), suggesting the two equation references may be swapped or one is incorrect.

comments.title: Inconsistent use of index n-1 vs k-1 in the first line
comments.explanation: The derivation in para 68-69 is set up with k ancestral lineages and the configuration A_k(t) = (α_1, …, α_{k-1}, α). The first line of the equation should therefore reference (α_1, …, α_{k-1}, β) and (α_1, …, α_{k-1}, α), but instead uses the subscript n-1, which is inconsistent with the k-lineage setup. The index should be k-1, not n-1.

comments.title: Missing division sign on the left-hand side
comments.explanation: The left-hand side should be a ratio (conditional probability), i.e., the joint probability divided by P{A_k(t)=...}, but as typeset the denominator P{A_k(t)=...} appears to be multiplied rather than dividing. Comparing with the analogous mutation derivation in para 69, the left-hand side should have a fraction bar (or division) between the sum and P{A_k(t)=...}.

comments.title: Missing factor of (k-1) in the coalescence rate
comments.explanation: In the look-down construction, line k looks down at rate (k-1) and then picks one of lines 1,...,k-1 uniformly. The event that line k copies specifically line k-1 occurs at rate (k-1)·(1/(k-1))=1, but the numerator should reflect the probability of the look-down event occurring in interval δ, which involves the rate factor. More importantly, the summation over β of P{A_k(t-δ)=(α₁,...,α_{k-2},α,β)} sums over all possible types for line k before the event, but the factor δ alone does not correctly account for the look-down rate. The intermediate step is missing the rate factor from the look-down mechanism (the rate 1 for copying a specific line), and the simplification to sum over β eliminating the joint probability needs more careful justification regarding what happened to the stationary probability terms.

comments.title: Incorrect distribution of total mutations on the two-sample coalescent
comments.quote: The result follows from the fact that $m_{1}+m_{2}$ is geometric with parameter $\theta/(1+\theta)$, independently of $\alpha_{1}$.
comments.explanation: In the n=1 coalescent (conditioning on one observed chromosome, so n=1 and we predict the type of a second), the formula in Definition 1 uses a geometric with parameter θ/(n+θ) = θ/(1+θ).
