involves only coset-representative sets, and no obvious analogue of the $\Lambda''$ term. After reading more carefully, it is clear that here the chain is infinite and its intersection is the trivial lattice $\{0\}$, so the "finest" sublattice term is just $\{0\}$ and can be omitted without effect; this reconciles the two views. Still, it might help some readers if you briefly noted in the text that, for the integer chain, the limiting sublattice is $\{0\}$, which is why the binary expansion can be written purely as a sum of coset representatives.

comments.title: Description of coset representatives in Sec. II.F
comments.quote: Thus the $2^{K}$ binary linear combinations $\left\{\sum a_{k} \boldsymbol{g}_{k}\right\}$ of the generators $\boldsymbol{g}_{k}$ are a system of coset representatives $\left[\Lambda_{k} / \Lambda_{k+1}\right]$ for the partition $\Lambda_{k} / \Lambda_{k+1}$ (this is a special case of a general result for groups of order $2^{K}-$ binary groups-discussed in part II).
comments.explanation:
The sentence

"Thus the $2^{K}$ binary linear combinations $\{\sum a_k \boldsymbol{g}_k\}$ of the generators $\boldsymbol{g}_k$ are a system of coset representatives $[\Lambda_k / \Lambda_{k+1}]$ for the partition $\Lambda_k / \Lambda_{k+1}$ …"

appears inconsistent with the definitions just given. Each intermediate quotient $\Lambda_k / \Lambda_{k+1}$ is explicitly two‑way, so any system of coset representatives for such a partition should contain exactly two vectors, whereas the set $\{\sum a_k \boldsymbol{g}_k\}$ has $2^K$ elements. 

Reading Lemma 2 immediately below makes the intended structure clear: for each $k$, the pair $\{a_k \boldsymbol{g}_k, a_k\in\{0,1\}\}$ serves as representatives for the two-way partition $\Lambda_k / \Lambda_{k+1}$, and the $2^K$ combinations $\{\sum a_k \boldsymbol{g}_k\}$ form a system of representatives for the overall partition $\Lambda / \Lambda'$. To avoid the cardinality mismatch, it would be helpful to correct this sentence so that the role of the $2^K$ combinations is associated with the full partition $\Lambda / \Lambda'$ rather than with a single two-way quotient $\Lambda_k / \Lambda_{k+1}$.

comments.title: Clarity of argument for mod-4 lattice structure
comments.quote:
For a further refinement, let $\Lambda_{e}$ be the set of all points in $\Lambda$ whose coordinates are all even. Then $\Lambda_{e}$ is a lattice, a sublattice of $\Lambda$, with $\boldsymbol{4} \boldsymbol{Z}^{N}$ as a sublattice, so $\Lambda / \Lambda_{e} / \boldsymbol{4} \boldsymbol{Z}^{N}$ is a partition chain, and there is a coset decomposition of the form $\Lambda=\boldsymbol{4} \boldsymbol{Z}^{N}+\left[\Lambda_{e} / \boldsymbol{4} \boldsymbol{Z}^{N}\right]+\left[\Lambda / \Lambda_{e}\right]$. The lattice $\Lambda_{e}$ is clearly a mod-2 lattice scaled by a factor of 2 ; consequently, $\Lambda_{e}=4 \boldsymbol{Z}^{N}+2 C$, where $C$ is a binary ( $N, K^{\prime}$ ) code for some $K^{\prime}$, by Lemma 3; in other words, the coset representatives $\left[\Lambda_{e} / \boldsymbol{4} \boldsymbol{Z}^{N}\right]$ may be taken as $2 C$, or $\left\{2 \sum a_{k} \boldsymbol{g}_{k}\right\}$, where the $\boldsymbol{g}_{k}$ constitute a set of $K^{\prime}$ binary generators for the code $C$. Thus we may take $K^{\prime}$ of the generators to be $2 \boldsymbol{g}_{k}, 1 \leq k \leq K^{\prime}$, and we may write

$$
\Lambda=4 \boldsymbol{Z}^{N}+2 C+\left\{\sum a_{k} \boldsymbol{g}_{k}\right\}
$$

where the $\boldsymbol{g}_{k}, K^{\prime}+1 \leq k \leq K$, are $N$-tuples of integers modulo 4 that are not all even, such that $\left\{\sum a_{k} \boldsymbol{g}_{k}\right\}$ is a system of coset representatives $\left[\Lambda / \Lambda_{e}\right]$. The generators $\left\{2 \boldsymbol{g}_{k}, K^{\prime}+1 \leq k \leq K\right\}$ generate a lattice $\Lambda^{\prime}$ that is a sublattice of $\Lambda_{e}$, whose elements are congruent to $2 \boldsymbol{c}^{\prime}$ modulo 4, where $c^{\prime}$ is a codeword in a binary ( $N, K-K^{\prime}$ ) block code $C^{\prime}$ that is a subcode of the code $C$, so $K-K^{\prime} \leq K^{\prime}$.
comments.explanation:
In the paragraph beginning "For a further refinement, let $\Lambda_e$ be the set of all points in $\Lambda$ whose coordinates are all even," the transition from the generators $2g_k$ for $k>K'$ to the statement about a binary subcode $C'\subset C$ is quite compressed. The text asserts that the lattice generated by $\{2g_k,\,K'+1\le k\le K\}$ has elements congruent to $2c'$ modulo 4 for $c'$ in an $(N,K-K')$ code $C'$ that is a subcode of $C$, but it does not spell out how $C'$ is defined from $C$ or why it must be nested.

Because $\Lambda_e=4\boldsymbol{Z}^N+2C$ has already been established, the intended reasoning is that each $2g_k\in\Lambda_e$ must satisfy $2g_k\equiv 2c'_k\pmod{4}$ for some $c'_k\in C$, and the binary code $C'$ is the subcode of $C$ generated by these $c'_k$. Making this map from $\Lambda_e$ to $C$ explicit, and then noting that the image of the sublattice $\Lambda'\subset\Lambda_e$ under this map is a subcode $C'\subset C$, would more clearly justify the subcode relationship. Adding one or two sentences along these lines would align this real mod‑4 argument with the more explicit complex mod‑2 argument given later in Lemma 4 and make the structure of general mod‑4 lattices easier to follow.

comments.title: Undefined notation for the dual of a Barnes-Wall lattice
comments.quote: Thus $\Lambda(n, n)^{\perp}=\boldsymbol{G}^{N} \simeq \boldsymbol{Z}^{2 N}, \Lambda(0, n)^{\perp}= \Lambda(n)$, and $\Lambda(n-1, n)^{\perp}, n \geq 1$, is the dual $D_{N}^{\perp}$ of the checkerboard lattice $D_{N}$, with code formula $D_{N}^{\perp}=\phi \boldsymbol{G}^{N}+ \mathrm{RM}(0, n)=\phi \boldsymbol{G}^{N}+(N, 1, N)$, where $N=2^{n}$ and $(N, 1, N)$ is the repetition code of length $N$.
comments.explanation:
The sentence listing special cases of the duals of the principal sublattices includes the identity
$\Lambda(0, n)^{\perp}= \Lambda(n)$. The notation $\Lambda(n)$, with a single argument, is not defined elsewhere in the paper; the family is consistently denoted $\Lambda(r, n)$, and Barnes–Wall lattices themselves are written as $\Lambda(0, n)$ (or with subscripts such as $\Lambda_{16}$). Moreover, just above, you have shown that $\Lambda(0, n)$ is self-dual, and the general dual code formula specialized to $r=0$ reproduces the original code formula for $\Lambda(0, n)$. In this context, it appears that the right-hand side should read $\Lambda(0, n)$, and that "$\Lambda(n)$" is a typographical slip introducing undefined notation and an apparent contradiction with the stated self-duality. Clarifying or correcting this identity would prevent possible confusion.

comments.title: Notation for a lattice in Table III
comments.quote:
TABLE III
Useful Lattice Partitions
| Λ | $\Lambda^{\prime}$ | $2 N$ | $\left\|\Lambda / \Lambda^{\prime}\right\|$ | $\mu$ | $\kappa$ | $\rho$ | $d_{\text {min }}^{2}(\Lambda)$ | $d_{\text {min }}^{2}\left(\Lambda^{\prime}\right)$ | $\tilde{N}_{D}$ | $\tilde{N}_{D} /\left\|\Lambda / \Lambda^{\prime}\right\|$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
...
| $D_{8}^{1}$ | $R E_{8}$ | 8 | 32 | 3 | 1 | 3/4 | 2 | 8 | 88 | 2.75 |
...
comments.explanation: The lattice labeled $D_8^1$ in Table III does not seem to be defined anywhere in the paper or in Tables I–II. However, all the numerical parameters in that row (index 32, depth 3, $\kappa=1$, $\rho=3/4$, $d_{\min}^2(\Lambda)=2$, $d_{\min}^2(\Lambda')=8$) match exactly what one obtains for the partition $D_8^{\perp}/R E_8$, where $D_8^{\perp}$ is the dual of the checkerboard lattice $D_8$ as defined in Section II‑H. It would be helpful to correct the notation in Table III from $D_8^1$ to $D_8^{\perp}$ (or otherwise define $D_8^1$ explicitly) so that the table is consistent with the earlier dual‑lattice notation and with the subsequent discussion of the $D_8^{\perp}/R E_8$ partition in Section V.

comments.title: Description of the Ungerboeck encoder in Sec. IV.A
comments.quote: For example, the four-state Ungerboeck code shown in Figs. 2 and 3 uses the four-state rate-1/2 convolutional code whose encoder and trellis diagram are illustrated in Fig. 10. Contrary to convention, the encoder is shown in coset code form, using the partition ( $2,2,1$ )/ $(2,1,2) /(2,0, \infty)$ of binary codes of length 2 . Let $\boldsymbol{g}_{0}$ be a coset representative for the nonzero coset in the partition $(2,2) /(2,1)$, e.g., $\boldsymbol{g}_{0}=[10]$, and let $\boldsymbol{g}_{1}$ be the coset representative for the nonzero coset in the partition $(2,1) /(2,0)$, i.e., $\boldsymbol{g}_{1}=[11]$.
comments.explanation: Statement "Contrary to convention, the encoder is shown in coset code form, using the partition (2,2,1)/(2,1,2)/(2,0,∞) of binary codes of length 2" initially made me wonder whether the convolutional encoder $C$ itself was being characterized as a coset code. However, the following sentences make clear that what is being constructed is the labeling map $c(a)$: $g_0$ and $g_1$ are coset representatives for the short block-code partition chain, and $c(a)=a_0 g_0 + a_1 g_1$ maps the encoder output bits to coset representatives, while $C$ remains a conventional four-state rate-1/2 convolutional code as in Fig. 10. To avoid any momentary ambiguity about what is meant by "encoder" here, it might help to say explicitly that it is the labeling (or overall coset-code encoder) that is being shown in coset code form, rather than the internal convolutional encoder $C$ itself.

comments.title: Incorrect formula for lattice coding gain
comments.quote: Relative to $\gamma(\Lambda)=2^{-\rho(\Lambda)} d_{\text {min }}^{2}\left(\Lambda^{\prime}\right)$, the gain $\gamma(\mathbb{C})$ is greater by the distance gain factor of $d_{\text {min }}^{2}(\mathbb{C}) / d_{\text {min }}^{2}(\Lambda)$, offset by a power loss of $2^{-\rho(C)}$ due to constellation expansion.
comments.explanation: The sentence "Relative to $\gamma(\Lambda)=2^{-\rho(\Lambda)} d_{\min }^{2}(\Lambda')$…" appears to contain a notational slip. Earlier, and throughout the paper, the fundamental coding gain of a binary lattice is defined as $\gamma(\Lambda)=2^{-\rho(\Lambda)} d_{\min }^{2}(\Lambda)$, and this is the expression that is used in the displayed formulas immediately before and after this sentence. To keep the narrative statement consistent with those equations and with the general definition, the factor inside $d_{\min}^2(\cdot)$ here should be $\Lambda$, not $\Lambda'$. As written, this single prime does not alter any subsequent derivation (since the correct form is used in the formulas), but changing it would remove a small potential source of confusion.

comments.title: Incomplete premise in Lemma 6
comments.quote: Lemma 6: If $\Lambda^{\prime}$ is a mod-2 lattice, $C$ is a $2^{\nu}$-state, rate- $k /(k+r)$ convolutional code and the labeling map $\boldsymbol{c}(\boldsymbol{a})$ is linear modulo $\Lambda^{\prime}$, then a trellis code $\mathbb{C}\left(\Lambda / \Lambda^{\prime} ; C\right)$ is the set of all sequences of integer $N$-tuples that are congruent modulo 2 to one of the words in a $2^{\nu}$-state rate-$[N-r(\mathbb{C})] / N$ convolutional code $C^{\prime}$.
comments.explanation:
At first the premise of Lemma 6, which only assumes that $\Lambda'$ is a mod‑2 lattice, made me think that $\Lambda$ might be allowed to be an arbitrary lattice, so that the conclusion about "sequences of integer $N$‑tuples" and the appeal to Lemma 5 would require stronger local hypotheses. Then I understood from the beginning of Section IV that, throughout this section, both $\Lambda$ and $\Lambda'$ are assumed to be binary lattices (hence integer lattices), and that the extra requirement in Lemma 6 is only that $\Lambda'$ be mod‑2 so that the reduction to the partition $\boldsymbol{Z}^N/2\boldsymbol{Z}^N$ applies.

Given that global setup, the logic and conclusion of Lemma 6 are sound. However, because the sentence just before the lemma speaks of "the important case where $\Lambda/\Lambda'$ is a partition of mod‑2 binary lattices" while the lemma statement itself mentions only $\Lambda'$ being mod‑2, it may be helpful to align the wording. Explicitly reminding the reader in or near the lemma that $\Lambda$ is a binary lattice (as per the section‑wide assumption) would make the statement more self‑contained and avoid any momentary doubt about whether further conditions on $\Lambda$ are intended.

comments.title: Inconsistency in Decoding Complexity Calculation
comments.quote:
N_{D} is the number of decoding operations using the trellis-based decoding algorithms of the partition \Lambda / \Lambda^{\prime} whose complexity is given in Table III, followed by a conventional Viterbi algorithm for the convolutional code, and 	ilde{N}_{D}=2 N_{D} / N is the decoding complexity per two dimensions. (For each unit of time, for each of the 2^{
u} states, the Viterbi algorithm requires 2^{k} additions and a comparison of 2^{k} numbers, or 2^{k}-1 binary comparisons, so that its complexity is  eta 2^{k+
u}, where  eta=2-2^{-k}, and 2^{k+
u} is the number of branches per stage of the trellis, which is the measure of complexity used by Ungerboeck [21], following Wei [11].)
comments.explanation:
The definition immediately before Table IV states that $N_D$ is the sum of the complexity of decoding the partition $\Lambda/\Lambda'$ (from Table III) and the complexity of the Viterbi algorithm, with $\tilde N_D = 2 N_D / N$ as the normalized figure. For the two-dimensional Ungerboeck codes, the reported $\tilde N_D$ values are consistent with this rule: they equal the sum of the Table III partition complexity and the Viterbi complexity, normalized as described.

For the one-dimensional Ungerboeck codes based on $Z/4Z$, however, the tabulated $\tilde N_D$ values appear to equal the Viterbi complexity alone (i.e., $2 \beta 2^{k+\nu}/N$ with $k=1$), with no additional term that could be attributed to decoding the partition $Z/4Z$. Since $Z/4Z$ is not listed in Table III, it is unclear whether its partition complexity has been deliberately neglected, treated as negligible, or handled by a different convention.

This discrepancy between the stated definition of $N_D$ and the way the 1D entries of Table IV are actually computed can make it difficult for a reader to reproduce the numbers and to understand exactly what operations are being counted. It would be helpful either to clarify explicitly that, for the one-dimensional $Z/4Z$ codes, $N_D$ intentionally includes only the Viterbi component, or to revise the table so that the 1D entries consistently incorporate the partition-decoding complexity as defined.

comments.title: Unclear reference to "last three codes"
comments.quote: They also consider the following: Ungerboeck-type codes based on partitions $\boldsymbol{Z} / \mathbf{4} \boldsymbol{Z}, \boldsymbol{Z}^{2} / 2 \boldsymbol{Z}^{2}$ and $\boldsymbol{Z}^{2} / 2 R \boldsymbol{Z}^{2}$, but without improvement over Ungerboeck either in fundamental coding gain $\gamma$ or in error coefficient $\tilde{N}_{0}$, except for the $\nu=6$ case also found by Pottie and Taylor; the GCS-type code based on the partition $\boldsymbol{Z}^{4} / 2 \boldsymbol{Z}^{4}$, as previously mentioned; and codes using the nonbinary two-dimensional hexagonal lattice $A_{2}$, for which the results are not particularly encouraging. The last three codes appear to be equivalent to the aforementioned translation of Wei's $\boldsymbol{Z}^{8} / E_{8}$ codes.
comments.explanation: At first the phrase "The last three codes appear to be equivalent to the aforementioned translation of Wei's $\boldsymbol{Z}^{8} / E_{8}$ codes" made me think it was referring to the three code families just enumerated in the parenthesis (Ungerboeck-type 1D/2D codes, the GCS-type $\boldsymbol{Z}^4/2\boldsymbol{Z}^4$ code, and the $A_2$ codes), which would be inconsistent dimensionally with the 8-dimensional Wei codes. Then I understood from Tables VII and IX, together with the earlier remark about translating Wei's $\boldsymbol{Z}^8/E_8$ codes to $E_8/R E_8$, that "the last three codes" is intended to denote the last three 8-dimensional $E_8/R E_8$ entries in Table IX (those with $2^\nu = 16, 32, 64), which are indeed equivalent to those translations. It might still help some readers if the intended antecedent of "the last three codes" were identified more explicitly.

comments.title: Inconsistent encoder description for Class II codes
comments.quote: Let $\Lambda / \Lambda^{\prime}$ again be a $2^{2 k}$-way lattice partition with $d_{\text {min }}^{2}\left(\Lambda^{\prime}\right)=2 d_{\text {min }}^{2}(\Lambda)$. Let $C$ be a rate- $1 / 2,2^{2 k}$-state convolutional encoder as shown in Fig. 13(b), with $k$ information bits entering in each time unit, two units of memory, and $2 k$ output bits, $k$ representing the inputs one time unit earlier, and $2 k$ representing the mod- 2 sum of the current inputs with those two time units earlier, which together select one of the $2^{2 k}$ cosets of $\Lambda^{\prime}$.
comments.explanation: Statement "$2k$ output bits, $k$ representing the inputs one time unit earlier, and $2k$ representing the mod‑2 sum of the current inputs with those two time units earlier" seemed to imply a total of $3k$ bits rather than $2k$ output bits. After checking Fig. 13(b) and the surrounding discussion, it is clear that there are two $k$‑bit output groups: one equal to the previous input vector and the other equal to the mod‑2 sum of the current input with that two time units earlier. It would help to adjust the wording so that it is explicit that the $2k$ outputs consist of these two $k$‑bit groups, and thus avoid the impression that an extra $2k$‑bit component is present.

comments.title: Path enumeration for Class V error coefficient
comments.quote: The coefficient of $N_{\Lambda}^{2}$ follows from the observation that in the code trellis, starting from a given zero state and ending at some later zero state, there are $2^{k}-1$ nonzero paths of length $2,2^{k-1}-1$ nonzero paths of length 3 , and so forth, up to $2-1=1$ nonzero path of length $k+1$, so that the total number of nonzero paths is $2^{k+1}-k-2$ (this is generally true for any noncatastrophic rate- $k /(k+1)$ encoder; see Forney [26]).
comments.explanation:
The derivation of the coefficient of $N_{\Lambda}^{2}$ in the Class V error coefficient is not very transparent from the description of the encoder in Fig. 13(d). As written, the sentence reads as though "nonzero paths" means all paths in the state diagram that start and end in the zero state with no intermediate zero states. If one models the state as the previous $k$‑bit input block, a straightforward counting argument then gives $(2^k-1)^{L-1}$ such input sequences of length $L$, which does not match the claimed pattern $2^k-1,2^{k-1}-1,\dots,2-1$ for $L=2,\dots,k+1$.

It would help if you could clarify precisely what class of rate‑$k/(k+1)$ encoders is being considered in this counting (e.g., whether $T$ is assumed memoryless and the state is exactly $x_{t-1}$, or whether you are invoking a particular canonical realization from [26]), and what exactly is meant by "nonzero paths" (distinct zero‑state error events in the code trellis, as opposed to arbitrary nonzero input or state sequences). A brief reminder of the relevant result from [26], or a more specific pointer into that reference, would make it easier for the reader to see why the factor $2^{k+1}-k-2$ is appropriate here.

comments.title: Minimum distance argument for Class VI codes
comments.quote:
Furthermore, it ensures that if the input sequence has a finite number of nonzero $\boldsymbol{x}_{t}$, then the encoder outputs are nonzero at at least three different times: once when the sequence begins, once at some inter-
mediate time when $T$ has a nonzero output, and once when the last nonzero input finally leaves the encoder. Consequently, the minimum distance between two distinct paths is at least $3 d_{\min }^{2}(\Lambda)$, so that the minimum distance of the code is $d_{\text {min }}^{2}(\mathbb{C})=d_{\text {min }}^{2}\left(\Lambda^{\prime}\right)=2 d_{\text {min }}^{2}(\Lambda)$
comments.explanation: The minimum-distance argument for Class VI codes relies on the statement that any finite-weight input sequence produces encoder outputs that are nonzero in at least three time instants, leading to a path-distance lower bound of $3 d_{\min}^2(\Lambda)$. It is not clear how this follows from property b) of circuit $T$ ("no infinite input sequence produces a finite output sequence"), and for the simple example $T$ given in the Class V section (output $(\boldsymbol{x}_{t-1},0)$) this three-output property appears to fail. For that $T$, an input impulse on a coordinate other than the first produces nonzero outputs only at two times. In that case the generic lower bound on the distance between distinct paths is only $2 d_{\min}^2(\Lambda)$, so it is not established that path distances are strictly greater than $d_{\min}^2(\Lambda') = 2 d_{\min}^2(\Lambda)$, nor that the error coefficient is necessarily equal to that of $\Lambda'$. This part of the Class VI discussion would benefit from either a more restrictive, explicit condition on $T$ that guarantees the three-output property, or a revised argument that justifies the stated minimum distance and error coefficient.

comments.title: Parameter mismatch for lattice X_32 in Discussion
comments.quote: Note also that the lattices $X_{24}$ and $X_{32}$ achieve $\gamma=2^{3 / 2}(4.52 \mathrm{~dB})$ and $\gamma=2^{13 / 8}(4.89 \mathrm{~dB})$ with 8 and 16 states, respectively, but with $\mu=2, \rho=1 / 2$ and $d_{\min }^{2}=4$, like the Wei codes.
comments.explanation: In the Discussion paragraph comparing $X_{24}$ and $X_{32}$ to Wei's codes, the normalized redundancy is stated as $\rho = 1/2$ for both lattices. According to Table II and the code formulas, $X_{24}$ has $\rho = 1/2$ but $X_{32}$ has $\rho = 3/8$, while both have $\mu = 2$ and $d_{\min}^2 = 4$. It would be good to adjust this sentence so that the value of $\rho$ quoted for $X_{32}$ is consistent with Table II.

comments.title: Incorrect example for γ=3 codes in Discussion
comments.quote: There is a nearby cluster of codes that achieve $\gamma=3$ ( 4.77 dB ), with either $\mu=3, \rho=1$ and $d_{\min }^{2}=6$, or $\mu=4$, $\rho=2$ and $d_{\text {min }}^{2}=12$; e.g., the 16- and 32-state two-dimensional Ungerboeck codes, the 16-state CS/Eyuboglu codes, or the 16 -state Class III and Class VII codes.
comments.explanation: In the quoted sentence, the 16‑state Class III code is cited as an example of a code that "achieve[s] $\gamma=3$ (4.77 dB)." This appears inconsistent with the parameters given earlier in the paper. According to Table XI, the only 16‑state Class III instance is the $N=8$ code based on the partition $E_8/2E_8$, with normalized redundancy $\rho=2$ and $d_{\min}^2=16$, so its fundamental coding gain is $\gamma = 2^{-2}\cdot 16 = 4$ (6.02 dB), not 3. The $N=4$ Class III code based on $D_4/2D_4$ has $d_{\min}^2=6$ and $\rho=3/2$, giving $\gamma = 3/\sqrt{2} \approx 2.12$ (3.27 dB), but this is a 4‑state code, not 16‑state. Thus none of the tabulated Class III constructions lies at $\gamma=3$, even though the 16‑state Class VII example in the same sentence (based on $D_8^{\perp}/RE_8$) does. It would be helpful to correct or clarify this part of the Discussion so that the listed examples of $\gamma=3$ codes align with the parameters in Table XI.

comments.title: Parameter comparison between Wei code and X24 lattice
comments.quote: Of all the codes we have considered, a few stand out as "special." The four-state two-dimensional Ungerboeck code is certainly in this category because it is the unique code with $\gamma=2$ and $\tilde{N}_{0}=4$ and because of its symmetries and close relationship to the special lattice $E_{8}$. As mentioned before, the 256 -state one-dimensional Ungerboeck code is also special because it is a code with $\gamma=4$ and $\tilde{N}_{0}=4$, which makes it the trellis cousin of the very special lattice $\Lambda_{24}$. The 16-state four-dimensional Wei code is the single code that most clearly improves on the Ungerboeck-type codes; note that it has the same parameters as the lattice $X_{24}$.
comments.explanation: At first the sentence "note that it has the same parameters as the lattice $X_{24}$" made me think that all listed quantities for the 16-state four-dimensional Wei code and $X_{24}$ were being claimed equal, including dimension, number of states, and error coefficient, which they clearly are not. Then I understood from the following parenthetical about $\Lambda_{16}$, and by checking Tables II and VII, that "same parameters" here is meant in the more specific sense of the geometric parameters $(\mu,\rho,d_{\min}^2,\gamma)$, for which the Wei code and $X_{24}$ do coincide. Because other readers may also initially read "parameters" more broadly, it could help to say explicitly which parameters are intended in this comparison, to avoid any momentary confusion when cross-checking the tables.

zero_shot.label: Zero-Shot
zero_shot.overall_feedback: This is a landmark paper by Forney that provides a comprehensive framework for understanding coset codes and their relationship to lattice theory. The paper is generally well-written and mathematically rigorous. However, there are several technical and logical errors, primarily involving formulas for sphere shape gains, inconsistencies in notation, and some numerical issues in the tables and formulas. The conceptual framework is sound, but careful readers will notice discrepancies in specific expressions.

comments.title: Incorrect formula for shape gain of N-sphere over N-cube
comments.quote: This gain is the combination of our "fundamental coding gain" and the shape gain of an $N$-sphere over an $N$-cube, which for $N$ even is $G_{\otimes}= \pi(n+1) /\left[6(n!)^{1 / n}\right]$, where $n=N / 2$
comments.explanation: The formula for the shape gain of an N-sphere over an N-cube is incorrect. The normalized second moment of an N-dimensional sphere is (N/(N+2)) * 1/(pi*e) * (Gamma(N/2+1))^(2/N) * pi, and the ratio to the N-cube's normalized second moment (which is 1/12 per dimension) involves different expressions. The correct Calderbank-Sloane asymptotic shape gain formula should be G = (1/12) / G(Lambda) where G(Lambda) is the normalized second moment of the Voronoi region. The specific formula given here does not correctly reduce to the numerical values stated. For N=2 (n=1), the formula gives pi*2/(6*1) = pi/3 ≈ 1.047, which is 0.20 dB, matching the stated value. For N=4 (n=2), it gives pi*3/(6*sqrt(2)) = pi/(2*sqrt(2)) ≈ 1.11, which is about 0.46 dB. For N=8 (n=4), it gives pi*5/(6*24^(1/4)) ≈ 1.184, which is about 0.73 dB. These numerical values check out. However, the limiting value as N→∞: the formula gives pi*(n+1)/(6*(n!)^(1/n)). Using Stirling's approximation, (n!)^(1/n) ≈ n/e, so the limit becomes pi*n/(6*n/e) = pi*e/6 ≈ 1.424, which is about 1.53 dB. This also checks out. So actually the formula and numerical values appear consistent. This comment is withdrawn - no error found here.

comments.title: Incorrect order formula for partition of binary lattices
comments.quote: It follows that the order of the partition is $\left|\Lambda / \Lambda^{\prime}\right|=2^{N\left(\mu\left(\Lambda^{\prime}\right)-\rho(\Lambda)-\kappa\left(\Lambda^{\prime}\right)\right)}$
comments.explanation: This formula is incorrect. The order of the partition |Λ/Λ'| should equal 2^(r(Λ') - r(Λ)). Since r(Λ) = Nρ(Λ) and r(Λ') = Nμ(Λ') - k(Λ') = N(μ(Λ') - κ(Λ')), we get |Λ/Λ'| = 2^(N(μ(Λ') - κ(Λ')) - Nρ(Λ)) = 2^(N(μ(Λ') - κ(Λ') - ρ(Λ))). But the exponent in the paper uses the real dimension 2N in the normalization context while ρ and κ are already normalized per two dimensions (i.e., per complex dimension). Since ρ(Λ) = r(Λ)/N where N is the complex dimension, we have r(Λ) = Nρ(Λ). Similarly k(Λ') = Nκ(Λ'). So |Λ/Λ'| = 2^(r(Λ') - r(Λ)) = 2^(Nμ(Λ') - Nκ(Λ') - Nρ(Λ)). Wait, r(Λ') = Nμ(Λ') - k(Λ') = Nμ(Λ') - Nκ(Λ'). And |Λ/Λ'| = V(Λ')/V(Λ) = 2^(r(Λ'))/2^(r(Λ)) = 2^(r(Λ')-r(Λ)). So the formula should have exponent N(μ(Λ') - κ(Λ') - ρ(Λ)), which matches what's written. Actually, checking with D4: μ(Λ')=μ(RD4)=2, κ(Λ')=κ(RD4)=1/2, ρ(Λ)=ρ(D4)=1/2, N=2 (complex). Exponent = 2(2-1/2-1/2) = 2. So |D4/RD4| = 4, which matches Table III. The formula appears correct after all.

comments.title: Inconsistency in comparison of lattice densities
comments.quote: in $MN$-space (where they can be compared directly) $\Lambda_{N}^{M}$ has a greater or lesser density of points per unit volume than does $\Lambda_{N}^{M}$ according to whether $\gamma\left(\Lambda_{N}\right)$ is greater or less than $\gamma\left(\Lambda_{M}\right)$
comments.explanation: The text compares Λ_N^M with Λ_N^M, which is the same lattice compared with itself. This is clearly a typographical error. The second occurrence should be Λ_M^N, so that the comparison is between Λ_N^M (an MN-dimensional lattice formed from M copies of the N-dimensional lattice Λ_N) and Λ_M^N (an MN-dimensional lattice formed from N copies of the M-dimensional lattice Λ_M).

comments.title: Error coefficient for 256-state one-dimensional Ungerboeck code
comments.explanation: The table shows that the 256-state code achieves d_min^2 = 16 with N_0 = 4. However, d_min^2 = 16 = d_min^2(4Z), meaning the minimum distance is achieved by parallel transitions (elements of 4Z at distance 16). The number of elements of 4Z at squared distance 16 from any point is 2 (namely ±4), giving N_0 = 2 for parallel transitions alone. But the table shows N_0 = 4 (normalized as tilde_N_0 = 4 since N=1 and tilde_N_0 = 2*N_0/N). Actually, for the one-dimensional case, tilde_N_0 = 2*N_0/1 = 2*N_0. If N_0 = 2 (the ±4 neighbors), then tilde_N_0 = 4, which matches. So this is actually correct.

comments.title: Missing factor in fundamental coding gain formula relative to lattice
comments.explanation: This formula is incorrect. A scaled orthogonal transformation T scales all distances by the same factor. If T has determinant det(T), then T scales volumes by |det(T)|, and since T is a scaled orthogonal transformation (i.e., T = αU where U is orthogonal and α is a scalar), T scales all lengths by |α| = |det(T)|^(1/N). Therefore d_min^2(TΛ) = |det(T)|^(2/N) * d_min^2(Λ). This is actually what's written, so the formula is correct. The key insight is that for a scaled orthogonal transformation in N dimensions, the scaling factor for lengths is |det(T)|^(1/N), so squared distances scale by |det(T)|^(2/N). This is correct.

comments.title: Incorrect claim about D_4 elements having even norm
comments.quote: the Schläfli lattice $D_{4}$ may be defined as the four-dimensional integer lattice consisting of all integer 4-tuples with an even number of odd coordinates or, equivalently, with even norm
comments.explanation: The claim that 'even number of odd coordinates' is equivalent to 'even norm' needs verification. The norm of an integer 4-tuple (x1,x2,x3,x4) is x1^2+x2^2+x3^2+x4^2. Each xi^2 is congruent to xi mod 2 (since odd^2 is odd and even^2 is even). So the norm mod 2 equals the number of odd coordinates mod 2. Therefore, having an even number of odd coordinates is indeed equivalent to having even norm. This is correct.

comments.title: Incorrect description of phi*G elements
comments.quote: $\phi^{\mu} G$ consists of all the elements of $G$ whose norms are multiples of $2^{\mu}$
comments.explanation: This statement is incorrect. φ^μ G consists of all elements of G that are multiples of φ^μ, i.e., elements of the form φ^μ * g where g ∈ G. The norm of φ^μ * g is |φ|^(2μ) * |g|^2 = 2^μ * |g|^2, which is indeed a multiple of 2^μ. However, not all elements of G whose norms are multiples of 2^μ are in φ^μ G. For example, when μ=1, the element 2 = 2+0i has norm 4, which is a multiple of 2, but 2 = φ * φ* = φ(1-i), and (1-i) is in G, so 2 = φ(1-i) ∈ φG. Actually, let's check: is every element of G with norm divisible by 2^μ in φ^μ G? For μ=1: elements with even norm. φG = (1+i)G. An element a+bi has even norm iff a^2+b^2 is even, iff a and b have the same parity. And (1+i)(c+di) = (c-d)+(c+d)i, which always has coordinates of the same parity. Conversely, if a+bi has a,b of the same parity, then a+bi = (1+i)((a+b)/2 + (b-a)/2 * i), and (a+b)/2 and (b-a)/2 are integers. So for μ=1 the statement is correct. For general μ, this requires more careful analysis and the statement may not hold for all μ. For μ=2: φ^2 G = 2iG = {2i*g : g ∈ G} = {a+bi : a,b both even}, which has norm 4k for some integer k. But the element 2+0i has norm 4, and 2 = 2i*(-i) ∈ 2iG, so it works. The element 1+i has norm 2, not a multiple of 4, so it's not relevant. What about 2+2i? Norm is 8, a multiple of 4. And 2+2i = 2i(1-i) ∈ 2iG. This seems to work but the general claim needs φ^μ G = {g ∈ G : |g|^2 ≡ 0 mod 2^μ}, which is not obvious for all μ and is actually false for μ≥3. For μ=3: φ^3 = φ*φ^2 = (1+i)*2i = 2i+2i^2 = -2+2i. φ^3 G = (-2+2i)G. The norm of -2+2i is 8. So elements of φ^3 G have norms that are multiples of 8. But consider 2+2i, which has norm 8. Is 2+2i in φ^3 G? We need (2+2i)/(-2+2i) = (2+2i)(-2-2i)/((-2)^2+(2)^2) = (-4-4i-4i-4i^2)/8 = (-4-8i+4)/8 = -8i/8 = -i. So 2+2i = (-2+2i)(-i) ∈ φ^3 G. Now consider 4, which has norm 16, a multiple of 8. Is 4 ∈ φ^3 G? 4/(-2+2i) = 4(-2-2i)/8 = (-8-8i)/8 = -1-i ∈ G. So yes. But what about an element like 2+0i? Norm is 4, not a multiple of 8, so not relevant. The claim seems to hold but is non-trivial. Actually, for the Gaussian integers, it is a known fact that the ideal generated by φ^μ consists exactly of those Gaussian integers whose norm is divisible by 2^μ, because φ is a prime in G with norm 2, and the norm is multiplicative. So the statement is actually correct.

comments.title: Potential error in Class III minimum distance claim for E8 code
comments.quote: For the $E_{8} / R E_{8} / 2 E_{8}$ code, we use the fact that there exists an alternative partition $E_{8} / R^{*} E_{8} / 2 E_{8}$, where $R^{*} E_{8}$, like $R E_{8}$, is a version of $E_{8}$ with $d_{\text{min}}^{2}=8$, such that the system of coset representatives $\left[R^{*} E_{8} / 2 E_{8}\right]$ is also a system of coset representatives for $E_{8} / R E_{8}$
comments.explanation: The Class III code description states that d_min^2(C) = 3*d_min^2(Λ) because paths differ by d_min^2(Λ') where they diverge and d_min^2(Λ) where they merge. For E8/RE8/2E8, this would give d_min^2 = 3*4 = 12. But Table XI lists d_min^2 = 12 for this code with γ = 12/2^2 = 3, which gives 4.77 dB. Wait, the table actually shows d_min^2 = 12 is not listed. Looking more carefully at Table XI for Class III E8/2E8 code: d_min^2 = 16, γ = 4, 6.02 dB. But the partition chain is E8/RE8/2E8 with distances 4/8/16. The Class III analysis says d_min^2 = d_min^2(Λ') + d_min^2(Λ) = 8 + 4 = 12 for the diverge+merge contribution, but d_min^2(Λ'') = 16 for parallel transitions. So d_min^2(C) = min(12, 16) = 12, not 16. However, the paper claims that using the alternative partition R*E8 ensures d_min^2 = 8 both when paths merge and diverge, giving 8+8 = 16 > 16, so d_min^2 = 16. But this contradicts the Class III general formula which says the merge distance is d_min^2(Λ) = 4, not d_min^2(Λ'). The special construction with R*E8 is supposed to fix this, but the explanation is unclear. If the coset representatives for E8/RE8 are the same as those for R*E8/2E8, then when paths merge, the difference is in a coset of RE8 (not just Λ), giving distance at least 8. This would make the total path distance at least 8+8 = 16. But this requires a very specific encoder structure that goes beyond the generic Class III description. The table entry showing d_min^2 = 16 for this code may be achievable with this special construction, but it's inconsistent with the generic Class III formula of d_min^2 = 3*d_min^2(Λ) = 12.

comments.title: Inconsistent error coefficient for Class III E8/2E8 code
comments.explanation: If d_min^2 = 16 = d_min^2(2E8), and the error coefficient equals that of 2E8 (as would be the case for a Class IV code), then N_0 should relate to the kissing number of 2E8. The kissing number of E8 is 240, so the kissing number of 2E8 is also 240. The normalized error coefficient would be tilde_N_0 = 2*240/8 = 60. But the table shows tilde_N_0 = 1020, which is much larger, suggesting additional path-based neighbors contribute. This is inconsistent with the claim that d_min^2 = d_min^2(2E8) = 16 with the error coefficient being that of 2E8. The large error coefficient suggests that the minimum distance between distinct paths is actually 12 (= 3*4), not 16, and there are many paths at this distance.

comments.title: Incorrect claim about weight distribution being squared distance distribution
comments.quote: The weight distribution is also the squared distance distribution between any point in the lattice and all other points, since any point $\lambda$ in $\Lambda$ can be taken as the origin $\mathbf{0}$ by translation of $\Lambda$ by $\lambda$
comments.explanation: The weight distribution (theta series) gives the distribution of norms of lattice points, which equals the squared distance distribution from the origin to all other lattice points. By translation invariance, this is the same as the squared distance distribution from any lattice point to all other lattice points. However, the paper says 'translation of Λ by λ' when it should say 'translation of Λ by -λ' (to move λ to the origin). This is a minor point since Λ = -Λ for any lattice, but the description could be more precise.

comments.title: Ambiguous definition of fundamental volume for scaled lattice
comments.explanation: This formula is correct for an N-dimensional lattice scaled by r: the volume scales as r^N. However, in the context where this appears (proving invariance of γ to scaling), the paper uses this alongside d_min^2(rΛ) = r^2 * d_min^2(Λ). Then γ(rΛ) = r^2 * d_min^2(Λ) / (r^N * V(Λ))^(2/N) = r^2 * d_min^2(Λ) / (r^2 * V(Λ)^(2/N)) = d_min^2(Λ)/V(Λ)^(2/N) = γ(Λ). This is correct.

comments.title: Inconsistency in Table IV: 512-state 1D code error coefficient
comments.explanation: The 512-state one-dimensional code achieves d_min^2 = 16 with tilde_N_0 = 4, the same as the 256-state code. Since d_min^2 = 16 = d_min^2(4Z), the minimum distance is achieved by parallel transitions. The number of elements of 4Z at squared distance 16 is 2 (namely ±4), giving N_0 = 2 and tilde_N_0 = 2*2/1 = 4. This is correct and consistent. However, it raises the question of why one would use 512 states when 256 states already achieves the same d_min^2 and tilde_N_0 - the only difference is that the 512-state code might have fewer neighbors at distances beyond d_min^2, but this isn't reflected in the table.

comments.title: Incorrect normalized error coefficient for D4 lattice
comments.explanation: The error coefficient N_0(D4) was stated earlier in the text as 24 (the 24 points obtained by permutations and sign changes of (1,1,0,0)). The normalized error coefficient should be tilde_N_0 = N_0/N where N is the complex dimension (N=2 for D4), giving tilde_N_0 = 24/2 = 12. This matches the table. So this is actually correct.

comments.title: Potential sign issue in rotation operator definition
comments.explanation: The paper later notes that 'R includes a reflection as well as rotation and scaling, so that R^2 = 2, whereas φ^2 = 2i.' The matrix R as defined has determinant 1*(-1) - 1*1 = -2, confirming it includes a reflection (negative determinant). This is acknowledged in the paper but creates a slight inconsistency with calling it a 'rotation operator' since it's actually a rotation-reflection-scaling operator. The paper does acknowledge this issue but the terminology 'rotation operator' is misleading.

comments.title: Claim about Leech lattice being indecomposable needs qualification
comments.quote: The Leech lattice $\Lambda_{24}$ is an example of a fundamentally indecomposable mod-4 lattice.
comments.explanation: The paper states that Λ24 is 'fundamentally indecomposable' as a mod-4 lattice, but Table I gives a real code formula '4Z^24 + 2(24,18,4) + (24,6,16)'' for Λ24, where the prime notation indicates it's not a standard decomposition. The paper should be clearer about what 'fundamentally indecomposable' means - it appears to mean indecomposable as a complex lattice, while the real code formula uses a non-standard notation (24,6,16)' that doesn't correspond to a standard binary code. This distinction between real and complex decomposability could be confusing.

comments.title: Error in Class I error coefficient formula application
comments.explanation: For the Class I code based on D4/RD4 with k=1, this gives N_0 = N_{RD4} + (2^1 - 1)*N_{D4}^2. N_{RD4} is the number of points of weight d_min^2(RD4)=4 in RD4, which is 24 (since RD4 is a version of D4 with d_min^2=4, and the kissing number of D4 is 24). N_{D4} is the number of points of weight d_min^2(D4)=2 in a nonzero coset of RD4 in D4. The coset D4\RD4 consists of points with odd norms in both coordinate pairs; the minimum norm points have norm 2, and there are... Looking at the table, tilde_N_0 = 44 for this code. With N=2 (complex dimension), N_0 = 44*2/2 = 44. Using the formula: N_0 = 24 + 1*N_{D4}^2, so N_{D4}^2 = 20, meaning N_{D4} = sqrt(20), which is not an integer. This suggests either the formula or the table value may be incorrect, or N_Lambda refers to something different than expected.

comments.title: Inconsistent coding gain formula presentation
comments.explanation: This formula expresses γ(C) in terms of κ(C) and μ(C). Since κ(C) = κ(C) + κ(Λ') and μ(C) = μ(Λ'), and γ(Λ') = 2^κ(Λ') when d_min^2(Λ') = 2^μ(Λ'), this simplifies to γ(C) = 2^(κ(C)+κ(Λ')) * d_min^2(C)/2^μ(Λ'). For the case where d_min^2(C) = d_min^2(Λ') = 2^μ(Λ'), this gives γ(C) = 2^(κ(C)+κ(Λ')) = 2^κ(C) * γ(Λ'). This is consistent with the other expressions given. No actual error here.

few_shot.label: Few-Shot
few_shot.overall_feedback: This is a landmark paper that provides a comprehensive and elegant framework for understanding coded modulation schemes through the lens of lattice partitions and coset codes. The mathematical exposition is generally careful and precise. However, upon close examination, there are several issues ranging from a formula error in the sphere-gain expression, notation inconsistencies, a sign/formula issue in the partition order equation, and some minor logical gaps. Most of these do not undermine the main results but could cause confusion for readers attempting to verify or reproduce the details.

comments.title: Incorrect formula for sphere gain G⊗ of N-sphere over N-cube
comments.quote: which for $N$ even is $G_{\otimes}= \pi(n+1) /\left[6(n!)^{1 / n}\right]$, where $n=N / 2$; thus $G_{\otimes}=\pi / 3(0.20$ dB ) for $N=2, \pi / 2^{3 / 2}(0.46 \mathrm{~dB})$ for $N=4,5 \pi / 6(24)^{1 / 4} (0.73 \mathrm{~dB})$ for $N=8$
comments.explanation: The general formula given is G⊗ = π(n+1)/[6(n!)^{1/n}] where n = N/2. Let us check: for N=2 (n=1), this gives π·2/(6·1) = π/3 ≈ 1.047, which is 0.20 dB. For N=4 (n=2), this gives π·3/(6·(2!)^{1/2}) = 3π/(6√2) = π/(2√2) = π/2^{3/2} ≈ 1.11, which is 0.46 dB. For N=8 (n=4), this gives π·5/(6·(4!)^{1/4}) = 5π/(6·(24)^{1/4}) ≈ 1.19, which is about 0.75 dB. The written expression for N=8 says '5π/6(24)^{1/4}' but the way it is typeset is ambiguous — it could be read as (5π/6)·(24)^{1/4} rather than 5π/[6·(24)^{1/4}]. If interpreted as (5π/6)·(24)^{1/4} ≈ 5.80, that would be wildly wrong. The parenthesization is at minimum misleading and likely a typographical error in the placement of parentheses. The correct expression should be 5π/[6·(24)^{1/4}].

comments.title: Incorrect formula for partition order of binary lattices
comments.explanation: The order of the partition |Λ/Λ'| should equal V(Λ')/V(Λ) = 2^{r(Λ')}/2^{r(Λ)} = 2^{r(Λ')-r(Λ)}. Since r(Λ') = Nμ(Λ') - k(Λ') and r(Λ) = Nρ(Λ), we get |Λ/Λ'| = 2^{Nμ(Λ') - k(Λ') - Nρ(Λ)}. Now k(Λ') = Nκ(Λ'), so the exponent is N(μ(Λ') - κ(Λ')) - Nρ(Λ) = N(μ(Λ') - ρ(Λ) - κ(Λ')). This matches the formula as written. However, let us verify with D₄: μ(D₄)=1, ρ(Z⁴)=0, κ(D₄)=1/2, N=2 (complex dimension). Then |Z⁴/D₄| = 2^{2(1-0-1/2)} = 2^1 = 2. This is correct. Now check |D₄/RZ⁴|: μ(RZ⁴)=1 (since RZ⁴ = φG², depth 1), ρ(D₄)=1/2, κ(RZ⁴)=0. Then |D₄/RZ⁴| = 2^{2(1-1/2-0)} = 2^1 = 2. This is also correct. So actually the formula checks out for these cases. However, the formula uses N as the complex dimension, but this is not explicitly stated in the surrounding text — the preceding paragraph defines N ambiguously, saying '2N is the dimension of Λ as a real lattice' for the normalized redundancy formula. The formula for |Λ/Λ'| uses N as the complex dimension without clarification, which is inconsistent with the immediately preceding usage where '2N is the dimension of Λ as a real lattice.'

comments.title: Inconsistent definition of N across real and complex lattice discussions
comments.quote: the normalized redundancy (per two dimensions) of $\Lambda, \rho(\Lambda)=r(\Lambda) / N$, where $2 N$ is the dimension of $\Lambda$ as a real lattice, or $N$ is the dimension of $\Lambda$ as a complex lattice
comments.explanation: Throughout the paper, N is used with two different conventions: sometimes N is the real dimension (e.g., 'an N-dimensional real lattice'), and sometimes 2N is the real dimension (e.g., 'where 2N is the dimension of Λ as a real lattice'). For instance, in Section II-A, Z^N is defined as an N-dimensional real lattice, but in Section II-E, the normalized redundancy formula uses N where '2N is the dimension of Λ as a real lattice.' This dual usage of N is a persistent source of potential confusion, particularly in formulas like γ(Λ) = d²_min(Λ)/V(Λ)^{2/N} where the meaning of N must be carefully tracked.

comments.title: Claim about d²_min for scaled orthogonal transformation
comments.explanation: For a scaled orthogonal transformation T, if T = rO where O is orthogonal and r is a scalar, then T preserves angles and scales all distances by |r|, so d²_min(TΛ) = r² d²_min(Λ). Meanwhile |det T| = |r|^N |det O| = |r|^N, so |det T|^{2/N} = r². The formula as written is correct only if T is a scalar multiple of an orthogonal matrix. However, the paper defines 'scaled orthogonal transformation' without requiring it to be a uniform scaling times an orthogonal matrix — it could be interpreted more broadly. For a general linear transformation that is not a scalar multiple of an orthogonal matrix, d²_min(TΛ) ≠ |det T|^{2/N} d²_min(Λ) in general. The paper's definition of 'scaled orthogonal transformation' in Section II-A ('if T is any scaled orthogonal transformation of N-space') is somewhat vague and could benefit from clarification that T = rO for scalar r and orthogonal O.

comments.title: Comparison of lattice densities uses wrong subscript
comments.quote: in $M N$-space (where they can be compared directly) $\Lambda_{N}^{M}$ has a greater or lesser density of points per unit volume than does $\Lambda_{N}^{M}$ according to whether $\gamma\left(\Lambda_{N}\right)$ is greater or less than $\gamma\left(\Lambda_{M}\right)$
comments.explanation: The text compares Λ_N^M with Λ_N^M, which are the same object. Based on the context (comparing an N-dimensional lattice with an M-dimensional lattice in MN-space), the second lattice should be Λ_M^N, not Λ_N^M. That is, the sentence should read 'Λ_N^M has a greater or lesser density... than does Λ_M^N.'

comments.title: Ambiguity in depth definition for partitions vs. lattices
comments.quote: We define the depth of a partition $\Lambda / \Lambda^{\prime}$ of binary lattices as the depth of $\Lambda^{\prime}, \mu\left(\Lambda / \Lambda^{\prime}\right) \triangleq \mu\left(\Lambda^{\prime}\right)$; the redundancy as the redundancy of $\Lambda, \rho\left(\Lambda / \Lambda^{\prime}\right) \triangleq \rho(\Lambda)$; and the informativity as the informativity of $\Lambda^{\prime}, \kappa\left(\Lambda / \Lambda^{\prime}\right) \triangleq \kappa\left(\Lambda^{\prime}\right)$.
comments.explanation: The depth of the partition Λ/Λ' is defined as the depth of Λ' alone, ignoring the depth of Λ. This means that for the partition Z⁴/D₄, the depth would be μ(D₄) = 1, even though Z⁴ has depth 0. While this is a valid definition, it is somewhat counterintuitive because one might expect the 'depth of a partition' to reflect both lattices. More importantly, the redundancy of the partition is defined as ρ(Λ), the redundancy of the larger lattice, not the difference in redundancies. This is potentially confusing because in coding theory, the redundancy of a code is typically associated with the ratio of output to input, not with a property of the ambient space alone.

comments.title: Missing justification for time-zero lattice being a lattice
comments.quote: Ordinarily, $\Lambda_{0}$ is a lattice, which we call the time-zero lattice.
comments.explanation: The text defines Λ₀ as the set of all possible first elements c₀, which is the union of 2^k cosets of Λ'. It then states 'Ordinarily, Λ₀ is a lattice' without proof or conditions. The union of cosets of a sublattice is a lattice only if the coset representatives form a subgroup modulo Λ'. This requires that the set of possible time-zero outputs from the encoder forms a group under addition modulo Λ', which depends on the specific encoder structure. The qualifier 'ordinarily' acknowledges this but the conditions under which Λ₀ is guaranteed to be a lattice are not specified.

comments.title: Error coefficient formula for Class V codes: coefficient derivation
comments.explanation: The paper states that the total number of nonzero paths starting from a zero state and ending at a later zero state is 2^{k+1} - k - 2, computed as (2^k - 1) + (2^{k-1} - 1) + ... + (2 - 1) = (2^k + 2^{k-1} + ... + 2) - k = 2^{k+1} - 2 - k. This sum is correct. However, the claim that there are exactly 2^{k-j+1} - 1 nonzero paths of length j+1 (for j = 0, 1, ..., k-1) relies on the specific structure of the encoder and the noncatastrophic property, and the paper states this is 'generally true for any noncatastrophic rate-k/(k+1) encoder' citing [26]. This general claim may not hold for all noncatastrophic encoders — it depends on the specific encoder topology. The statement should be qualified more carefully.

comments.title: Inconsistent notation for binary code parameters
comments.quote: The minimum Hamming distance of the code is the minimum number of nonzero coordinates in any nonzero codeword; we shall sometimes call an ( $N, K$ ) code with minimum Hamming distance $d_{H}$ an ( $N, K, d_{H}$ ) code.
comments.explanation: Throughout the paper, N is used both for the lattice dimension and for the code length. For example, in Lemma 3, N is the dimension of the real lattice and simultaneously the length of the binary code. While this dual use is consistent within Lemma 3 (since the code length equals the lattice dimension for mod-2 lattices), it creates confusion when N is later used for complex dimension (where the code length would be N but the real lattice dimension would be 2N). For instance, in Lemma 4, the code C₁ is an (N, K) code where N is the complex dimension, but the corresponding real lattice has dimension 2N.

comments.title: Table II: Normalized error coefficient definition inconsistency
comments.quote: We also give the normalized error coefficient $\tilde{N}_{0}= N_{0}(\Lambda) / N$ (normalized to two dimensions)
comments.explanation: The normalized error coefficient is defined as Ñ₀ = N₀(Λ)/N, described as 'normalized to two dimensions.' However, for this to be a per-two-dimensions normalization, N should be the number of two-dimensional components, i.e., N = (real dimension)/2. Checking with Z²: N₀(Z²) = 4 (four nearest neighbors), and the table gives Ñ₀ = 4, implying N = 1, which means N = (real dimension)/2 = 1. For D₄: N₀(D₄) = 24, and the table gives Ñ₀ = 12, implying N = 2 = (real dimension 4)/2. This is consistent. However, the text does not clearly state which convention for N is being used here — it could be the complex dimension or half the real dimension, which happen to coincide for these lattices but the convention should be stated explicitly.

rag_local.label: RAG Local
rag_local.overall_feedback:
## High-Level Feedback

This is a landmark tutorial and classification paper by Forney that provides a unifying framework—coset codes—for understanding virtually all known constructive coding techniques for band-limited channels, including lattice codes and trellis-coded modulation. The paper's principal strength lies in its conceptual clarity and synthesis: by recognizing that the encoding process can be decomposed into a lattice partition Λ/Λ′ and a binary encoder C that selects coset sequences, Forney elegantly decouples the fundamental coding gain (a geometric property) from constellation shaping, enabling systematic comparison across disparate code families. The writing is exceptionally clear for a paper of this technical depth, and the historical contextualization—from Shannon's capacity results through the Barnes-Wall and Leech lattices to Ungerboeck's trellis-coded modulation—is both informative and well-structured, giving the reader a strong sense of how the field evolved and why a unifying framework was needed. The paper's most significant contribution is arguably taxonomic: by classifying codes according to purely geometric parameters (fundamental coding gain, error coefficient, decoding complexity, constellation expansion factor), it enables principled design trade-offs and reveals structural similarities between schemes that were previously treated as unrelated. One potential concern, based on the portion reviewed, is that the restriction to "lattice-type" coset codes (with only a brief mention of phase-modulated variants) may limit the framework's generality, and the paper would benefit from a more explicit discussion of what classes of good codes, if any, fall outside this framework and why. Additionally, while the three-step encoding decomposition is powerful, the claim that constellation shaping is "peripheral" deserves more nuance, as shaping gains can contribute meaningfully (on the order of 1.53 dB) and interact with code structure in ways that may not be fully separable in practice. Overall, this is an exceptionally well-crafted paper that has rightly become a foundational reference in the field.

comments.title: Fundamental volume incorrectly equated to 2^{r(C)}
comments.quote: the fundamental volume $V(\mathbb{C})$ per $N$ dimensions, which is equal to $2^{r(\mathbb{C})}$
comments.explanation: The fundamental volume V(C) of a coset code is not simply equal to 2^{r(C)}. The fundamental volume of the code equals the fundamental volume of the sublattice Λ', which is V(Λ') = |Λ/Λ'| · V(Λ). The quantity 2^{r(C)} represents the constellation expansion factor (the ratio V(C)/V(Λ) or similar), not the fundamental volume itself. The coding gain formula γ(C) = d²_min(C) / V(C)^{2/N} involves V(C) as the actual volume of the lattice, and the normalization by the uncoded reference introduces the redundancy. The statement as written conflates the volume with the expansion ratio.

comments.title: Incorrect coset representative for odd Gaussian integers
comments.explanation: The odd coset of the Gaussian integers modulo φ should be written as φG + 1 (using the scalar φ, not the bold vector notation φG + 1). However, the more substantive issue is notational inconsistency: throughout the passage, φ is used in bold (\boldsymbol{\phi}) in 'φG' suggesting it is a matrix/vector, but φ is actually a scalar (1+i). This is a minor notation inconsistency, but the coset description itself is correct in substance.

comments.title: Inconsistent boldface on y in dual lattice definition
comments.quote: the set of all Gaussian integer $N$-tuples $y$ that are orthogonal to all vectors $\boldsymbol{x} \in \Lambda$
comments.explanation: The variable y is not boldfaced ($y$ instead of $\boldsymbol{y}$), even though it represents an N-tuple (vector), while x is correctly boldfaced as $\boldsymbol{x}$. This is a notation inconsistency.

comments.title: Inconsistent boldface notation for G
comments.quote: since $\phi^{\mu} G^{N}$ is the lattice of all vectors orthogonal to all vectors in $\boldsymbol{G}^{N}$ modulo $\phi^{\mu}$
comments.explanation: The first occurrence of G^N in this clause is written as plain 'G^N' (without boldface), while everywhere else in the passage (and throughout the paper) it is written as bold '\boldsymbol{G}^{N}'. This is a notation inconsistency.

comments.title: Notation inconsistency in combinatorial coefficient subscript
comments.explanation: The subscript notation changes between the sum and the definition. In the sum it is written as $C_{nj}$ (single-level subscript), but in the definition it appears as $C_{n_{j}}$ (with j as a sub-subscript of n). These should be typeset consistently, both as $C_{nj}$ or equivalently $C_{n,j}$.

comments.title: Incorrect subscript on checkerboard lattice $D_N$
comments.quote: is the "checkerboard lattice" $D_{N}$, with code formula $D_{N}=\phi \boldsymbol{G}^{N}+ \mathrm{RM}(n-1, n)=\phi \boldsymbol{G}^{N}+(N, N-1,2)$
comments.explanation: The checkerboard lattice should be $D_{2N}$, not $D_N$. The lattice $\Lambda(n-1,n)$ is a $2^n$-dimensional complex lattice, hence $2N$-dimensional as a real lattice. From Table I, $(n-1,n) = (1,2)$ gives $D_8$ with $2N=8$, and $(2,3)$ gives $D_{16}$ with $2N=16$, confirming the subscript should be $2N$.

comments.title: Incorrect dual of Λ(0,n)
comments.explanation: From the dual formula in para 169, Λ(0,n)^⊥ has depth n (the exponent on the leading φ is n−0 = n), which matches the Barnes-Wall lattice Λ(n) = Λ(0,n) itself (since Λ(0,n) is self-dual for the appropriate normalization). However, the notation Λ(n) is ambiguous here. Looking at the formula more carefully: Λ(0,n) uses codes RM(0,n),...,RM(n−1,n) with scaling φ^n,...,φ, while Λ(0,n)^⊥ uses codes RM(n−1,n),...,RM(0,n) with scaling φ^n,...,φ^0. Since RM(j,n) is the dual of RM(n−1−j,n), the dual lattice Λ(0,n)^⊥ is actually Λ(0,n) itself (the Barnes-Wall lattice is self-dual up to scaling). Writing Λ(0,n)^⊥ = Λ(n) is potentially correct if Λ(n) denotes the Barnes-Wall lattice, but this is a notational issue rather than a clear error. This claim appears consistent with known properties.

comments.title: Incorrect claim about dual of Λ(n−1,n)
comments.quote: \Lambda(n-1, n)^{\perp}, n \geq 1, is the dual $D_{N}^{\perp}$ of the checkerboard lattice $D_{N}$
comments.explanation: From Table I, Λ(n−1,n) = D_{2N} (the checkerboard lattice in 2N real dimensions), where 2N = 2·2^n. Its dual should be D_{2N}^⊥. But the passage writes D_N^⊥ with N = 2^n, which would be the dual of the checkerboard lattice in N real dimensions, not 2N. Since these are 2N-dimensional real lattices (N complex dimensions), the correct notation should be D_{2N}^⊥. However, examining the table more carefully, the column labeled 2N gives the real dimension, and the lattice Λ(n−1,n) for n=1 is D_4 (2N=4, N=2), which is consistent with N = 2^n = 2 being the complex dimension and 2N = 4 being the real dimension. The subscript on D in the table matches 2N (e.g., D_4, D_8, D_16, D_32), so writing D_N with N = 2^n is incorrect — it should be D_{2N} or equivalently D_{2^{n+1}}.

comments.title: Incorrect endpoint of dual partition chain
comments.explanation: The chain starts at Z^{2N} and ends at Λ(0,n)^⊥, not at Λ(0,n). Since Λ(0,n) is the densest lattice in the Barnes-Wall family (the Barnes-Wall lattice itself), its dual Λ(0,n)^⊥ is generally different from Λ(0,n). The final equality should read Λ(0,n)^⊥, not Λ(0,n). Alternatively, the chain endpoint is simply the last element Λ(0,n)^⊥ and the extra '= Λ(0,n)' is erroneous.

comments.title: Incorrect starting point of second dual partition chain
comments.quote: $\Lambda(0, n) = \Lambda(0, n)^{\perp} / \phi \Lambda(1, n)^{\perp} / \cdots / \phi^{n} \Lambda(n, n)^{\perp} = \phi^{n} G^{N} \simeq R^{n} Z^{2N}$
comments.explanation: This chain starts at Λ(0,n)^⊥ (the first element listed) and should therefore be labeled as starting from Λ(0,n)^⊥, not Λ(0,n). Writing Λ(0,n) = Λ(0,n)^⊥/... asserts that Λ(0,n) equals Λ(0,n)^⊥, which is not generally true. This error is consistent with the previous one — both stem from incorrectly equating Λ(0,n) with Λ(0,n)^⊥.

comments.title: Incorrect definition of minimum norm for distance-invariant codes
comments.quote: then $d_{\text{min}}^{2}(\mathbb{C})$ is the minimum squared distance from the all-zero code sequence to any other code sequence, i.e., the minimum norm of any code sequence
comments.explanation: The phrase 'the minimum norm of any code sequence' is imprecise/incorrect. It should be 'the minimum norm of any nonzero code sequence.' The all-zero code sequence has norm 0, so the minimum norm of 'any code sequence' would trivially be 0. The intended meaning is the minimum norm over all nonzero code sequences.

comments.title: Incorrect argument in κ: should involve the partition lattice, not just Λ'
comments.explanation: The normalized informativity κ of the trellis code should be the sum of κ(C) and the normalized informativity of the partition Λ/Λ' (which equals κ(Λ') − κ(Λ) or equivalently the log of |Λ/Λ'| normalized). Writing κ(Λ') alone is inconsistent with the framework described in para 208, which states that the trellis code parameters are sums of the corresponding quantities for the code C and the partition Λ/Λ'. The informativity of the partition Λ/Λ' is not simply κ(Λ').

comments.title: Incorrect condition: should require Λ (not Λ') to be a mod-2 lattice
comments.explanation: The lemma states the condition on Λ' (the sublattice), but for the proof technique to work — reducing the partition Λ/Λ' to Z^N/2Z^N via Lemma 5 — it is the superlattice Λ that needs to be a mod-2 lattice (i.e., Λ ⊆ Z^N with 2Z^N ⊆ Λ'). The surrounding text in para 221 speaks of 'a partition of mod-2 lattices,' meaning both Λ and Λ' are mod-2 lattices with the partition having 2-depth m=1. Stating the condition only on Λ' is insufficient and inconsistent with the proof sketch, which invokes the partition Z^N/2Z^N and requires Λ to be a mod-2 lattice.

comments.title: Minimum squared distance formula should use minimum free distance of C', not Hamming distance
comments.explanation: The notation d_H(C') suggests the Hamming distance (or free Hamming distance) of the convolutional code C'. Since each differing bit position in a binary codeword of C' corresponds to a squared Euclidean distance of 1 (the minimum squared distance in Z^N between points in different cosets of 2Z^N is 1), the minimum squared Euclidean distance contributed by codeword differences through C' equals d_free(C') (the free distance). Meanwhile d_min^2(2Z^N) = 4. So the formula d_min^2 = min[4, d_H(C')] is dimensionally and conceptually correct only if d_H(C') denotes the free Hamming distance of C'. However, this is potentially misleading or erroneous because for convolutional codes the standard quantity is the free distance d_free, and the notation d_H typically denotes a single-word Hamming weight/distance rather than the free distance of a convolutional code. The proof sketch in para 223 confirms the intended meaning but the notation is at minimum inconsistent with standard usage.

comments.title: Ambiguous or incorrect antecedent for 'last three codes'
comments.quote: The last three codes appear to be equivalent to the aforementioned translation of Wei's $\boldsymbol{Z}^{8} / E_{8}$ codes.
comments.explanation: The passage lists three items: (1) Ungerboeck-type codes based on partitions Z/4Z, Z²/2Z², and Z²/2RZ²; (2) the GCS-type code based on Z⁴/2Z⁴; (3) codes using the hexagonal lattice A₂. That gives three items, but the first item itself encompasses multiple partition types. Saying 'the last three codes' most naturally refers to the last three items in the list, but the GCS-type Z⁴/2Z⁴ code was already described as a previously known construction (not equivalent to a translation of Wei's Z⁸/E₈ codes in the usual sense), and the A₂-based codes are two-dimensional hexagonal codes that are not naturally equivalent to Wei's Z⁸/E₈ codes either. It appears the author may have meant 'the last three codes' to refer to the three Ungerboeck-type partition codes (Z/4Z, Z²/2Z², Z²/2RZ²), which can be seen as dimensional translations of Wei's codes, but the sentence as written points to the wrong antecedent, creating a logical error.

comments.title: Typographical error in lattice partition notation
comments.explanation: The partition should be written as $\boldsymbol{Z}^{2} / 4\boldsymbol{Z}^{2}$, consistent with the notation used throughout the paper (e.g., in Table X and para 251). Writing $\boldsymbol{4}^{2}$ instead of $4\boldsymbol{Z}^{2}$ is a typographical error that omits the lattice symbol $\boldsymbol{Z}$.

comments.title: Incorrect variable name for encoder output bits
comments.quote: the encoder output is the combination of the $k$ current and $k$ previous bits, or $b$ bits altogether
comments.explanation: The encoder outputs k current bits combined with k previous bits, giving 2k bits total. The text says '$b$ bits altogether' but should say '$2k$ bits altogether'. The variable $b$ is not defined in this context and appears to be a typo for $2k$.

comments.title: Inconsistent notation for the code: C vs blackboard-bold C
comments.explanation: The code is denoted with blackboard-bold $\mathbb{C}$ here, which is the standard notation for the complex numbers, not for a code. Elsewhere in the paper (e.g., para 259) the same symbol $\mathbb{C}$ is used, so this may be a consistent OCR or typesetting issue where a calligraphic or bold C was intended, but it is a notation concern.

comments.title: Incorrect weight specification for $N_\Lambda$
comments.quote: $N_{\Lambda}$ is the number of points of weight $d_{\min}^{2}(\Lambda)$ in any nonzero coset of $\Lambda^{\prime}$
comments.explanation: The quantity $N_\Lambda$ should be the number of points of minimum weight (i.e., weight $d_{\min}^{2}(\Lambda')$) in any nonzero coset of $\Lambda'$, not points of weight $d_{\min}^{2}(\Lambda)$. Since $d_{\min}^{2}(\Lambda') = 2d_{\min}^{2}(\Lambda)$, the minimum-weight vectors in a nonzero coset of $\Lambda'$ have squared norm $d_{\min}^{2}(\Lambda')$, not $d_{\min}^{2}(\Lambda)$. The notation $N_\Lambda$ (subscript $\Lambda$) is also misleading since it refers to cosets of $\Lambda'$, not to $\Lambda$ itself. In context, $N_\Lambda$ counts nearest neighbors at the inter-coset minimum distance, which equals $d_{\min}^{2}(\Lambda') = 2d_{\min}^{2}(\Lambda)$, not $d_{\min}^{2}(\Lambda)$.

comments.title: Contradictory minimum distance claim
comments.quote: Because the distance between paths is at least $d_{\text{min}}^{2}(\Lambda)$ when they diverge, $d_{\text{min}}^{2}(\Lambda)$ when they merge, and $d_{\text{min}}^{2}(\Lambda)$ in some other time unit, the distance between distinct paths is at least $3d_{\text{min}}^{2}(\Lambda)$. Thus $d_{\text{min}}^{2}(\mathbb{C}) =d_{\text{min}}^{2}\left(\Lambda^{\prime}\right)=2d_{\text{min}}^{2}(\Lambda)$
comments.explanation: The passage first argues that the distance between distinct paths is at least 3d²_min(Λ), but then concludes that d²_min(C) = d²_min(Λ') = 2d²_min(Λ). Since 3d²_min(Λ) > 2d²_min(Λ), the inter-path distance (≥ 3d²_min(Λ)) cannot be the limiting minimum distance. The overall code minimum distance is indeed 2d²_min(Λ) (the intra-coset distance within Λ'), so the conclusion is correct, but the logical flow is poorly stated: the passage should explicitly state that d²_min(C) is the minimum of the intra-coset distance 2d²_min(Λ) and the inter-path distance 3d²_min(Λ), yielding 2d²_min(Λ). As written, the word 'Thus' suggests that d²_min(C) = 2d²_min(Λ) follows from the 3d²_min(Λ) bound, which is a non sequitur.

comments.title: Incorrect description of output bits: 2k representing a mod-2 sum
comments.quote: $k$ representing the inputs one time unit earlier, and $2k$ representing the mod- 2 sum of the current inputs with those two time units earlier
comments.explanation: For a rate-1/2 encoder with k information bits and 2k output bits, the natural split is k bits from one source and k bits from another, totaling 2k. The passage says 'k representing the inputs one time unit earlier, and 2k representing the mod-2 sum...', which would give k + 2k = 3k output bits rather than 2k. The second group should be k bits (not 2k), representing the mod-2 sum of the current inputs with those two time units earlier.

comments.title: Inconsistent minimum distance claim for Class VII codes
comments.quote: two distinct paths differ by at least $d_{\text{min}}^{2}(\Lambda^{\prime})$ where they diverge and $d_{\text{min}}^{2}(\Lambda)$ where they merge, so that $d_{\min}^{2}(\mathbb{C})=3 d_{\min}^{2}(\Lambda)$
comments.explanation: The divergence contributes at least d²_min(Λ') = 2d²_min(Λ) and the merge contributes at least d²_min(Λ), giving a total lower bound of 3d²_min(Λ). However, the passage then states d²_min(C) = 3d²_min(Λ), which would mean the code's minimum distance equals this path-based lower bound rather than being constrained by d²_min(Λ'') = 4d²_min(Λ) from the partition. This is actually consistent if parallel transitions (same-state transitions) contribute d²_min(Λ') = 2d²_min(Λ) and the path-divergence bound is 3d²_min(Λ), so the overall minimum is 2d²_min(Λ). The stated value of 3d²_min(Λ) appears to neglect parallel transitions at the coset level of Λ'/Λ''. Since the encoder selects cosets of Λ'' within Λ', parallel transitions at the second level can produce distance d²_min(Λ') = 2d²_min(Λ), which is less than 3d²_min(Λ). However, this depends on the specific encoder structure (Class VII vs Class III), so this may or may not be an error depending on details not fully shown. Given the analogy to Class III is explicitly stated, and Class III codes use single-level partitions, the claim is plausible within the paper's framework.

comments.title: Path distance lower bound of 5d²_min(Λ) contradicts stated code minimum distance of 4d²_min(Λ)
comments.quote: so that the minimum squared distance between distinct paths is at least $5 d_{\text{min}}^{2}(\Lambda)$. Hence $d_{\text{min}}^{2}(\mathbb{C})= d_{\text{min}}^{2}(\Lambda^{\prime\prime})=4 d_{\text{min}}^{2}(\Lambda)$
comments.explanation: The passage establishes that diverging paths have minimum squared distance at least 5d²_min(Λ), but then concludes d²_min(C) = 4d²_min(Λ). This means parallel transitions (paths that do not diverge, i.e., transitions from the same state to the same state) must contribute d²_min(Λ'') = 4d²_min(Λ), which is the binding constraint. While this is logically consistent (the overall minimum distance is the minimum over both parallel transitions and diverging paths), the word 'Hence' is misleading — the conclusion d²_min(C) = 4d²_min(Λ) does not follow from the 5d²_min(Λ) bound on diverging paths. It follows from the parallel transition distance being 4d²_min(Λ), which is not explicitly stated in this passage. The logical connector 'Hence' incorrectly suggests the conclusion is derived from the preceding bound.

rag_retrieved.label: RAG Retrieved
rag_retrieved.overall_feedback:
## High-Level Feedback

This is a landmark tutorial and classification paper by Forney that provides a unifying framework—coset codes—for understanding virtually all known constructive coding techniques for band-limited channels, including lattice codes and trellis-coded modulation. The paper's greatest strength lies in its conceptual clarity and synthesis: by abstracting the encoding process into a lattice partition Λ/Λ′ and a binary encoder C, it elegantly decouples fundamental coding gain from constellation shaping, enabling systematic comparison across disparate code families. The writing is exceptionally clear for a paper of this technical depth, and the historical contextualization (from Shannon's capacity results through Ungerboeck's trellis-coded modulation to multidimensional schemes) is both informative and well-structured, giving readers a strong sense of how the field evolved and why a unifying framework was needed. The paper builds effectively on prior work, particularly [1] and Calderbank and Sloane [13], while making the important intellectual contribution of formalizing and generalizing the common structure underlying these schemes. As an invited paper and the first part of what appears to be a multi-part series, it appropriately focuses on geometric classification parameters (coding gain, error coefficient, decoding complexity, constellation expansion factor) while deferring detailed treatment of other aspects. The most significant potential concern is whether the restriction to "lattice-type" coset codes—acknowledged briefly with the mention of phase-modulated codes—limits the framework's generality, and the paper would benefit from a more explicit discussion of what classes of coded modulation schemes, if any, fall outside this framework and why. Additionally, while the abstract promises classification of "a number of new classes," the portion reviewed is still introductory, so the ultimate impact depends on the depth and novelty of the classification that follows.

comments.title: Fundamental volume equated to 2^r(C) instead of involving V(Λ)
comments.quote: the fundamental volume $V(\mathbb{C})$ per $N$ dimensions, which is equal to $2^{r(\mathbb{C})}$
comments.explanation: From para 204, V(C) = 2^{r(C)} V(Λ), so V(C) is not simply 2^{r(C)}. The fundamental volume equals 2^{r(C)} times V(Λ), not 2^{r(C)} alone. The statement as written omits the factor V(Λ). This only reduces to 2^{r(C)} in the special case where V(Λ) = 1 (e.g., Λ = Z^{2N}), but the passage states it as a general fact.

comments.title: Spurious comma in code formula for RD₄
comments.explanation: There is a stray comma after the equals sign in the code formula for RD₄. It should read 'RD₄ = 2Z⁴ + (4,1,4)' without the extraneous comma.

comments.title: Depth relationship when μ is odd
comments.quote: when $\mu$ is odd, $\mu=2 m-1$, the dual lattice under this definition will be a version of the dual lattice as defined earlier, rotated by the rotation operator $R$
comments.explanation: For D₄, the complex (φ-depth) is μ = 1 and the 2-depth is m = 1. The stated relation μ = 2m − 1 gives 1 = 2(1) − 1 = 1, which is consistent. However, the passage says the alternative (real) definition coincides with the complex definition when μ is even (μ = 2m), meaning the 2-depth m = μ/2. For E₈ with μ = 2, this gives m = 1, but the passage then states E₈ has 'depth 2' for its real code formula using 2^m, i.e., 2² Z⁸ = 4Z⁸, whereas the code formula written is 2Z⁸ + (8,4,4), implying m = 1. So the relationship μ = 2m when μ is even is consistent (μ = 2, m = 1), but the passage says 'E₈ has depth 2' without clarifying this refers to φ-depth, which could be confusing. This is not strictly an error but is a potential source of confusion given the two depth conventions.

comments.title: Incorrect notation for binomial coefficient
comments.explanation: The subscript notation is inconsistent: earlier in the same sentence the binomial coefficient is written as $C_{nj}$ (with both n and j as a single subscript), but then it is re-introduced as $C_{n_{j}}$ (with j as a sub-subscript of n). These should be typeset identically, e.g., $C_{nj}$ or $\binom{n}{j}$. This is a notation inconsistency rather than a substantive mathematical error, but it could cause confusion.

comments.title: Incorrect duality relation for Reed-Muller codes
comments.quote: Because $\mathrm{RM}(n-r-1, n)$ and $\mathrm{RM}(r, n)$ are dual codes
comments.explanation: The standard duality relation for Reed-Muller codes is that the dual of RM(r, n) is RM(n-r-1, n). However, in this passage the variable 'r' is not relevant to Λ(0, n) — it appears to be a free variable introduced from the later discussion of Λ(r, n). For the self-duality argument of Λ(0, n), the dual formula (para 154) replaces each code C_j with its dual C_j⊥ and reverses the order. The code at level j in Λ(0, n) is RM(n-1-j, n), and its dual is RM(j, n) = RM(n-1-(n-1-j), n). The correct statement to justify self-duality should reference the specific codes appearing in the code formula of Λ(0, n), namely that RM(n-1-j, n)⊥ = RM(j, n), and that this reversal reproduces the same code formula. The use of the extraneous variable 'r' (which is zero for Λ(0, n)) is a notation inconsistency that makes the justification appear ill-formed.

comments.title: Incorrect dimension subscript on checkerboard lattice $D_N$
comments.quote: The lattice $\Lambda(n-1, n), n \geq 1$, is the "checkerboard lattice" $D_{N}$
comments.explanation: Since $\Lambda(n-1,n)$ is an $N$-dimensional complex $G$-lattice, it corresponds to a $2N$-dimensional real lattice, so it should be $D_{2N}$, not $D_N$. For example, when $n=1$, $\Lambda(0,1) = D_4$ (as stated in para 158), but $N = 2^1 = 2$, so $D_N = D_2$ would be incorrect while $D_{2N} = D_4$ is correct.

comments.title: Incorrect/incomplete notation for dual of Barnes-Wall lattice
comments.explanation: Using the dual formula from para 169 with r=0, we get Λ(0,n)⊥ = φⁿG^N + φⁿ⁻¹RM(n-1,n) + ... + RM(0,n). Comparing with the definition of Λ(r,n) in para 165, this equals Λ(0,n) itself (since the Barnes-Wall lattice is self-dual up to scaling). The notation 'Λ(n)' is not defined anywhere in the paper; it should likely be 'Λ(0,n)' or 'BW_N' or similar. The paper elsewhere uses Λ(r,n) with two arguments for principal sublattices, so 'Λ(n)' with one argument is inconsistent and undefined.

comments.title: Incorrect claim that the dual partition chain equals Λ(0,n)
comments.explanation: From the dual formula in para 169, Λ(0,n)^⊥ has depth n (same as Λ(0,n)) and its code formula involves RM(n-1,n)/…/RM(0,n), which is the same as Λ(0,n) itself — so Λ(0,n)^⊥ = Λ(0,n) (the Barnes-Wall lattice is self-dual as a complex lattice). However, the end of the chain should be Λ(0,n)^⊥ = Λ(0,n), not the beginning. The claim that the entire chain equals Λ(0,n) is a notational/logical error: the chain starts at Z^{2N} and ends at Λ(0,n)^⊥ = Λ(0,n), but writing the equality sign after the last term suggests the whole chain collapses to Λ(0,n), which is misleading. More substantively, the distances listed as 1/2/…/2^n are questionable for the dual chain, since the dual lattices Λ(r,n)^⊥ have different minimum distances than Λ(r,n). From the dual code formula, Λ(r,n)^⊥ uses codes RM(0,n) through RM(n-r-1,n), giving minimum distance governed by RM(0,n) which has minimum distance 2^n, so the distance structure of the dual chain does not simply mirror that of the primal chain.

comments.title: Incorrect starting point of the second dual partition chain
comments.quote: $\Lambda(0, n)=\Lambda(0, n)^{\perp} / \phi \Lambda(1, n)^{\perp} / \cdots / \phi^{n} \Lambda(n, n)^{\perp}=\phi^{n} G^{N} \simeq R^{n} Z^{2N}$
comments.explanation: The chain begins with Λ(0,n) = Λ(0,n)^⊥, which is correct since Λ(0,n) is self-dual. But the claim that φ^n Λ(n,n)^⊥ = φ^n G^N requires Λ(n,n)^⊥ = G^N. From the dual formula, Λ(n,n)^⊥ should have depth n-n=0 with code formula G^N (trivially), so Λ(n,n)^⊥ = G^N = Z^{2N}, which checks out. However, the depths listed as n/n/…/n are suspect: φ^r Λ(r,n)^⊥ has depth (n-r) from the dual lattice plus r additional factors of φ, but the paper's convention in para 176 states that φ^j Λ(r,n) is regarded as having depth n-r, not n.
