Proposition 4-Variance Control: Consider problem (IT-G) with a cost of intervention satisfying Assumption 5. Suppose Assumptions 1 and 2 hold and the network game satisfies Property A. Let the optimal intervention be $\mathcal{B}^{*}$, and let $\boldsymbol{b}^{*}$ be a typical realization. We have the following:

1. Suppose the planner likes variance (i.e., in (9), $w>0$ ). If the game has strategic complements ( $\beta>0$ ), then $\operatorname{Var}\left(\boldsymbol{u}^{\ell}(\boldsymbol{G}) \cdot \boldsymbol{b}^{*}\right)$ is weakly decreasing in $\ell$; if the game has strategic substitutes $(\beta<0)$, then $\operatorname{Var}\left(\boldsymbol{u}^{\ell}(\boldsymbol{G}) \cdot \boldsymbol{b}^{*}\right)$ is weakly increasing in $\ell$.
2. Suppose the planner dislikes variance (i.e., $w<0$ ). If the game has strategic complements ( $\beta>0$ ), then $\operatorname{Var}\left(\boldsymbol{u}^{\ell}(\boldsymbol{G}) \cdot \boldsymbol{b}^{*}\right)$ is weakly increasing in $\ell$; if the game has strategic substitutes ( $\beta<0$ ), then $\operatorname{Var}\left(\boldsymbol{u}^{\ell}(\boldsymbol{G}) \cdot \boldsymbol{b}^{*}\right)$ is weakly decreasing in $\ell$.

We now provide the intuition for Proposition 4. Shocks to individual's standalone marginal returns create variability in the players' equilibrium actions. The assumption that the intervention is mean-neutral (part (a) of Assumption 5) leaves the planner to control only the variances and covariances of these marginal returns with her intervention. Hence, the solution to the intervention problem describes what the planner should do to induce second moments of the action distribution that maximize ex ante expected welfare.

Suppose first that investments are strategic complements. Then a perfectly correlated (random) shock in individual standalone marginal returns is amplified by strategic interactions. In fact, the type of shock that is most amplifying (at a given size) is the one that

[^16]is perfectly correlated across individuals: a common deviation from the mean is scaled by the vector $\boldsymbol{u}^{1}$-the individuals' eigenvector centralities. Such shocks are exactly what $\underline{b}_{1}^{*}=\boldsymbol{u}^{1}(\boldsymbol{G}) \cdot \boldsymbol{b}^{*}$ captures. Hence, this is the dimension of volatility that the planner most wants to increase if she likes variance in actions $(w>0)$ and most wants to decrease if she dislikes variance in actions ( $w<0$ ).

If investments are strategic substitutes, then a perfectly correlated shock does not create a lot of variance in actions: the first-order response of all individuals to an increase in their standalone marginal returns is to increase investment, but that in turn makes all individuals decrease their investment somewhat because of the strategic substitutability with their neighbors. Hence, highly positively correlated shocks do not translate into high volatility. The shock profiles (of a fixed norm) that create the most variability in equilibrium actions are actually the ones in which neighbors have negatively correlated shocks. A planner that likes variance in actions will then prioritize such shocks. Because the last eigenvector of the system has entries that are as different as possible across neighbors, this is exactly the type of volatility that will be most amplified, and this is what the planner will focus on most.

Example 3-Illustration in the Case of the Circle: Figure 1 depicts six of the eigenvectors/principal components of a circle network with 14 nodes. The first principal component is a positive vector and so $\mathcal{B}$ projected on $\boldsymbol{u}^{1}(\boldsymbol{G})$ captures shocks that are positively correlated across all players. The second principal component (top left panel of Figure 1) splits the graph into two sides, one with positive entries and the other with negative entries. Hence, $\mathcal{B}$ projected on $\boldsymbol{u}^{2}(\boldsymbol{G})$ captures shocks that are highly positively correlated on each side of the circle network, with the two opposite sides of the circle being anticorrelated. As we move along the sequence of eigenvectors $\boldsymbol{u}^{\ell}$, we can see that $\mathcal{B}$ projected on the $\ell$ th eigenvector represents patterns of shocks that "vary more" across the network. At the extreme, $\mathcal{B}$ projected on $\boldsymbol{u}^{14}(\boldsymbol{G})$ (bottom-right panel of Figure 1) captures the component of shocks that is perfectly anti-correlated across neighbors. ${ }^{32}$

## 6. CONCLUDING REMARKS

We have studied the problem of a planner who seeks to optimally target incentive changes in a network game. Our framework allows for a broad class of strategic and nonstrategic spillovers across neighbors. The main contribution of the paper is methodological: we show that principal components of the network of interaction provide a useful basis for analyzing the effects of an intervention. This decomposition leads to our main result: there is a close connection between the strategic properties of the game (whether actions are strategic complements or substitutes) and the weight that different principal components receive in the optimal intervention. To develop these ideas in the simplest way, we have focused on a model in which the matrix of interaction is symmetric, the costs of intervention are quadratic, and the intervention itself takes the form of altering the standalone marginal returns of actions. In the Supplemental Material, we relax these restrictions and develop extensions of our approach to non-symmetric matrices of interaction and to more general costs of intervention, including a model where interventions occur via monetary incentives for activity. We also relax Property A, a technical condition which facilitated our basic analysis, and cover a more general class of externalities.

[^17]We briefly mention two further applications. In some circumstances, the planner seeks a budget-balanced tax/subsidy scheme in order to improve the economic outcome. In an oligopoly market, for example, a planner could tax some suppliers, thereby increasing their marginal costs, and then use that tax revenue to subsidize other suppliers. The planner will solve a problem similar to the one we have studied here, with the important difference that she will face a different constraint-namely, a budget-balance constraint. In ongoing work, Galeotti, Golub, Goyal, Talamàs, and Tamuz (2020) show that the principal component approach that we employed in this paper is useful in deriving the optimal taxation scheme and, in turn, in determining the welfare gains that can be achieved via tax/subsidy interventions in supply chains. ${ }^{33}$

We have focused on interventions that alter the standalone marginal returns of individuals. Another interesting problem is the study of interventions that alter the matrix of interaction. We hope this paper stimulates further work along these lines.

## APPENDIX: PROOFS

Proof of Theorem 1: We wish to solve

$$
\begin{array}{rl}
\max _{\boldsymbol{b}} & w \boldsymbol{a}^{\top} \boldsymbol{a} \\
\text { s.t.: } & {[\boldsymbol{I}-\beta \boldsymbol{G}] \boldsymbol{a}=\boldsymbol{b},} \\
& \sum_{i \in \mathcal{N}}\left(b_{i}-\hat{b}_{i}\right)^{2} \leq C .
\end{array}
$$

We transform the maximization problem into the basis given by the principal components of $\boldsymbol{G}$. To this end, we first rewrite the cost and the objective in the principal components basis, using the fact that norms do not change under the orthogonal transformation $\boldsymbol{U}^{\top}$. (The norm symbol $\|\cdot\|$ always refers to the Euclidean norm.) Letting $\boldsymbol{y}=\boldsymbol{b}-\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}$,

and

$$
w \boldsymbol{a}^{\top} \boldsymbol{a}=w\|\boldsymbol{a}\|^{2}=w\|\underline{\boldsymbol{a}}\|^{2}=w \underline{\boldsymbol{a}^{\top}} \underline{\boldsymbol{a}} .
$$

By recalling that, in equilibrium, $\underline{\boldsymbol{a}}^{*}=[\boldsymbol{I}-\boldsymbol{\beta} \boldsymbol{\Lambda}]^{-1} \underline{\boldsymbol{b}}$, and using the definition $\alpha_{\ell}= \frac{1}{\left(1-\beta \lambda_{\ell}(\boldsymbol{G})\right)^{2}}$, the intervention problem (IT) can be rewritten as

(IT-PC)

[^18]We now transform the problem so that the control variable is $\boldsymbol{x}$ where $x_{\ell}=y_{\ell} / \underline{\hat{b}}_{\ell}$. We obtain
Note that, for all $\ell, \alpha_{\ell}$ are well-defined (by Assumption 1) and strictly positive (by genericity of $\boldsymbol{G}$ ). This has two implications. ${ }^{34}$

First, at the optimal solution $\boldsymbol{x}^{*}$, the resource constraint problem must bind. To see this, note that Assumption 3 says that either $w>0$, or $w<0$ and $\sum_{\ell=1}^{n} \hat{\underline{b}}_{\ell}^{2}>C$. Suppose that at the optimal solution, the constraint does not bind. Then, without violating the constraint, we can slightly increase or decrease any $x_{\ell}$. If $w>0$ (resp. $w<0$ ), the increase or the decrease is guaranteed to increase (resp. decrease) the corresponding $\left(x_{\ell}+1\right)^{2}$ (since the $\alpha_{\ell}$ are all strictly positive).

Second, we show that the optimal solution $\boldsymbol{x}^{*}$ satisfies $x_{\ell}^{*} \geq 0$ for every $\ell$ if $w>0$, and $x_{\ell}^{*} \in[-1,0]$ for every $\ell$ if $w<0$. Suppose $w>0$ and, for some $\ell, x_{\ell}^{*}<0$. Then $\left[-x_{\ell}^{*}+\right. 1]^{2}>\left[x_{\ell}^{*}+1\right]^{2}$. Since $w>0$ and every $\alpha_{\ell}$ is positive, we can raise the aggregate utility without changing the cost by flipping the sign of $x_{\ell}^{*}$. Analogously, suppose $w<0$. It is clear that if $x_{\ell}^{*}<-1$, then by setting $x_{\ell}=-1$, the objective improves and the constraint is relaxed; hence, at the optimum, $x_{\ell}^{*} \geq-1$. Suppose next that $x_{\ell}>0$ for some $\ell$. Then $\left[-x_{\ell}^{*}+1\right]^{2}<\left[x_{\ell}^{*}+1\right]^{2}$. Since $w<0$ and every $\alpha_{\ell}$ is positive, we can improve the value of the objective function without changing the cost by flipping the sign of $x_{\ell}^{*}$.

We now complete the proof. Observe that the Lagrangian corresponding to the maximization problem is

$$
\mathcal{L}=w \sum_{\ell=1}^{n} \alpha_{\ell}\left(1+x_{\ell}\right)^{2} \underline{\hat{b}}_{\ell}+\mu\left[C-\sum_{\ell=1}^{n} \hat{b}_{\ell}^{2} x_{\ell}^{2}\right] .
$$

Taking our observation above that the constraint is binding at $\boldsymbol{x}=\boldsymbol{x}^{*}$, together with the standard results on the Karush-Kuhn-Tucker conditions, the first-order conditions must hold exactly at the optimum with a positive $\mu$ :

$$
0=\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x_{\ell}}=2 \hat{b}_{\ell}^{2}\left[w \alpha_{\ell}\left(1+x_{\ell}^{*}\right)-\mu x_{\ell}^{*}\right]=0 .
$$

We take a generic $\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}$ such that $\hat{\underline{b}}_{\ell} \neq 0$ for all $\ell$. If, for some $\ell$, we had $\mu=w \alpha_{\ell}$, then the right-hand side of the second equality in (11) would be $2 \hat{b}_{\ell}^{2} w \alpha_{\ell}$, which, by the generic assumption we just made and the positivity of $\alpha_{\ell}$, would contradict (11). Thus, the following holds with a nonzero denominator:

$$
x_{\ell}^{*}=\frac{w \alpha_{\ell}}{\mu-w \alpha_{\ell}}
$$

[^19]and the Lagrange multiplier $\mu$ is therefore pinned down by

$$
\sum_{\ell=1}^{n} w^{2} \hat{b}_{\ell}^{2}\left(\frac{\alpha_{\ell}}{\mu-w \alpha_{\ell}}\right)^{2}=C .
$$
Proof of Proposition 1: Part 1. From expression (6) of Theorem 1, it follows that if $C \rightarrow 0$, then $\mu \rightarrow \infty$. The result follows by noticing that
Part 2. Suppose that $\beta>0$. Using the derivation of the last part of the proof of Theorem 1, we write

with $x_{\ell}^{*}=\frac{w \alpha_{\ell}}{\mu-w \alpha_{\ell}}$. From expression (6) of Theorem 1, it follows that if $C \rightarrow \infty$, then $\mu \rightarrow w \alpha_{1}$. This implies that $x_{\ell}^{*} \rightarrow \frac{\alpha_{\ell}}{\alpha_{1}-\alpha_{\ell}}$ for all $\ell \neq 1$. As a result, if $C \rightarrow \infty$, then $\rho\left(\boldsymbol{y}^{*}, \boldsymbol{u}^{\ell}(\boldsymbol{G})\right) \rightarrow$ 0 for all $\ell \neq 1$. Furthermore, we can rewrite expression (6) of Theorem 1 as

$$
\sum_{\ell=1}^{n}\left(\|\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}\| \rho\left(\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}, \boldsymbol{u}^{\ell}(\boldsymbol{G})\right) \frac{x_{\ell}^{*}}{\sqrt{C}}\right)^{2}=1
$$

and therefore

$$
\lim _{C \rightarrow \infty} \sum_{\ell=1}^{n}\left(\|\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}\| \rho\left(\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}, \boldsymbol{u}^{\ell}(\boldsymbol{G})\right) \frac{x_{\ell}^{*}}{\sqrt{C}}\right)^{2}=\lim _{C \rightarrow \infty}\left(\|\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}\| \rho\left(\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}, \boldsymbol{u}^{1}(\boldsymbol{G})\right) \frac{x_{1}^{*}}{\sqrt{C}}\right)^{2}=1,
$$

where the first equality follows because $x_{\ell}^{*} \rightarrow \frac{\alpha_{\ell}}{\alpha_{1}-\alpha_{\ell}}$ for all $\ell \neq 1$. The proof for the case of $\beta<0$ follows the same steps, with the only exception that if $C \rightarrow \infty$, then $\mu \rightarrow w \alpha_{n}$. Q.E.D.
Proof of Proposition 2: We first prove the result on welfare and then turn to the result on cosine similarity.

Welfare. Consider the case of strategic complementarities, $\beta>0$. Define by $\tilde{x}$ the simple intervention, and note that $\tilde{x}_{1}=\sqrt{C} / \underline{\hat{b}}_{1}$ and that $\tilde{x}_{\ell}=0$ for all $\ell>1$. The aggregate utility obtained under the simple intervention is

$$
W^{s}=\sum_{\ell=1}^{n} \hat{\underline{b}}_{\ell}^{2} \alpha_{\ell}\left(1+\tilde{x}_{\ell}\right)^{2}=\hat{\underline{b}}_{1}^{2} \alpha_{1} \tilde{x}_{1}\left(\tilde{x}_{1}+2\right)+\sum_{\ell=1}^{n} \alpha_{\ell} \hat{\underline{b}}_{\ell}^{2} .
$$

The aggregate utility at the optimal intervention is

$$
W^{*}=\sum_{\ell=1}^{n} \hat{b}_{\ell}^{2} \alpha_{\ell}\left(1+x_{\ell}^{*}\right)^{2}=\hat{b}_{1}^{2} \alpha_{1} x_{1}^{*}\left(x_{1}^{*}+2\right)+\sum_{\ell=2}^{n} \hat{b}_{\ell}^{2} \alpha_{\ell} x_{\ell}^{*}\left(x_{\ell}^{*}+2\right)+\sum_{\ell=1}^{n} \alpha_{\ell} \hat{b}_{\ell}^{2}
$$
Hence, letting $D=\underline{\hat{b}}_{1}^{2} \alpha_{1} \tilde{x}_{1}\left(\tilde{x}_{1}+2\right)+\sum_{\ell=1}^{n} \alpha_{\ell} \hat{\hat{b}}_{\ell}^{2}$,

$$
\begin{array}{rlr}
\frac{W^{*}}{W^{s}} & =\frac{\hat{b}_{1}^{2} \alpha_{1} x_{1}^{*}\left(x_{1}^{*}+2\right)+\sum_{\ell=1}^{n} \alpha_{\ell} \hat{b}_{\ell}^{2}}{D}+\frac{\sum_{\ell=2}^{n} \hat{b}_{\ell}^{2} \alpha_{\ell} x_{\ell}^{*}\left(x_{\ell}^{*}+2\right)}{D} & \\
& \leq 1+\frac{\sum_{\ell=2}^{n} \hat{b}_{\ell}^{2} \alpha_{\ell} x_{\ell}^{*}\left(x_{\ell}^{*}+2\right)}{D} & \\
& \leq 1+\frac{\sum_{\ell=2}^{n} \hat{b}_{\ell}^{2} \alpha_{\ell} x_{\ell}^{*}\left(x_{\ell}^{*}+2\right)}{\hat{b}_{1}^{2} \alpha_{1} \tilde{x}_{1}^{2}} & \\
& =1+\frac{\sum_{\ell=2}^{n} \hat{b}_{\ell}^{2} \alpha_{\ell} x_{\ell}^{*}\left(x_{\ell}^{*}+2\right)}{\alpha_{1} C} & \\
& \leq 1+\frac{2 \alpha_{1}-\alpha_{2}^{*}}{\alpha_{1}} \frac{\|\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}\|^{2}}{C}\left(\frac{\alpha_{2}}{\alpha_{1}-\alpha_{2}}\right)^{2} & \\
& \leq 1+\frac{2\|\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}\|^{2}}{C}\left(\frac{\alpha_{2}}{\alpha_{1}-\alpha_{2}}\right)^{2} & \\
& \text { see calculation below } D \text { are positive }
\end{array}
$$

The fact $\underline{b}_{1}^{2} \tilde{x}_{1}^{2}=C$, used above, follows because the simple policy allocates the entire budget to changing $\underline{b}_{1}$. The inequality after that statement follows because

$$
\begin{array}{rlr}
\sum_{\ell=2}^{n} \hat{b}_{\ell}^{2} \alpha_{\ell} x_{\ell}^{*}\left(x_{\ell}^{*}+2\right) & \leq \alpha_{2} \sum_{\ell=2}^{n} \hat{b}_{\ell}^{2} x_{\ell}^{*}\left(x_{\ell}^{*}+2\right) & \text { ordering of the } \alpha_{\ell} \\
& \leq \alpha_{2} x_{2}^{*}\left(x_{2}^{*}+2\right) \sum_{\ell=2}^{n} \hat{b}_{\ell}^{2} & \text { Corollary } 1 \\
& \leq \alpha_{2} \frac{w \alpha_{2}}{\mu-w \alpha_{2}}\left(\frac{w \alpha_{2}}{\mu-w \alpha_{2}}+2\right) \sum_{\ell=2}^{n} \underline{\hat{b}}_{\ell}^{2} & \text { Theorem 1 } \\
& \leq \alpha_{2} \frac{w \alpha_{2}}{w \alpha_{1}-w \alpha_{2}}\left(\frac{w \alpha_{2}}{w \alpha_{1}-w \alpha_{2}}+2\right)\|\underline{\hat{b}}\|^{2} & \\
& =\left(\frac{\alpha_{2}}{\alpha_{1}-\alpha_{2}}\right)^{2}\left(2 \alpha_{1}-\alpha_{2}\right)\|\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}\|^{2} . &
\end{array}
$$

Hence, the inequality

$$
C>\frac{2\|\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}\|^{2}}{\epsilon}\left(\frac{\alpha_{2}}{\alpha_{1}-\alpha_{2}}\right)^{2}
$$

is sufficient to establish that $\frac{W^{*}}{W^{s}}<1+\epsilon$. The proof for the case of strategic substitutes follows the same steps; the only difference is that we use $\alpha_{n}$ instead of $\alpha_{1}$ and $\alpha_{n-1}$ instead of $\alpha_{2}$.

Cosine similarity. We now turn to the cosine similarity result. We focus on the case of strategic complements. The proof for the case of strategic substitutes is analogous. We start by writing a useful explicit expression for $\rho\left(\Delta \boldsymbol{b}^{*}, \sqrt{C} \boldsymbol{u}^{1}\right)$ :

$$
\rho\left(\Delta \boldsymbol{b}^{*}, \sqrt{C} \boldsymbol{u}^{1}\right)=\frac{\left(\boldsymbol{b}^{*}-\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}\right) \cdot\left(\sqrt{C} \boldsymbol{u}^{1}\right)}{\left\|\boldsymbol{b}^{*}-\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}\right\|\left\|\sqrt{C} \boldsymbol{u}^{1}\right\|}=\frac{\left(\boldsymbol{b}^{*}-\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}\right) \cdot\left(\boldsymbol{u}^{1}\right)}{\sqrt{C}},
$$
where the last equality follows because, at the optimum, $\left\|\boldsymbol{b}^{*}-\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}\right\|^{2}=C$. At the optimal intervention, by Theorem 1,
now, using the definition $\underline{\boldsymbol{b}}=\boldsymbol{U}^{T} \boldsymbol{b}$, we have that

and therefore

$$
\left(\boldsymbol{b}^{*}-\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}\right) \cdot \boldsymbol{u}^{1}=\sum_{i} \sum_{\ell=1}^{n} u_{i}^{1} u_{i}^{\ell} \frac{w \alpha_{\ell}}{\mu-w \alpha_{\ell}} \hat{\underline{b}}_{\ell}=\sum_{\ell=1}^{n} \frac{w \alpha_{\ell}}{\mu-w \alpha_{\ell}} \hat{\underline{b}}_{\ell}\left(\boldsymbol{u}^{1} \cdot \boldsymbol{u}^{\ell}\right)=\frac{w \alpha_{1}}{\mu-w \alpha_{1}} \hat{\underline{b}}_{1} .
$$

Hence, using this in equation (12), we can deduce that

$$
\rho\left(\Delta \boldsymbol{b}^{*}, \boldsymbol{u}^{1}\right)=\frac{1}{\sqrt{C}} \frac{w \alpha_{1}}{\mu-w \alpha_{1}} \hat{b}_{1} \geq \sqrt{1-\epsilon} \quad \text { iff } \quad\left(\frac{w \alpha_{1}}{\mu-w \alpha_{1}}\right)^{2} \hat{b}_{1}^{2}-C(1-\epsilon) \geq 0 .
$$
We now claim that the inequality in the above display after the "if and only if" follows from our hypothesis that

$$
C>\frac{2\|\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}\|^{2}}{\epsilon}\left(\frac{\alpha_{2}}{\alpha_{1}-\alpha_{2}}\right)^{2} .
$$

This claim is established by the following lemma.
Lemma 1: Assume

$$
C>\frac{2\|\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}\|^{2}}{\epsilon}\left(\frac{\alpha_{2}}{\alpha_{1}-\alpha_{2}}\right)^{2} .
$$

Then

$$
\left(\frac{w \alpha_{1}}{\mu-w \alpha_{1}}\right)^{2} \hat{b}_{1}^{2} \geq C(1-\epsilon) .
$$

Proof of Lemma 1: Note that

$$
C>\frac{2\|\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}\|^{2}}{\epsilon}\left(\frac{\alpha_{2}}{\alpha_{1}-\alpha_{2}}\right)^{2} \Longrightarrow \epsilon C>\|\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}\|^{2}\left(\frac{\alpha_{2}}{\alpha_{1}-\alpha_{2}}\right)^{2}
$$

and therefore

$$
C(1-\epsilon)<C-\|\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}\|^{2}\left(\frac{\alpha_{2}}{\alpha_{1}-\alpha_{2}}\right)^{2} .
$$

But then we have the following chain of statements, explained immediately after the display:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left(\frac{w \alpha_{1}}{\mu-w \alpha_{1}}\right)^{2} \hat{b}_{1}^{2}-C(1-\epsilon) & \geq\left(\frac{w \alpha_{1}}{\mu-w \alpha_{1}}\right)^{2} \hat{b}_{1}^{2}-C+\|\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}\|^{2}\left(\frac{\alpha_{2}}{\alpha_{1}-\alpha_{2}}\right)^{2} \\
& =\left(\frac{w \alpha_{1}}{\mu-w \alpha_{1}}\right)^{2} \hat{b}_{1}^{2}-\sum_{\ell=1}^{n}\left(\frac{w \alpha_{\ell}}{\mu-w \alpha_{\ell}}\right)^{2} \hat{b}_{\ell}^{2}+\|\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}\|^{2}\left(\frac{\alpha_{2}}{\alpha_{1}-\alpha_{2}}\right)^{2} \\
& =\|\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}\|^{2}\left(\frac{\alpha_{2}}{\alpha_{1}-\alpha_{2}}\right)^{2}-\sum_{\ell=2}^{n}\left(\frac{w \alpha_{\ell}}{\mu-w \alpha_{\ell}}\right)^{2} \hat{b}_{\ell}^{2} \\
& =\left(\frac{\alpha_{2}}{\alpha_{1}-\alpha_{2}}\right)^{2} \sum_{\ell=1}^{n} \hat{b}_{\ell}^{2}-\sum_{\ell=2}^{n}\left(\frac{w \alpha_{\ell}}{\mu-w \alpha_{\ell}}\right)^{2} \hat{b}_{\ell}^{2}>0 .
\end{aligned}
$$

The first inequality follows from substituting the upper bound on $C(1-\epsilon)$, statement (15) above, which we derived from our initial condition on $C$. The equality after that follows by substituting the condition on the binding budget constraint at the optimum, which we derived in Theorem 1. The next equality follows by isolating the term for the first component in the summation and by noticing that that cancels with the first term. The next equality follows by noticing that $\|\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}\|^{2}=\|\underline{\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}}\|^{2}$. The final inequality follows because, from the facts that $\mu>w \alpha_{1}$ and that $\alpha_{1}>\alpha_{2}>\cdots>\alpha_{n}$, we can deduce that for each $\ell>1$,

$$
\frac{w \alpha_{\ell}}{\mu-w \alpha_{\ell}}<\frac{w \alpha_{\ell}}{w \alpha_{1}-w \alpha_{\ell}}=\frac{\alpha_{\ell}}{\alpha_{1}-\alpha_{\ell}}<\frac{\alpha_{2}}{\alpha_{1}-\alpha_{2}} .
$$

This concludes the proof of Proposition 2.

    ${ }^{9}$ See Ballester, Calvó-Armengol, and Zenou (2006) and Bramoullé, Kranton, and d'Amours (2014) for detailed discussions of this assumption and the interpretation of the solution given by (3).
[^5]: ${ }^{10}$ Other examples include workers keeping areas on a factory floor clean and safe, and businesses in a retail area contributing to the maintenance of their surroundings.

[^6]:
    ${ }^{11}$ See Spielman (2007), especially Section 16.5.1, on this interpretation. For book-length treatments of spectral graph theory, see Cvetkovic, Cvetković, Rowlinson, and Simic (1997) and Chung and Graham (1997).
    ${ }^{12}$ The circle network is invariant to rotations (cyclic permutations) of the nodes and so the eigenvectors here are determined only up to a rotation.
    ${ }^{13}$ For formal treatments of this phenomenon, see Davies, Gladwell, Leydold, and Stadler (2001) and Urschel (2018).

[^7]:
    ${ }^{14}$ Assumption 2 on the spectral radius implies that $\beta \boldsymbol{\Lambda}$ has no entries larger than 1 .
    ${ }^{15}$ In the local public goods application (recall Example 2), $w=-1$, and so when $C \geq\|\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}\|$, the optimal intervention satisfies $b_{i}^{*}=0$. Recalling our change of variables there ( $b_{i}=\left[\tau-\tilde{b}_{i}\right] / 2$ ), the optimal intervention in that case is to modify the endowment of each individual so that everyone accesses the optimal level of the local public good without investing.

[^8]: ${ }^{16}$ It can be verified that, for every $\ell \in\{1, \ldots, n-1\}$, the ratio $x_{\ell} / x_{\ell+1}$ is increasing (decreasing) in $\beta$ for the case of strategic complements (substitutes): thus the intensity of the strategic interaction shapes the relative importance of different principal components.

[^9]: ${ }^{17}$ Analogously, if $w<0$, for all $\ell, \ell^{\prime}$ such that $\alpha_{\ell}>\alpha_{\ell^{\prime}}$, we have that $\frac{w \alpha_{\ell}}{\mu-w \alpha_{\ell}}$ and $r_{\ell}^{*} / r_{\ell^{\prime}}^{*}$ are both decreasing in $C$.

[^10]:
    ${ }^{18}$ As costs are quadratic, a small relaxation in the budget around zero can have a large impact on aggregate welfare.
    ${ }^{19}$ When individuals' initial standalone marginal returns are zero ( $\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}=\mathbf{0}$ ), we can dispense with the approximations invoked for a large budget $C$. Assuming that $\boldsymbol{G}$ is generic, if $\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}=\mathbf{0}$, then, for any $C$, the entire budget is spent either (i) on changing $\underline{b}_{1}$ (if $\beta>0$ ) or (ii) on changing $\underline{b}_{n}$ (if $\beta<0$ ).
    ${ }^{20}$ About 125 times larger than $\|\widehat{\boldsymbol{b}}\|^{2}$.

[^11]:
    ${ }^{21}$ Supplemental Material Section OA2.1 presents a discussion of eigenvector centrality and how it compares to centrality measures that turn out to be important in related targeting problems.
    ${ }^{22}$ The last eigenvector of a graph is useful in diagnosing the bipartiteness of a graph and its chromatic number. Desai and Rao (1994) characterized the smallest eigenvalue of a graph and related it to a measure of the graph's bipartiteness. Alon and Kahale (1997) related the last eigenvector to a coloring of the underlying graph-a labeling of nodes by a minimal set of integers such that no neighboring nodes share the same label.
    ${ }^{23}$ The definition also specifies the interventions in more detail-that is, that they are proportional to the appropriate $u_{i}^{\ell}$. We could have instead left this flexible in this definition of simplicity and specified the dependence on the network explicitly in Proposition 2.

[^12]: ${ }^{24}$ Recall that $\left\|\frac{1}{n} \hat{\boldsymbol{b}}\right\|^{2}$ is equal to the sum of $\left(\frac{1}{n} \sum_{i \in \mathcal{N}} \hat{b}_{i}\right)^{2}$ (the squared mean of the entries of $\boldsymbol{b}$ ) and the sum of squared deviations of the entries of the vector $\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}$ from their mean.

[^13]:
    ${ }^{25}$ See Hartfiel and Meyer (1998), Levin, Peres, and Wilmer (2009), and Golub and Jackson (2012) for discussions and further citations to the literature on spectral gaps.
    ${ }^{26}$ The eigenvalue is in fact negative, as a consequence of the assumption that $g_{i i}=0$ for all $i$ : The trace of $\boldsymbol{G}$ is zero, and therefore its eigenvalues sum to 0 . By the Perron-Frobenius theorem, the maximum eigenvalue of the nonnegative matrix $\boldsymbol{G}$ is positive, so the minimum one must be negative.

[^14]:
    ${ }^{27}$ Intuitively, because $\boldsymbol{u}^{n}$ does not correspond to a perfect bipartition, it is easier for a vector orthogonal to $\boldsymbol{u}^{n}$ to achieve a similarly low value of $\sum_{i, j \in \mathcal{N}} g_{i j} u_{i} u_{j}$.
    ${ }^{28}$ The domain of this function is the set of all random vectors taking values in $\mathbb{R}^{n}$ defined on our probability space.
    ${ }^{29}$ It is possible to go further and allow for incomplete information among the individuals about each other's $b_{i}$. We do not pursue this substantial generalization here; see Golub and Morris (2020) and Lambert, Martini, and Ostrovsky (2018) for analyses in this direction.

[^15]: ${ }^{30}$ This reduction is justified as follows: since only the first two moments of standalone marginal returns affect equilibrium welfare, the planner should choose the cost-minimizing way to achieve a desired combination of these. We may take the cost of a given intervention to be the corresponding minimizer.

[^16]: ${ }^{31}$ When we look at the variance-covariance matrix of $\tilde{\boldsymbol{b}}$ defined by $\tilde{\boldsymbol{b}}-\overline{\boldsymbol{b}}=\boldsymbol{O}(\boldsymbol{b}-\overline{\boldsymbol{b}})$, the variance-covariance matrix becomes $\boldsymbol{O} \boldsymbol{\Sigma} \boldsymbol{O}^{\top}$, and this has the same eigenvalues and therefore the same trace.
[^17]: ${ }^{32}$ As usual in this example, a generic $\boldsymbol{G}$ will not be perfectly symmetric and so a particular orientation of these eigenvectors will be selected.
[^18]: ${ }^{33}$ In a recent paper, Gaitonde, Kleinberg and Tardos (2020) use spectral methods to study interventions that polarize opinions in a social network.

[^19]: ${ }^{34}$ Note that if Assumption 3 does not hold (i.e., $w<0$ and $\sum_{\ell=1}^{n} \hat{\underline{b}}_{\ell}^{2} \leq C$ ), then the optimal solution is $x_{\ell}^{*}=-1$ for all $\ell$. This is what we ruled out with Assumption 3, before Theorem 1.

ground_truth.label: Ground Truth (Expert)
ground_truth.overall_feedback:
**Central Claim**
The paper characterizes optimal incentive targeting in network games by decomposing policies along the principal components of the interaction matrix, finding that optimal interventions load on top eigenvectors for strategic complements and bottom eigenvectors for substitutes, eventually converging to simple single-component policies as budgets increase.

**Main Areas for Reflection**

- **Generalizing the welfare specification**
The current analysis relies on Property A, where aggregate welfare takes the form $W(b,G) \propto a^{* \top} a^*$. To assist readers interested in broader applications, it may be helpful to briefly discuss how the principal-component logic extends to general quadratic welfare forms. Incorporating a result from the current appendices into the main text could concisely demonstrate that the high-versus-low spectral targeting insight remains robust beyond this specific scalar structure.

- **Asymmetries and directed networks**
Since the decomposition $G=U\Lambda U^\top$ assumes symmetry, readers might naturally wonder about the applicability to directed networks common in economic settings. A short derivation or proposition clarifying how the analysis adapts—perhaps by focusing on the symmetric component $(G+G^\top)/2$ or singular vectors—would help clarify the scope. This could reinforce the qualitative findings regarding spectral modes without requiring a complete re-derivation of the model.

- **Instrument costs and heterogeneity**
The optimization currently exploits the rotational invariance of the cost function $K(b,\hat b)=\sum (b_i-\hat b_i)^2$. It might be beneficial to briefly address how the conceptual insights translate when costs are heterogeneous or when instruments operate on actions directly (e.g., subsidies). A short note mapping these more complex instruments into the existing $b$-space framework could clarify the conditions under which the eigenbasis decoupling remains a valid guide for policy.

- **Distinction from centrality measures**
Given the rich literature on spectral centrality, distinguishing the specific contributions of this decomposition approach is valuable. It could be illuminating to explicitly contrast the "bottom-eigenvector" targeting for substitutes against standard centrality heuristics found in prior work. A simple worked example where this method's recommendations diverge from traditional key-player policies would effectively highlight the distinct economic prescriptions offered by this framework.

comments.title: Comparative static claim in Footnote 16
comments.quote: It can be verified that, for every $\ell \in\{1, \ldots, n-1\}$, the ratio $x_{\ell} / x_{\ell+1}$ is increasing (decreasing) in $\beta$ for the case of strategic complements (substitutes): thus the intensity of the strategic interaction shapes the relative importance of different principal components.
comments.explanation:
Footnote 16 states that, for each $\ell$, the ratio $x_\ell/x_{\ell+1}$ is increasing in $\beta$ when $\beta>0$ and decreasing in $\beta$ when $\beta<0$. Using the expression from Theorem 1, $x_\ell^* = w\alpha_\ell/(\mu - w\alpha_\ell)$ with $\alpha_\ell=(1-\beta\lambda_\ell)^{-2}$ and $\mu$ determined by (6), one can look at the small‑budget limit $C\to 0$, where $\mu\to\infty$ and hence
\[
\frac{x_\ell^*}{x_{\ell+1}^*}\to \frac{\alpha_\ell}{\alpha_{\ell+1}} = \left(\frac{1-\beta\lambda_{\ell+1}}{1-\beta\lambda_\ell}\right)^2.
\]
Differentiating this limiting expression with respect to $\beta$ gives a strictly positive derivative for all admissible $\beta$, irrespective of the sign of $\beta$. Thus, at least for small budgets, the ratio $x_\ell^*/x_{\ell+1}^*$ is increasing in $\beta$ both for strategic complements and for strategic substitutes, contrary to the literal wording of the footnote.

This is a local, not a global, calculation, but by continuity it is hard to reconcile with the claim that the ratio is everywhere decreasing in $\beta$ when $\beta<0$. It would be helpful to double‑check this comparative static and either (i) reverse the direction for the substitutes case, or (ii) clarify that the intended statement is about monotonicity in the intensity $|\beta|$ rather than in $\beta$ itself, or else drop the claim.

comments.title: Limiting direction of intervention in Proposition 1
comments.quote:
If $\beta>0$ (the game features strategic complements), then the similarity of $\boldsymbol{y}^{*}$ and the first principal component of the network tends to $1: \rho\left(\boldsymbol{y}^{*}, \boldsymbol{u}^{1}(\boldsymbol{G})\right) \rightarrow 1$.
2b. If $\beta<0$ (the game features strategic substitutes), then the similarity of $\boldsymbol{y}^{*}$ and the last principal component of the network tends to $1: \rho\left(\boldsymbol{y}^{*}, \boldsymbol{u}^{n}(\boldsymbol{G})\right) \rightarrow 1$.
comments.explanation:
In Proposition 1(2a–b) you state that, as $C\to\infty$, the cosine similarity between the optimal intervention and the relevant principal component converges to 1, and you later write that $\boldsymbol{y}^*\to\sqrt{C}\,\boldsymbol{u}^1(\boldsymbol{G})$ (or $\sqrt{C}\,\boldsymbol{u}^n(\boldsymbol{G})$). 

Given Theorem 1 and the budget constraint, what can generally be shown under Assumptions 1–3 and Property A is that $\rho(\boldsymbol{y}^*,\boldsymbol{u}^1)^2\to 1$ when $\beta>0$ (and analogously for $\boldsymbol{u}^n$ when $\beta<0$). For $w>0$ we have $x_\ell^* = w\alpha_\ell/(\mu-w\alpha_\ell)\ge 0$ and $\underline{y}_\ell^* = \underline{\hat b}_\ell x_\ell^*$. As $C\to\infty$, the component $\underline{y}_1^*$ dominates the budget, with $(\underline{y}_1^*)^2\sim C$, so
\[
\rho(\boldsymbol{y}^*,\boldsymbol{u}^1) 
= \frac{\underline{y}_1^*}{\sqrt{C}}
\to \mathrm{sign}(\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}\cdot\boldsymbol{u}^1),
\]
and similarly with $\boldsymbol{u}^n$ in the substitutes case. Thus, for a fixed orientation of the eigenvectors, the limit of the cosine similarity is $\pm 1$, with the sign determined by $\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}\cdot\boldsymbol{u}^1$ (or $\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}\cdot\boldsymbol{u}^n$). 

Since eigenvectors are only defined up to sign, one can always reorient $\boldsymbol{u}^1$ (or $\boldsymbol{u}^n$) so that the limiting cosine similarity is $+1$, and the economic content of the result is that the intervention concentrates on the corresponding one-dimensional eigenspace. It would nonetheless be helpful to make this explicit—e.g., by formulating Proposition 1 in terms of $|\rho(\boldsymbol{y}^*,\boldsymbol{u}^1)|\to 1$ (and $|\rho(\boldsymbol{y}^*,\boldsymbol{u}^n)|\to 1$), or by stating any normalization of eigenvectors and/or restriction on the status quo vector that guarantees $\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}\cdot\boldsymbol{u}^1>0$ and $\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}\cdot\boldsymbol{u}^n>0.

comments.title: Sign error in discussion of Proposition 2 (substitutes case)
comments.quote:
If the game has the strategic substitutes property, $\beta<0$, then for any $\epsilon>0$, if $C> \frac{2\|\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}\|^{2}}{\epsilon}\left(\frac{\alpha_{n-1}}{\alpha_{n}-\alpha_{n-1}}\right)^{2}$, then...
...
If $\beta<0$, then the term $\alpha_{n-1} /\left(\alpha_{n-1}-\alpha_{n}\right)$ is large when the difference $\lambda_{n-1}-\lambda_{n}$, which we call the "bottom gap," is small.
comments.explanation: In the paragraph interpreting Proposition 2 for the substitutes case, the factor governing the bound on $C$ is described as $\alpha_{n-1}/(\alpha_{n-1}-\alpha_n)$, whereas in the formal statement of the proposition the factor is $\alpha_{n-1}/(\alpha_n-\alpha_{n-1})$ inside a square. Since for $\beta<0$ one has $\alpha_n>\alpha_{n-1}$, the latter uses a positive denominator and matches the expression used in the proof, while the former has the opposite sign. Because the ratio is squared in the bound, this does not affect the actual condition on $C$, but it introduces an avoidable notational inconsistency. It would be helpful to align the discussion with the proposition (using $\alpha_n-\alpha_{n-1}$) so that the "corresponding factor for bottom $\alpha$'s" is written with the same ordering throughout.

comments.title: "Maximizer" vs. "minimizer" for smallest eigenvalues
comments.quote:
Turning next to strategic substitutes, recall that the smallest two eigenvalues, $\lambda_{n}$ and $\lambda_{n-1}$, can be written as follows:
$$
\lambda_{n}=\min _{\boldsymbol{u}:\|\boldsymbol{u}\|=1} \sum_{i, j \in \mathcal{N}} g_{i j} u_{i} u_{j}, \quad \lambda_{n-1}=\min _{\substack{\boldsymbol{u}:\|\boldsymbol{u}\|=1 \\ \boldsymbol{u} \cdot \boldsymbol{u}^{n}=0}} \sum_{i, j \in \mathcal{N}} g_{i j} u_{i} u_{j} .
$$
Moreover, the eigenvector $\boldsymbol{u}^{n}$ is a maximizer of the first problem, while $\boldsymbol{u}^{n-1}$ is a maximizer of the second; these are uniquely determined under Assumption 2.
comments.explanation: In the substitutes discussion you correctly write the Rayleigh–Ritz characterizations for $\lambda_n$ and $\lambda_{n-1}$ as minimization problems, but then state that $\boldsymbol{u}^n$ and $\boldsymbol{u}^{n-1}$ are "maximizers" of these problems. Since the display uses $\min$ and the next sentence reintroduces $\boldsymbol{u}^n$ via an explicit $\arg\min$, these eigenvectors should be described as minimizers, not maximizers. This is clearly just a wording slip and does not affect any results, but it would be good to correct it for consistency with the complements case and with standard spectral terminology.

comments.title: Typo in Lagrangian in proof of Theorem 1
comments.quote:
Observe that the Lagrangian corresponding to the maximization problem is

$$
\mathcal{L}=w \sum_{\ell=1}^{n} \alpha_{\ell}\left(1+x_{\ell}\right)^{2} \underline{\hat{b}}_{\ell}+\mu\left[C-\sum_{\ell=1}^{n} \hat{b}_{\ell}^{2} x_{\ell}^{2}\right] .
$$

Taking our observation above that the constraint is binding at $\boldsymbol{x}=\boldsymbol{x}^{*}$, together with the standard results on the Karush-Kuhn-Tucker conditions, the first-order conditions must hold exactly at the optimum with a positive $\mu$ :
comments.explanation:
The displayed Lagrangian in the proof of Theorem 1 appears to omit a square on the status‑quo component. Just above, the problem in the $x_\ell$ variables is written as
$$
\max_x\; w\sum_{\ell=1}^n \alpha_\ell (1+x_\ell)^2 \underline{\hat b}_\ell^2
\quad \text{s.t. } \sum_{\ell=1}^n \underline{\hat b}_\ell^2 x_\ell^2 \le C,
$$
so the corresponding Lagrangian should involve $\underline{\hat b}_\ell^2$ in the first term as well. The first‑order condition you derive immediately afterward matches the derivative of this corrected Lagrangian, not the one as currently printed. This is a minor typographical issue, but correcting the exponent (and, if desired, harmonizing the underline notation for $\hat b_\ell$) would remove the internal inconsistency in this display.

comments.title: Unclear notation in proof of Proposition 2
comments.quote:
Cosine similarity. We now turn to the cosine similarity result. We focus on the case of strategic complements. The proof for the case of strategic substitutes is analogous. We start by writing a useful explicit expression for $\rho\left(\Delta \boldsymbol{b}^{*}, \sqrt{C} \boldsymbol{u}^{1}\right)$:

$$
\rho\left(\Delta \boldsymbol{b}^{*}, \sqrt{C} \boldsymbol{u}^{1}\right)=\frac{\left(\boldsymbol{b}^{*}-\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}\right) \cdot\left(\sqrt{C} \boldsymbol{u}^{1}\right)}{\left\|\boldsymbol{b}^{*}-\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}\right\|\left\|\sqrt{C} \boldsymbol{u}^{1}\right\|}=\frac{\left(\boldsymbol{b}^{*}-\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}\right) \cdot\left(\boldsymbol{u}^{1}\right)}{\sqrt{C}},
$$

where the last equality follows because, at the optimum, $\left\|\boldsymbol{b}^{*}-\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}\right\|^{2}=C$. ... Hence, using this in equation (12), we can deduce that

$$
\rho\left(\Delta \boldsymbol{b}^{*}, \boldsymbol{u}^{1}\right)=\frac{1}{\sqrt{C}} \frac{w \alpha_{1}}{\mu-w \alpha_{1}} \hat{b}_{1} \geq \sqrt{1-\epsilon} \quad \text { iff } \quad\left(\frac{w \alpha_{1}}{\mu-w \alpha_{1}}\right)^{2} \hat{b}_{1}^{2}-C(1-\epsilon) \geq 0 .
$$
comments.explanation:
At first the cosine‑similarity part of the proof of Proposition 2 was a bit hard to parse because of notation. The vector $\Delta\boldsymbol{b}^*$ is not explicitly defined, and the text switches between $\rho(\Delta\boldsymbol{b}^*,\sqrt{C}\boldsymbol{u}^1)$ and $\rho(\Delta\boldsymbol{b}^*,\boldsymbol{u}^1)$. From the displayed equality
$$
\rho(\Delta\boldsymbol{b}^*,\sqrt{C}\boldsymbol{u}^1)
= \frac{(\boldsymbol{b}^*-\hat{\boldsymbol{b}})\cdot(\sqrt{C}\boldsymbol{u}^1)}
{\|\boldsymbol{b}^*-\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}\|\,\|\sqrt{C}\boldsymbol{u}^1\|},
$$
one can infer that $\Delta\boldsymbol{b}^*=\boldsymbol{b}^*-\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}$, and using $\|\boldsymbol{b}^*-\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}\|^2=C$ and $\|\boldsymbol{u}^1\|=1$ it is easy to verify that $\rho(\Delta\boldsymbol{b}^*,\sqrt{C}\boldsymbol{u}^1)=\rho(\Delta\boldsymbol{b}^*,\boldsymbol{u}^1)$. Still, defining $\Delta\boldsymbol{b}^*$ explicitly (or sticking to $\boldsymbol{y}^*$) and noting the scale‑invariance of cosine similarity would make this part of the proof smoother to follow.

zero_shot.label: Zero-Shot
zero_shot.overall_feedback: This is a well-written and technically sophisticated paper that makes a clear methodological contribution to the study of optimal interventions in network games. The principal component decomposition approach is elegant and yields interpretable results. However, there are several technical errors in formulas and definitions, as well as some logical inconsistencies in the presentation that need to be addressed.

comments.title: Incorrect definition of cosine similarity
comments.quote: DEFINITION 1: The cosine similarity of two nonzero vectors y and z is ρ(y, z) = y·z / ‖y‖.
comments.explanation: The definition of cosine similarity is missing the norm of z in the denominator. The correct formula should be ρ(y, z) = y·z / (‖y‖ · ‖z‖). This is clearly the intended definition since the paper later uses the correct formula in proofs (e.g., in the proof of Theorem 1 where ρ(y*, u^ℓ(G)) = y*·u^ℓ(G) / (‖y*‖ · ‖u^ℓ(G)‖)).

comments.title: Incorrect change of variables in local public goods example
comments.quote: Performing the change of variables b_i = [τ - b_i]/2 and β = -β̃/2
comments.explanation: The change of variables uses b_i on both sides of the equation, which is circular. Based on the context, it should read b_i = [τ - b̃_i]/2 (using the tilde notation for the original base level parameter). This is confirmed by the status quo formula that follows: hat{b}_i = [τ - b̃_i]/2.

comments.title: Incorrect welfare formula for local public goods
comments.quote: The aggregate equilibrium utility is W(b, G) = −(a*)⊤a*.
comments.explanation: This needs verification. The utility function is U_i = -1/2(τ - x_i)^2 - 1/2 a_i^2. After the change of variables and computing equilibrium, the welfare should involve specific factors. The claim that W = -(a*)⊤a* (i.e., w = -1) needs to be checked carefully. Given the 1/2 factors in the utility function and the change of variables involving division by 2, the proportionality constant may not be exactly -1.

comments.title: Assumption 3 inconsistency with norm notation
comments.quote: Assumption 3: Either w < 0 and C < ‖b̂‖, or w > 0.
comments.explanation: Based on the context (the cost function K = Σ(b_i - b̂_i)^2 ≤ C involves squared norms), the condition should be C < ‖b̂‖² rather than C < ‖b̂‖. This is confirmed by the earlier discussion which states 'when the budget is large enough—that is, C ≥ ‖b̂‖²' for achieving the first-best, and footnote 15 which also uses ‖b̂‖ without the square. There is an inconsistency: the budget C has units of squared norm (since K is a sum of squares), so the comparison should be with ‖b̂‖².

comments.title: Eigenvector minimizer vs maximizer error for bottom eigenvalues
comments.quote: Moreover, the eigenvector u^n is a maximizer of the first problem, while u^{n-1} is a maximizer of the second
comments.explanation: Since λ_n and λ_{n-1} are defined as minimizers of the respective Rayleigh quotient problems, the eigenvectors u^n and u^{n-1} should be called minimizers, not maximizers. The text correctly states the problems involve 'min' but then incorrectly calls the solutions 'maximizers'.

comments.title: Missing square in the residual matrix decomposition
comments.quote: Now, if we consider the "residual" matrix G^(2) = G − u^1(u^1)⊤, we can perform the same type of decomposition on G^(2) and obtain the second eigenvector u^2 as the best rank-one approximation.
comments.explanation: The rank-one approximation from the first eigenvector should be λ_1 u^1(u^1)⊤, not u^1(u^1)⊤. Since u^1 is normalized to have unit norm, the best rank-one approximation to G is λ_1 u^1(u^1)⊤, and the residual should be G^(2) = G − λ_1 u^1(u^1)⊤. Without the eigenvalue scaling, the stated residual is incorrect.

comments.title: Incorrect equilibrium action formula using square root of alpha
comments.quote: and note that a̲*_ℓ = √α_ℓ · b̲_ℓ is the equilibrium action in the ℓth principal component of G (see equation (4)).
comments.explanation: From equation (4), a̲*_ℓ = b̲_ℓ/(1 - βλ_ℓ). Since α_ℓ = 1/(1 - βλ_ℓ)², we have √α_ℓ = 1/|1 - βλ_ℓ|. But the amplification factor from equation (4) is 1/(1 - βλ_ℓ), which can be negative if 1 - βλ_ℓ < 0 (though Assumption 2 rules this out). Under Assumption 2, 1 - βλ_ℓ > 0, so √α_ℓ = 1/(1 - βλ_ℓ), and the formula is correct. However, the notation is potentially misleading since √α_ℓ is always positive while 1/(1-βλ_ℓ) is also positive under Assumption 2, so this is actually fine.

comments.title: Inconsistent cost function notation in incomplete information section
comments.quote: K(B_y) = Σ_{i∈N} y_i², and K(B) is ∞ for any other random variable.
comments.explanation: This cost function definition is inconsistent with the complete information case where K(b, b̂) = Σ(b_i - b̂_i)². In the complete information case, the cost depends on the deviation from the status quo b̂, but in Assumption 4, the cost is simply Σy_i² where y is the deterministic shift. These are consistent only if we interpret y = b - b̂, which is stated, but the notation K(B_y) taking a random variable as argument while the cost depends only on the deterministic y is somewhat confusing but not strictly an error.

comments.title: Proof of Proposition 2 uses incorrect bound step
comments.explanation: The justification via Corollary 1 is that |r*_ℓ| is monotone in ℓ, which means x*_ℓ(x*_ℓ + 2) is bounded by x*_2(x*_2 + 2) for ℓ ≥ 2 under strategic complements. However, this requires that x*_ℓ(x*_ℓ + 2) is decreasing in ℓ for ℓ ≥ 2. Since x*_ℓ ≥ 0 (as shown in the proof of Theorem 1 for w > 0) and x*_ℓ is decreasing in ℓ (from Corollary 1), the function x(x+2) is increasing in x for x ≥ 0, so x*_2(x*_2 + 2) ≥ x*_ℓ(x*_ℓ + 2) for ℓ ≥ 2. This step is actually correct.

comments.title: Proposition 2 bound uses μ > wα₁ but this needs justification
comments.explanation: This step replaces μ with wα₁ in the denominator, using μ > wα₁. Since wα₂/(μ - wα₂) is decreasing in μ (for μ > wα₂), replacing μ with the smaller value wα₁ gives an upper bound. This is correct. However, the fact that μ > wα₁ follows from equation (6) of Theorem 1 and needs the condition that the sum equals C with all terms being finite, which requires μ > wα_ℓ for all ℓ, and in particular μ > wα₁. This is stated in Theorem 1.

comments.title: Proposition 2 intermediate inequality has an unexplained factor
comments.explanation: The notation 'α₂*' appears to be a typo. Looking at the derivation, the factor should be (2α₁ - α₂)/α₁, not (2α₁ - α₂*)/α₁. The asterisk seems to be a typographical error, as α₂* is not defined anywhere in the paper.

comments.title: Sign issue in the proof of Theorem 1 for w < 0 case
comments.quote: Analogously, suppose w < 0. It is clear that if x*_ℓ < -1, then by setting x_ℓ = -1, the objective improves and the constraint is relaxed; hence, at the optimum, x*_ℓ ≥ -1.
comments.explanation: When w < 0, the planner wants to minimize Σα_ℓ(1+x_ℓ)²b̂_ℓ². If x*_ℓ < -1, then (1+x*_ℓ)² > 0, and setting x_ℓ = -1 gives (1+x_ℓ)² = 0, which reduces the objective (makes it less negative in absolute value). Since w < 0, reducing Σα_ℓ(1+x_ℓ)²b̂_ℓ² actually improves the objective w·Σα_ℓ(1+x_ℓ)²b̂_ℓ² (makes it less negative). Also, |x_ℓ| decreases, relaxing the constraint. So the argument is correct.

comments.title: Incomplete specification of proportionality in Theorem 1
comments.explanation: The proportionality symbol ∝ means the left side equals a constant times the right side, where the constant is the same for all ℓ. From the proof, this constant is ‖b̂‖/√C, which is positive. This should be stated more explicitly, as the sign of the proportionality constant matters for the interpretation. The proof does show this, but the theorem statement could be clearer.

comments.title: Footnote 15 inconsistency with Assumption 3
comments.quote: In the local public goods application (recall Example 2), w = −1, and so when C ≥ ‖b̂‖, the optimal intervention satisfies b*_i = 0.
comments.explanation: The condition should be C ≥ ‖b̂‖² (squared norm), not C ≥ ‖b̂‖, since the cost function K(b, b̂) = Σ(b_i - b̂_i)² = ‖b - b̂‖². Setting b = 0 costs ‖b̂‖², so the budget must satisfy C ≥ ‖b̂‖². This same error appears in Assumption 3.

few_shot.label: Few-Shot
few_shot.overall_feedback: This is a well-written and methodologically rigorous paper that makes a clear contribution to the study of optimal interventions in network games. The principal component decomposition approach is elegant and the main results are clearly stated. However, there are several notation inconsistencies, a missing factor in a key definition, minor logical issues in the proofs, and some imprecise statements that merit attention. Most issues are relatively minor but a few could cause confusion for readers attempting to verify the formal results.

comments.title: Incomplete definition of cosine similarity (missing denominator term)
comments.quote: DEFINITION 1: The cosine similarity of two nonzero vectors $\boldsymbol{y}$ and $\boldsymbol{z}$ is $\rho(\boldsymbol{y}, \boldsymbol{z})=\frac{\boldsymbol{y} \cdot \boldsymbol{z}}{\|\boldsymbol{y}\|}$.
comments.explanation: The standard definition of cosine similarity is $\rho(\boldsymbol{y}, \boldsymbol{z}) = \frac{\boldsymbol{y} \cdot \boldsymbol{z}}{\|\boldsymbol{y}\| \|\boldsymbol{z}\|}$. The denominator is missing the factor $\|\boldsymbol{z}\|$. The text immediately following says 'This is the cosine of the angle between the two vectors,' which confirms the intended definition requires both norms in the denominator. The correct formula is used later in the proofs (e.g., in the proof of Proposition 2, equation (12), where both norms appear).

comments.title: Inconsistent notation: $\mathcal{N}$ vs $N$ for the set of individuals
comments.quote: We consider a simultaneous-move game among individuals $\mathcal{N}=\{1, \ldots, n\}$
comments.explanation: The set of individuals is defined as $\mathcal{N}$ in the model section, but in Assumption 2 the paper writes 'for every $i \in N, g_{ii}=0$' using $N$ instead of $\mathcal{N}$. This is a minor notation inconsistency.

comments.title: Eigenvectors described as 'minimizers' instead of 'maximizers' for bottom eigenvalues
comments.quote: Moreover, the eigenvector $\boldsymbol{u}^{n}$ is a maximizer of the first problem, while $\boldsymbol{u}^{n-1}$ is a maximizer of the second
comments.explanation: The two problems displayed just before this sentence are minimization problems (using $\min$). Therefore $\boldsymbol{u}^{n}$ and $\boldsymbol{u}^{n-1}$ should be called 'minimizers,' not 'maximizers.' This is a clear verbal error, though the mathematical content (the variational characterizations) is correct.

comments.title: Change of variables error in Example 2 (Local Public Goods)
comments.quote: Performing the change of variables $b_{i}=\left[\tau-b_{i}\right] / 2$ and $\beta=-\tilde{\beta} / 2$
comments.explanation: The change of variables writes $b_i = [\tau - b_i]/2$, which uses $b_i$ on both sides of the equation. Based on the context, this should read $b_i = [\tau - \tilde{b}_i]/2$, using the base level $\tilde{b}_i$ on the right-hand side. This is confirmed by the status quo formula given immediately after: $\hat{b}_i = [\tau - \tilde{b}_i]/2$.

comments.title: Welfare formula sign issue in Example 2
comments.explanation: After the change of variables, the welfare in Example 2 is stated as $W = -(\boldsymbol{a}^*)^\top \boldsymbol{a}^*$, which corresponds to $w = -1$ in Property A. However, verifying this from the utility function $U_i = -\frac{1}{2}(\tau - x_i)^2 - \frac{1}{2}a_i^2$ and the change of variables requires careful algebra. The equilibrium utility of each agent involves terms from both the public good shortfall and the cost of effort. The claim that the aggregate simplifies to exactly $-(\boldsymbol{a}^*)^\top \boldsymbol{a}^*$ (rather than $-\frac{1}{2}(\boldsymbol{a}^*)^\top \boldsymbol{a}^*$ or some other form) is not verified in the paper and appears to require additional justification given the factor of $1/2$ in the change of variables.

comments.title: Assumption 3 uses norm instead of squared norm for the $w < 0$ case
comments.explanation: The budget constraint in problem (IT) is $\sum_i (b_i - \hat{b}_i)^2 \leq C$, and the text preceding Assumption 3 states that the planner can achieve the first-best when $C \geq \|\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}\|^2$. Therefore, the condition for a non-trivial problem when $w < 0$ should be $C < \|\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}\|^2$, not $C < \|\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}\|$. The squared norm is what appears in the preceding paragraph.

comments.title: Proof of Proposition 2: incorrect intermediate factor in welfare bound
comments.explanation: The notation $\alpha_2^*$ appears to be a typo; it should be $\alpha_2$ (there is no starred version of $\alpha$ defined anywhere). Moreover, the factor $(2\alpha_1 - \alpha_2)/\alpha_1$ in the numerator is claimed to be bounded by 2 in the next line. While $2\alpha_1 - \alpha_2 \leq 2\alpha_1$ is true (since $\alpha_2 > 0$), the intermediate expression itself contains the unexplained notation $\alpha_2^*$.

comments.title: Proof of Proposition 2: unjustified step using Corollary 1 for bounding $x_\ell^*(x_\ell^* + 2)$
comments.explanation: This step factors out $x_2^*(x_2^* + 2)$ from the sum, claiming that $x_\ell^*(x_\ell^* + 2) \leq x_2^*(x_2^* + 2)$ for all $\ell \geq 2$ by Corollary 1. Corollary 1 establishes that $|r_\ell^*|$ is decreasing in $\ell$ for $\beta > 0$, which implies $x_\ell^* \leq x_2^*$ for $\ell \geq 2$ (since all $x_\ell^* \geq 0$ when $w > 0$). The function $f(x) = x(x+2)$ is increasing for $x \geq 0$, so the bound is valid. However, the justification via 'Corollary 1' is indirect—what is actually needed is the monotonicity of $x_\ell^*$ in $\ell$, which follows from Theorem 1 and the ordering of $\alpha_\ell$, not directly from the similarity ratio ordering of Corollary 1.

comments.title: Proof of Theorem 1: Lagrangian missing squared term on $\hat{b}_\ell$
comments.explanation: In the Lagrangian, the first term has $\underline{\hat{b}}_\ell$ (without square), but the objective function from the transformed problem (IT-PC with the $x_\ell$ substitution) has $\underline{\hat{b}}_\ell^2$. This appears to be a typo: it should be $\underline{\hat{b}}_\ell^2$ in the Lagrangian. The first-order condition that follows is consistent with $\underline{\hat{b}}_\ell^2$, confirming this is a typo.

comments.title: Proof of Theorem 1: notation switch between $\hat{b}_\ell$ and $\underline{\hat{b}}_\ell$
comments.explanation: The first-order condition uses $\hat{b}_\ell^2$ (without the underline), but in the principal component basis the correct notation should be $\underline{\hat{b}}_\ell^2$. Throughout the proof, the underline notation is used inconsistently—sometimes $\hat{b}_\ell$ appears where $\underline{\hat{b}}_\ell$ is meant. This is confusing because $\hat{b}_\ell$ (without underline) refers to the $\ell$th entry of $\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}$ in the original basis, while $\underline{\hat{b}}_\ell$ is the projection onto the $\ell$th principal component.

comments.title: Equation (6) notation inconsistency with underlines
comments.explanation: In equation (6) of Theorem 1, $\hat{b}_\ell^2$ appears without the underline notation, but this equation is in the principal component basis and should use $\underline{\hat{b}}_\ell^2$. The same inconsistency appears in the sketch derivation and in the proof. The budget constraint in the principal component basis involves $\underline{\hat{b}}_\ell^2$, not $\hat{b}_\ell^2$.

comments.title: Rank-one approximation description omits eigenvalue scaling
comments.quote: The minimizer turns out to be a scaling of the eigenvector $\boldsymbol{u}^{1}$.
comments.explanation: The best symmetric rank-one approximation to $\boldsymbol{G}$ in the Frobenius norm sense is $\lambda_1 \boldsymbol{u}^1 (\boldsymbol{u}^1)^\top$, not $\boldsymbol{u}^1 (\boldsymbol{u}^1)^\top$. The minimizer of $\sum_{i,j}(g_{ij} - u_i u_j)^2$ over unit vectors $\boldsymbol{u}$ is indeed $\boldsymbol{u}^1$, but the text says 'a scaling of the eigenvector $\boldsymbol{u}^1$' for the vector $\boldsymbol{u}$ itself, which is slightly misleading since $\boldsymbol{u}^1$ is already normalized. The residual matrix is then written as $\boldsymbol{G}^{(2)} = \boldsymbol{G} - \boldsymbol{u}^1(\boldsymbol{u}^1)^\top$, which should be $\boldsymbol{G} - \lambda_1 \boldsymbol{u}^1(\boldsymbol{u}^1)^\top$ for the standard PCA decomposition to work correctly.

comments.title: Proposition 2 bound condition uses strict inequality but should match the $\geq$ in the conclusion
comments.explanation: In the proof of Proposition 2, the chain of inequalities establishes $W^*/W^s \leq 1 + \frac{2\|\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}\|^2}{C}(\frac{\alpha_2}{\alpha_1 - \alpha_2})^2$. The stated condition $C > \frac{2\|\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}\|^2}{\epsilon}(\frac{\alpha_2}{\alpha_1 - \alpha_2})^2$ then gives $W^*/W^s \leq 1 + \epsilon$ (with weak inequality), but the proposition states strict inequality $W^*/W^s < 1 + \epsilon$. This is a very minor issue but technically the bound derived in the proof gives $\leq$ not $<$.

comments.title: Proof of Proposition 1 Part 2: incomplete justification for $\mu \to w\alpha_1$
comments.quote: From expression (6) of Theorem 1, it follows that if $C \rightarrow \infty$, then $\mu \rightarrow w \alpha_{1}$.
comments.explanation: The claim that $\mu \to w\alpha_1$ as $C \to \infty$ (when $\beta > 0$, so $w\alpha_1 = \max_\ell w\alpha_\ell$) requires showing that $\mu$ must approach $w\alpha_1$ from above for the left-hand side of equation (6) to diverge to infinity. While this is correct (as $\mu \downarrow w\alpha_1$, the $\ell=1$ term in the sum diverges), the proof does not explicitly verify that $\mu$ cannot converge to some other value or that the sum indeed diverges only when $\mu$ approaches $w\alpha_1$. A brief argument noting that $\mu > w\alpha_\ell$ for all $\ell$ (from Theorem 1) and that the sum is monotonically increasing as $\mu$ decreases toward $w\alpha_1$ would complete the justification.

rag_local.label: RAG Local
rag_local.overall_feedback: This paper makes a valuable and elegant theoretical contribution to the literature on optimal policy design in network games. The core idea—decomposing interventions into the eigenvector basis of the interaction matrix to exploit orthogonality and the multiplicative structure of equilibrium responses—is both technically clean and economically intuitive, yielding sharp characterizations that connect the nature of strategic spillovers (complements vs. substitutes) to the spectral properties of the network. The writing is generally clear, and the paper does a good job of building intuition for why complementarities favor interventions aligned with top eigenvectors (capturing global, diffusive network structure) while substitutes favor bottom eigenvectors (capturing local, bipartite-like structure). However, several significant concerns merit attention. First, the framework relies heavily on the linear-quadratic structure of payoffs and the assumption that the interaction matrix is symmetric, which together deliver the clean spectral decomposition; the paper would benefit from a more thorough discussion of how robust the insights are to asymmetric interactions, nonlinear best responses, or heterogeneous cost structures, since many empirically relevant networks feature directed links and asymmetric influence. Second, while the large-budget simplicity results (Propositions 1 and 2) are striking, their practical relevance depends on how large "large enough" is in realistic settings—some numerical illustrations or calibrations would substantially strengthen the paper's applied value. Third, the stochastic extension, while a natural and welcome addition, appears to assume the planner knows the full network structure but not individual-level returns; in many policy-relevant contexts, the network itself is imperfectly observed, and addressing this form of uncertainty would significantly broaden applicability. Finally, the connection to the existing literature on key player identification and optimal seeding in networks could be drawn more precisely, clarifying when and why the eigenvector-based approach yields qualitatively different policy prescriptions than targeting based on simpler centrality measures such as degree or betweenness.

comments.title: Variable defined in terms of itself
comments.explanation: The change of variables defines $b_i$ in terms of itself, which is circular. Based on the context (where $\tilde{b}_i$ is the base level of the public good), this should read $b_i = [\tau - \tilde{b}_i] / 2$.

comments.title: Inconsistent notation: bold beta in matrix expression
comments.explanation: The scalar parameter β is typeset in bold (\boldsymbol{\beta}) in the matrix expression, suggesting it is a vector or matrix. Throughout the paper (e.g., in para 51 and the amplification factor discussion), β is a scalar and should not be bolded. The correct notation, consistent with para 51, is [I - β Λ]^{-1}.

comments.title: Incorrect claim that correlation tends to 0 for all ℓ ≠ 1
comments.quote: As a result, if $C \rightarrow \infty$, then $\rho\left(\boldsymbol{y}^{*}, \boldsymbol{u}^{\ell}(\boldsymbol{G})\right) \rightarrow$ 0 for all $\ell \neq 1$.
comments.explanation: The fact that $x_{\ell}^{*} \rightarrow \frac{\alpha_{\ell}}{\alpha_{1}-\alpha_{\ell}}$ (a finite constant) for $\ell \neq 1$ does not by itself imply that $\rho(\boldsymbol{y}^{*}, \boldsymbol{u}^{\ell}(\boldsymbol{G})) \rightarrow 0$. From the formula in para 150, $\rho(\boldsymbol{y}^{*}, \boldsymbol{u}^{\ell}(\boldsymbol{G})) = \frac{\|\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}\|}{\sqrt{C}} \rho(\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}, \boldsymbol{u}^{\ell}(\boldsymbol{G})) x_{\ell}^{*}$. The conclusion requires that $\frac{x_{\ell}^{*}}{\sqrt{C}} \rightarrow 0$ for $\ell \neq 1$, which is true since $x_{\ell}^{*}$ stays finite while $\sqrt{C} \rightarrow \infty$. However, the passage presents the reasoning as if the finiteness of the limit of $x_{\ell}^{*}$ alone is the reason, without explicitly invoking the $1/\sqrt{C}$ factor. The stated logical chain ('This implies... As a result...') is incomplete, though the final conclusion is correct when the full formula is considered.

comments.title: Insufficient justification for dropping all ℓ ≠ 1 terms
comments.explanation: The justification given in para 154 is that x_ℓ* → α_ℓ/(α_1 − α_ℓ) for ℓ ≠ 1, i.e., x_ℓ* converges to a finite constant. But x_1* = wα_1/(μ − wα_1) diverges as C → ∞ (since μ → wα_1). The claim that the ℓ ≠ 1 terms vanish requires showing that (x_ℓ*)²/C → 0 for ℓ ≠ 1, which is true since x_ℓ* stays bounded while C → ∞. However, the stated reason ('because x_ℓ* → α_ℓ/(α_1 − α_ℓ)') is incomplete — it should explicitly note that the finite limit of x_ℓ* combined with division by √C drives those terms to zero. This is a reasoning gap rather than an outright error, but the justification as written is imprecise.

comments.title: Garbled notation for β < 0
comments.quote: The proof for the case of $\betax3C0$
comments.explanation: This appears to be a rendering/encoding error. The text '$\betax3C0$' should read 'β < 0' (i.e., strategic substitutes). The Unicode character U+003C ('<') seems to have been corrupted into 'x3C'.

comments.title: Incomplete justification for dropping ℓ ≠ 1 terms in the limit
comments.quote: where the first equality follows because $x_{\ell}^{*} \rightarrow \frac{\alpha_{\ell}}{\alpha_{1}-\alpha_{\ell}}$ for all $\ell \neq 1$
comments.explanation: The fact that x_ℓ* converges to a finite constant for ℓ ≠ 1 is necessary but not sufficient by itself; one must also note that these finite limits are divided by √C → ∞, so that (x_ℓ*/√C)² → 0 for ℓ ≠ 1. The stated reason is incomplete: the key point is that x_ℓ* remains bounded while √C → ∞, making those terms vanish.

comments.title: Typo in numerator factor: spurious asterisk on α₂
comments.explanation: The factor in the numerator is written as (2α₁ − α₂*), where the asterisk on α₂ is erroneous. From the supporting calculation in para 162, the numerator of the bound is (2α₁ − α₂), not (2α₁ − α₂*). The superscript * is a notation error (α₂* is not defined and is not the eigenvalue α₂).

comments.title: First inequality numerator uses wrong summation index
comments.explanation: The first fraction's numerator contains ∑_{ℓ=1}^{n} α_ℓ b̂_ℓ² with b̂_ℓ (optimal coefficients), but D is defined with ∑_{ℓ=1}^{n} α_ℓ \underline{\hat{b}}_ℓ² (simple-policy coefficients). For the subsequent inequality (≤ 1) to hold, one needs the first fraction's numerator to be ≤ D, which requires comparing optimal and simple-policy quantities. The notation switches between b̂_ℓ and \underline{\hat{b}}_ℓ without justification, making the claimed inequality ≤ 1 non-obvious or the notation inconsistent.

comments.title: Denominator replacement drops positive terms without justification in display
comments.explanation: D = \underline{\hat{b}}_1^2 α_1 x̃_1(x̃_1+2) + ∑ α_ℓ \underline{\hat{b}}_ℓ², which equals \underline{\hat{b}}_1^2 α_1 x̃_1² + 2\underline{\hat{b}}_1^2 α_1 x̃_1 + ∑ α_ℓ \underline{\hat{b}}_ℓ². Replacing D by just \hat{b}_1^2 α_1 x̃_1² (dropping positive terms) is valid for making the fraction larger, but the notation switches from \underline{\hat{b}}_1 to \hat{b}_1, which are different quantities (simple vs. optimal policy coefficients).

comments.title: Missing normalization factor in the 'iff' condition
comments.explanation: The correlation ρ is defined as the cosine (normalized dot product), so ρ = (1/√C) · [w α₁/(μ - w α₁)] · b̂₁ / ‖Δb*‖. Squaring the inequality ρ ≥ √(1−ε) should give [(w α₁/(μ−w α₁))² b̂₁²] / [C · ‖Δb*‖²] ≥ (1−ε). The displayed 'iff' condition omits the ‖Δb*‖² denominator (or equivalently treats ρ as an unnormalized dot product rather than a true correlation). If ρ is literally the cosine of the angle between Δb* and u¹, then the equivalence as written is incorrect because it replaces ‖Δb*‖² with C.

comments.title: Notation inconsistency: b̂₁ vs b̂₁ with underline
comments.explanation: In para 171 the quantity appearing is \hat{\underline{b}}_1 (the component of b in the eigenbasis, defined via b̲ = Uᵀb), but in para 172 the underline is dropped and it is written as \hat{b}_1. These are different objects: \hat{b}_1 is a component of the original vector b̂, while \hat{\underline{b}}_1 is a component of Uᵀ\hat{b}. The notation should be consistent with para 171.

comments.title: Incorrect implication: factor of 2 dropped without justification
comments.explanation: Multiplying both sides of the hypothesis by ε gives εC > 2‖b̂‖²(α₂/(α₁−α₂))², which is strictly stronger than the stated conclusion εC > ‖b̂‖²(α₂/(α₁−α₂))². The implication is technically valid (the conclusion follows), but presenting it as the direct consequence obscures the factor of 2. More importantly, the intermediate step used later (para 177) appears to need C(1−ε) < C − ‖b̂‖²(α₂/(α₁−α₂))², which actually requires only εC > ‖b̂‖²(α₂/(α₁−α₂))². So while the implication is not wrong, the derivation is misleading: the natural single-step consequence of multiplying by ε retains the factor 2, and the authors silently weaken the bound. This is not an error per se but a lossy step that could confuse readers; however, the implication arrow is still logically correct.

comments.title: Corrupted inequality expression
comments.explanation: The expression contains garbled text 'x3CC' which should be a 'less than' symbol (<). Based on the context of para 176 (which establishes that εC > ||b̂||²(α₂/(α₁−α₂))²), the intended statement is C(1−ε) < C − ||b̂||²(α₂/(α₁−α₂))², which follows by rearranging εC > ||b̂||²(α₂/(α₁−α₂))² to C − ||b̂||²(α₂/(α₁−α₂))² > C − εC = C(1−ε). The rendering corruption obscures the inequality direction.

comments.title: Incorrect direction of final inequality
comments.explanation: For ℓ > 1, since α₂ ≥ αℓ and α₁ - α₂ ≤ α₁ - αℓ, the fraction αℓ/(α₁ - αℓ) is less than or equal to α₂/(α₁ - α₂), not greater. The inequality sign should be ≤ (i.e., the 'x3C' which appears to encode '<' is actually correct here). However, examining the context: the passage needs (wαℓ/(μ - wαℓ))² < (α₂/(α₁ - α₂))² for the final positivity argument. The first inequality (wαℓ/(μ - wαℓ) < wαℓ/(wα₁ - wαℓ)) uses μ > wα₁, which is correct. The last inequality should also be ≤ rather than <, since αℓ/(α₁ - αℓ) ≤ α₂/(α₁ - α₂) for ℓ ≥ 2. The 'x3C' tokens appear to be corrupted renderings of '<', and both inequality directions are actually correct for the argument. No substantive error is present.

comments.title: Corrupted inequality symbols rendered as 'x3C'
comments.explanation: The tokens 'x3C' appear twice and are corrupted representations of the '<' symbol (Unicode U+003C). They should each read '<' (or '\leq'). This is a formatting/encoding error rather than a mathematical one, but it renders the displayed formula incorrect as written.

comments.title: Incorrect decomposition of squared norm
comments.quote: $\left\|\frac{1}{n} \hat{\boldsymbol{b}}\right\|^{2}$ is equal to the sum of $\left(\frac{1}{n} \sum_{i \in \mathcal{N}} \hat{b}_{i}\right)^{2}$ (the squared mean of the entries of $\boldsymbol{b}$) and the sum of squared deviations of the entries of the vector $\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}$ from their mean.
comments.explanation: The identity $\|\frac{1}{n}\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}\|^2 = \frac{1}{n^2}\sum_i \hat{b}_i^2$. By the bias-variance decomposition, $\frac{1}{n}\sum_i \hat{b}_i^2 = \left(\frac{1}{n}\sum_i \hat{b}_i\right)^2 + \frac{1}{n}\sum_i (\hat{b}_i - \bar{b})^2$, so $\|\frac{1}{n}\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}\|^2 = \frac{1}{n}\left[\left(\frac{1}{n}\sum_i \hat{b}_i\right)^2 + \frac{1}{n}\sum_i(\hat{b}_i-\bar{b})^2\right]$. The stated decomposition omits the factor of $1/n$ (or equivalently $1/n^2$) needed to reconcile the squared norm $\|\frac{1}{n}\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}\|^2$ with the variance decomposition. As written, the right-hand side equals $\frac{1}{n}\sum_i \hat{b}_i^2$, not $\frac{1}{n^2}\sum_i \hat{b}_i^2 = \|\frac{1}{n}\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}\|^2$. The claim also says 'sum of squared deviations' rather than 'mean of squared deviations' (i.e., variance), which further mismatches the scaling.

comments.title: Garbled Unicode for inequality symbol
comments.explanation: The string 'wx3C0' appears to be a corrupted rendering of a Unicode character. Based on the hex code x3C (which is '<') and the trailing '0', this should read 'w < 0' (i.e., the parameter w is less than zero). This is a encoding/typesetting error that renders the condition unintelligible.

rag_retrieved.label: RAG Retrieved
rag_retrieved.overall_feedback: This paper makes a strong and elegant theoretical contribution to the literature on optimal policy design in network games. The core idea—decomposing interventions into the eigenvector basis of the interaction matrix and characterizing optimal targeting in terms of principal components—is both technically clean and economically intuitive, yielding sharp comparative statics on how strategic complements versus substitutes shape the structure of optimal policy. The writing is generally clear, and the paper does a good job of building intuition for why "global" (high-eigenvalue) principal components matter under complements while "local" (low-eigenvalue) components matter under substitutes. The result that optimal interventions become "simple" (proportional to a single eigenvector) for large budgets is particularly appealing, as it offers practical guidance and connects to the broader theme of when complex network information can be reduced to tractable statistics. That said, several issues merit attention. First, the framework relies on a quadratic (or at least linear best-response) structure, and it would strengthen the paper to discuss more explicitly how fragile the results are to departures from this functional form—whether the eigenvector decomposition retains any optimality properties with nonlinear best responses or corner solutions. Second, the assumption that the interaction matrix is symmetric (or at least normal, so that it admits an orthogonal eigenvector decomposition) deserves more scrutiny, since many empirically relevant networks are directed and asymmetric, which would complicate the spectral approach considerably. Third, while the large-budget simplicity results are striking, the paper could do more to characterize the practically relevant intermediate-budget regime, where the interplay between status quo heterogeneity and network structure is most complex. Finally, the connection to empirical applications—where networks are estimated with error, where the planner may face constraints on which nodes can be targeted, and where spillovers may be heterogeneous across links—could be developed further to enhance the paper's applied relevance.

comments.title: Variable defined in terms of itself
comments.explanation: The change of variables defines $b_i$ in terms of itself, which is circular. Based on the context (para 36 uses $\tilde{b}_i$ for the base level), this should read $b_i = [\tau - \tilde{b}_i]/2$, consistent with the status quo definition $\hat{b}_i = [\tau - \tilde{b}_i]/2$ given immediately after.

comments.title: Missing factor of 1/2 in aggregate equilibrium utility
comments.explanation: From the utility function in para 35, each agent's utility involves $-\frac{1}{2}(\tau - x_i)^2 - \frac{1}{2}a_i^2$. Following the derivation analogous to Example 1 (para 32), where $W = \frac{1}{2}(\boldsymbol{a}^*)^\top \boldsymbol{a}^*$, and noting Property A requires $W = w \cdot (\boldsymbol{a}^*)^\top \boldsymbol{a}^*$, the correct expression should be $W(\boldsymbol{b}, \boldsymbol{G}) = -\frac{1}{2}(\boldsymbol{a}^*)^\top \boldsymbol{a}^*$. The factor of $\frac{1}{2}$ is missing.

comments.title: Missing normalization factor in cosine similarity definition
comments.explanation: The standard cosine similarity formula is (y · z) / (||y|| ||z||). The denominator is missing the factor ||z||. This is confirmed by para 146, where the authors themselves use ρ(y*, u^ℓ) = (y* · u^ℓ) / (||y*|| ||u^ℓ||), which includes both norms in the denominator.

comments.title: Incorrect limit of x_ℓ* when μ → wα₁
comments.explanation: When μ → wα₁, we have x_ℓ* = wα_ℓ/(μ - wα_ℓ) → wα_ℓ/(wα₁ - wα_ℓ) = α_ℓ/(α₁ - α_ℓ). This finite limit for ℓ ≠ 1 is correct in itself, but note that x₁* = wα₁/(μ - wα₁) → ∞. The claim that ρ(y*, u^ℓ(G)) → 0 for ℓ ≠ 1 relies on the normalization condition (para 152): since x₁* → ∞ while x_ℓ* stays finite for ℓ ≠ 1, the normalization forces the finite-x_ℓ* terms to vanish relative to the diverging x₁* term. So the conclusion is correct, but the passage presents it as if the finite limit of x_ℓ* alone implies ρ → 0, which is not the direct reasoning — it is the divergence of x₁* combined with the normalization that drives ρ(y*, u^ℓ) → 0 for ℓ ≠ 1. The stated implication ('This implies that... As a result...') is misleading as written, since a finite limit of x_ℓ* does not by itself imply ρ → 0; one needs the normalization and the divergence of x₁*.

comments.title: Incorrect norm expression — missing factor or wrong scaling
comments.quote: $\left\|\frac{1}{n} \hat{\boldsymbol{b}}\right\|^{2}$ is equal to the sum of $\left(\frac{1}{n} \sum_{i \in \mathcal{N}} \hat{b}_{i}\right)^{2}$ (the squared mean of the entries of $\boldsymbol{b}$) and the sum of squared deviations of the entries of the vector $\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}$ from their mean.
comments.explanation: The squared norm $\|\frac{1}{n}\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}\|^2 = \frac{1}{n^2}\sum_i \hat{b}_i^2$. By the bias-variance decomposition, $\frac{1}{n}\sum_i \hat{b}_i^2 = \left(\frac{1}{n}\sum_i \hat{b}_i\right)^2 + \frac{1}{n}\sum_i (\hat{b}_i - \bar{\hat{b}})^2$, so $\frac{1}{n^2}\sum_i \hat{b}_i^2 = \left(\frac{1}{n}\sum_i \hat{b}_i\right)^2 \cdot \frac{1}{1} + \frac{1}{n^2}\sum_i(\hat{b}_i-\bar{\hat{b}})^2$. The claim as stated — that $\|\frac{1}{n}\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}\|^2$ equals the squared mean plus the sum of squared deviations — is dimensionally/numerically wrong. The correct decomposition is $\frac{1}{n}\|\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}\|^2 = (\bar{\hat{b}})^2 + \frac{1}{n}\sum_i(\hat{b}_i - \bar{\hat{b}})^2$, i.e., the variance decomposition applies to $\frac{1}{n}\|\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}\|^2$, not $\|\frac{1}{n}\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}\|^2$. Likely the intended expression was $\frac{1}{n}\|\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}\|^2$ or equivalently $\|\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}\|^2/n$.

comments.title: Inconsistent use of $\boldsymbol{b}$ vs $\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}$
comments.quote: (the squared mean of the entries of $\boldsymbol{b}$)
comments.explanation: The footnote discusses $\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}$ throughout but then refers to 'the entries of $\boldsymbol{b}$' instead of 'the entries of $\hat{\boldsymbol{b}}$'. This is a notation inconsistency.

comments.title: Notation inconsistency: Σ missing subscript
comments.explanation: The variance-covariance matrix of 𝒷 is denoted Σ_𝒷 (with subscript ℬ) in the main text (para 117-118), but in this footnote it is written simply as Σ without the subscript ℬ. This is a minor notation inconsistency with the surrounding text.

rag_retrieved_cot.label: RAG Retrieved CoT
rag_retrieved_cot.overall_feedback: This paper makes a strong and elegant theoretical contribution to the literature on optimal policy design in network games. The core idea—decomposing interventions into the eigenvector basis of the interaction matrix and characterizing optimal targeting in terms of principal components—is both technically clean and conceptually illuminating. The main results connecting strategic complements to top eigenvectors (global network structure) and strategic substitutes to bottom eigenvectors (local network structure) are intuitive and well-developed, and the asymptotic simplicity results for large budgets are particularly appealing for practical guidance. The writing is generally clear and well-organized, with the introduction doing a good job of conveying the key economic intuitions before the formal analysis. However, several issues merit attention. First, the reliance on linear-quadratic payoffs, while standard in the network games literature, limits the generality of the results, and the paper would benefit from more discussion of how robust the insights are to nonlinear settings or corner solutions. Second, the assumption that the planner knows the full network structure is strong, and while the stochastic extension addresses uncertainty about standalone marginal returns, uncertainty about the network itself—arguably the more empirically relevant concern—appears underexplored.
